findings.title: in-<think> op-gap detector (#15) is COUNT-based, not identity-based → false "your op did NOT run" warning when the model drafts ops in <think> then re-emits the SAME ops outside
findings.where: core/native_tools.py — call_with_json_ops, the `_think_warn` block (lines ~3766-3776), appended to the RESULTS turn at line ~3821 (and the nudge at ~3780)
findings.evidence: Round 1 the model wrote its ops TWICE: inside <think> (lines 27-28: `{"tool":"edit_file",...}` then `{"tool":"done",...}`) AND outside (lines 30-31, identical). The detector does `_vis_raw,_,_ = _scan_json_ops(<think-stripped>)` → ops=[edit_file] (len 1; `done` is returned separately, not counted). `_full_raw` over the full text → ops=[edit_file(think), edit_file(outside)] (len 2). Then `if len(_full_raw) > len(_vis_raw):` is 2>1 → TRUE, so it appends to round 1's RESULTS turn: "⚠ 1 JSON op(s) you wrote INSIDE a <think> block did NOT run — ops in your reasoning channel are ignored. Emit EVERY op you want executed OUTSIDE <think>, one per line."
findings.why_bug: The in-think edit_file is byte-identical to the one emitted outside that DID run (diff at lines 36-44 proves it applied). The model lost NOTHING — it used the canonical gpt-oss "plan the JSON in <think>, then output it" pattern, shown verbatim in this trace. Telling it "1 op did NOT run / emit it outside" is factually false and self-contradictory (it WAS outside, it DID run). For a weak coder this risks a double-apply (re-emitting an already-landed edit) or a wasted reconciliation round. The heuristic counts ops instead of comparing op identity.
findings.fix: Compare by op IDENTITY, not count. Build the multiset of (tool, json.dumps(args,sort_keys=True)) for `_vis_raw` and for `_full_raw`; warn only about full-set ops whose identity is NOT matched by a visible op (i.e. ops that ONLY exist in <think>). e.g. `_missing = _full_ids - _vis_ids` (multiset-aware), warn `len(_missing)` and only if >0. Re-drafting identical ops then produces no warning.

findings.title: verify-gate forces an extra round that the weak coder satisfies with a hollow re-assertion — no actual verification, pure round/API-call cost
findings.where: core/native_tools.py — call_with_json_ops verify-gate (lines ~3860-3864) + _JSON_OPS_VERIFY (~3673-3677)
findings.evidence: Round 1 the model already emitted `done` alongside the edit. Because files_changed is now non-empty and `_run_code_verified` is False, the gate appends _JSON_OPS_VERIFY ("Before you finish: re-trace your edit on the spec's hardest case (or run_code a quick check)...") and `continue`s. Round 2 the model does NOT run_code or re-inspect the diff — its reasoning (lines 48-50) just re-derives from the spec: "We did that. No other changes needed... We could run a quick check... Not needed. Just finalize." then re-emits the identical `done`.
findings.why_bug: The gate's intent (force a real verification pass) is not achieved — a weak model satisfies it by re-asserting "looks right" without looking at the applied diff or executing anything. Net effect on this trivial pure-literal edit (a content-anchored string swap the applier already verified): one wasted round + a 16k-max_tokens API call with zero added safety. There is no cheap-edit fast-path (e.g. skip verify when the only change is a within-line literal substitution with no indent/structural delta).
findings.fix: Add a fast-path: skip the verify nudge when every landed hunk is a within-line edit whose old/new differ only in a string/identifier substring (no line-count or indent delta) — the class with no structural risk. Or make the gate demand an ACTUAL verification artifact (a run_code op) rather than accepting a second bare `done`, so the round buys real signal instead of a hollow re-assertion.

findings.title: stacked, near-duplicate "when to finish" instructions across two consecutive user turns after an edit
findings.where: core/native_tools.py — _do_edit result string (~2022-2034) + RESULTS-turn footer (~3818-3821) + _JSON_OPS_VERIFY (~3673-3677)
findings.evidence: After the round-1 edit the model receives, in immediate succession: (a) the edit-result message ending with finish/anchor guidance ("...COPY the relevant line from your VIEW VERBATIM as `old`... You do NOT need to read_file again...", line 35); (b) the RESULTS footer appended to the SAME turn: "Emit your next ops, or `{\"tool\":\"done\"...}` when the edit is complete AND verified."; then (c) the next user turn, _JSON_OPS_VERIFY: "Before you finish: re-trace your edit... If it's correct, emit `done` again; otherwise emit the fixing edit_file op."
findings.why_bug: Three overlapping directives about how/when to finish land within two back-to-back user messages — redundant token load and mild contradiction (the result message says you don't need to read again and implies you can proceed/finish; the very next turn says don't finish, re-trace first). For a weak coder this is noise that dilutes the single signal it needs. It is also why round 2 produced only a hollow re-confirm.
findings.fix: When the verify-gate is about to fire, suppress the RESULTS-footer's "...or done when complete AND verified" clause (the verify message already owns the finish instruction), so the coder gets ONE clear next-step directive instead of two competing ones.

findings.title: PRE-LOADED RESEARCH for log.py truncated to 142/650 lines under a full-file "(650 lines)" header, omitting the very symbols being moved — with NO truncation marker
findings.where: core/tool_call.py CODE-tag handler / _store (the value cached into research_cache) + workflows/code.py:_format_research_cache
findings.evidence: The merger's PRE-LOADED RESEARCH block reads `=== Code: qutebrowser/utils/log.py (650 lines) ===` then shows only lines 1-142, ending at `142:0|greasemonkey = logging.getLogger('greasemonkey')` followed by a blank line and `--- model response ---`. The entire task is to move `hide_qt_warning` (log.py 362-372) and `QtWarningFilter` (log.py 404-419) — neither body appears anywhere in the research. The header claims 650 lines and there is no `…(truncated)…` notice, so the body silently stops at 142 (right after the logger declarations) with no signal that 508 lines are missing.
findings.why_bug: The merger is the ONLY refinement step and is told to verify drafts against real code, but the one file it most needs to see the source of is served as the irrelevant top-of-file (imports/format-constants/logger list) with a header that falsely implies the full 650-line file is present. A reader cannot distinguish 'symbol absent from file' from 'research was cut before the symbol'. This is the skeleton-trap failure class: the merger had to take the drafts' line-number claims (362-372 / 404-419) on faith because the cached artifact never actually contained those bodies. It also means the merger could not catch a wrong anchor even though the doctrine tells it to.
findings.fix: When a cached CODE entry is shorter than the line count in its own `(N lines)` header, append an explicit `…(NNN lines not shown — partial cache, re-read [VIEW: path L-R])…` marker, or store the full ≤1500-line file. At minimum make the header reflect the actual lines present (e.g. `lines 1-142 of 650`) so the merger knows it is partial.

findings.title: PRE-LOADED RESEARCH covers only log.py — none of the other 3 files the plan must edit (qtlog.py, run_vulture.py, qtnetworkdownloads.py) — forcing re-reads and an admitted unverified gap
findings.where: workflows/code.py merge driver / _format_research_cache (research_cache aggregation fed to the finalizer)
findings.evidence: The only cached entry in PRE-LOADED RESEARCH is log.py (1-142). The final plan touches 3 files — qtlog.py (STEP 1), log.py (STEP 2), run_vulture.py (STEP 3) — and references qtnetworkdownloads.py:124. The merger had to spend Round-1 tool calls (`[CODE: qutebrowser/utils/qtlog.py]`, two `[REFS:]`) re-reading qtlog because it wasn't cached, and still shipped with `## CONFIDENCE ... Gap: haven't verified run_vulture.py line 80 content directly (from pre-loaded research only).`
findings.why_bug: The PRE-LOADED RESEARCH banner says 'The planning AIs already looked these up. Use this data directly. If you need something NOT listed here, you can still use tool tags.' — but for this task the drafts cited run_vulture.py:80 and qtnetworkdownloads.py:32/124 as edit anchors, yet neither file's content was cached. The merger consumes a round re-reading qtlog and then cannot close the run_vulture anchor at all, so it emits a plan with an unverified file:line the coder will execute blind. The cache under-covers exactly the anchors the plan depends on.
findings.fix: Have the merge driver pre-load (or auto-RAG) every file named in a draft's `FILES:`/anchor lines, not just whichever file a planner happened to fully read. At minimum cache the small support files (run_vulture.py, qtnetworkdownloads.py) the drafts cite as edit sites before invoking the finalizer.

findings.title: INPUT PLAN #2 is a degenerate non-plan whose body is a leaked harness tool-reject/nudge message, passed verbatim to the merger un-stripped and un-filtered
findings.where: workflows/code.py merge-prompt assembly (input-plan sanitizer / degenerate-draft filter)
findings.evidence: `[INPUT PLAN #2] — by nemotron-3-super-120b-a12b` contains zero plan content: it is `[tool use] [VIEW: ...] [VIEW: ...] [/tool use]` followed by the harness's own reject text `[CODE: path L-R][/tool use]` then `[STOP][CONFIRM_STOP]` (a missing `]` or a tag outside [tool use] fires nothing). If you still need to read code, RE-ISSUE the call CORRECTLY. If you already have enough, write your === PLAN === / answer now. Do NOT give up on investigating.]` then `Continue: [/tool use]`. This whole reject-nudge string is fed to the merger as 'a draft plan to merge'.
findings.why_bug: A draft that is 100% tool-call noise plus a leaked runtime reject message carries no plan signal and actively injects contradictory instructions ('RE-ISSUE the call CORRECTLY', 'Do NOT give up on investigating') into the merge input. The `<think>`/`[SYSTEM NOTE]` strip and the degenerate-draft filter were added for exactly this class, but this leaked tool-reject variant slipped past both — the plan was neither dropped nor stripped. The merger had to spend reasoning to label it ('Plan #2 is incomplete/malformed'), and a weaker finalizer could try to honor the embedded 'RE-ISSUE'/'investigate' directives.
findings.fix: Extend the merge-input sanitizer to (a) strip leaked runtime reject/nudge text (the same family as `[SYSTEM NOTE]`), and (b) drop any draft that contains no `=== PLAN ===`/`### STEP`/`## GOAL` structure — a draft that is only `[tool use]` blocks + reject text is degenerate and should not occupy an INPUT PLAN slot.

findings.title: Round 2 wasted: model declared tool intent but emitted no [tool use], no plan, and no closing signal; harness advanced without a nudge and round-2 reasoning was discarded; leaked channel tokens 'think'/'text'
findings.where: core/tool_call.py:call_with_tools round loop (no-tool/no-signal handling + think-channel stripping) on the planner/merger path
findings.evidence: Round-2 model response ends: 'Let me verify the vulture whitelist and the qtnetworkdownloads import to confirm Plan #3's details.think' / 'text' ... 'I need to verify: - run_vulture.py line 80 ... - qtnetworkdownloads.py imports ... Then I'll write the final plan based on Plan #3.' No `[tool use]` block, no `[PLAN DONE]`, no closing signal appears. Round 3 then opens with 'I have all the information I need from the pre-loaded research and my Round 1 tool calls' — citing Round 1, not Round 2, so the Round-2 analysis was not carried forward. Bare tokens `think` and `text` (lines 2026-2027) are harmony channel-marker residue leaking into visible output.
findings.why_bug: The model narrated an intent to fire two verification lookups and to then write the plan, but emitted neither tools nor a signal — a fully wasted round. The runtime's documented behavior ('No signal AND no tool tags → treat as COMPLETE') did not fire (it gave a round 3), yet nothing nudged the model that it had stated a tool intent without wrapping it in `[tool use]`. The leaked `think`/`text` bare words indicate the channel-strip left residue, which (had this been an INPUT PLAN) would have polluted a downstream merge just like bug above.
findings.fix: When a round contains tool-tag intent phrasing or unwrapped tags but no executed `[tool use]` block and no signal, inject the existing 'a tag outside [tool use] fires nothing — re-issue inside [tool use]' nudge instead of silently advancing. Tighten the `<think>`/channel stripper so bare `think`/`text`/`analysis` channel markers don't survive into visible output.

findings.title: Re-emitted static INPUT PLANS + PRE-LOADED RESEARCH sit at the prompt tail while fresh tool results are buried above it; round-window also starts mid-word
findings.where: core/tool_call.py:call_with_tools prompt assembly/ordering for the merger; per-round window builder
findings.evidence: Rounds 2/3 are labeled 'showing last 40000 chars = newest tool results + manifest', but the actual tail of those windows is the static INPUT PLANS block followed by the truncated PRE-LOADED RESEARCH log.py, ending at `142:0|greasemonkey...` then `--- model response ---` — i.e. the tail is the SAME static, already-seen material re-emitted verbatim every round, NOT the newest tool results. The window also begins mid-word at `ion, a` (a sliced 'option, a'), showing the truncation is not boundary-aware.
findings.why_bug: The label's own assumption (tail = newest results + manifest) is contradicted by the content, indicating the freshest, most decision-relevant data (Round-1 REFS/CODE results) is positioned ABOVE the large redundant INPUT-PLANS/RESEARCH block rather than nearest the generation point. Recency-weighted models do worst on exactly the info pushed furthest from the end, and the mid-word slice can decapitate the [SYSTEM] FINALIZER framing / TOOL TABLE for later rounds.
findings.fix: Order the merger prompt so dynamic per-round tool results sit at the TAIL (closest to generation) and the bulky, unchanging INPUT PLANS / PRE-LOADED RESEARCH appear once, higher up; make any per-round char-window cut on a section/line boundary, not mid-word.

findings.title: Verify-gate is UNSATISFIABLE for a file whose import chain pulls an absent 3rd-party dep (PyQt5) — forces a wasted round AND pushes the coder into the exact stub/monkey-patch workaround the harness just forbade
findings.where: core/native_tools.py:call_with_json_ops — the finish verify-gate (lines 3860-3864), interacting with the run_code env-block path (_do run_code, ~2700-2718)
findings.evidence: Round 2 run_code result: "⚠ run_code: `import PyQt5` failed (ModuleNotFoundError)... this is an ENVIRONMENT limitation... NOT a bug in your edit... DO NOT: ... create stub/shim modules, wrap imports in try/except, or change your design to dodge PyQt5... Reason about PyQt5 statically and move on." Round 3 the model does exactly that and emits {"tool":"done",...}. But ROUND 4 still happens: the gate `if ctx.get("files_changed") and not _verify_nudged and not ctx.get("_run_code_verified")` fires because `_run_code_verified` is only set on a CLEAN exit-0 run (line 2689-2690), and a file importing PyQt5 can NEVER exit-0 in the smoke box. In round 4 the model then does the prohibited thing — `qtlog = types.ModuleType('qutebrowser.utils.qtlog')` + `sys.modules['qutebrowser.utils.qtlog'] = qtlog` (a stub/shim + monkey-patch) — which the round-2 message explicitly banned, and it ends in a confusing TypeError.
findings.why_bug: The verify-gate demands a green run_code before accepting the first `done`, but for any file whose transitive imports need an unavailable 3rd-party package the gate is mathematically unsatisfiable. This (a) burns an extra streaming round — the very wall-clock cost ckpt-221's `_run_code_verified` skip was meant to avoid on timeout-prone instances — and (b) directly contradicts the harness's own environmental-ModuleNotFoundError message, steering the weak coder toward stub modules / sys.modules monkey-patching / import-dodging that corrupt the patch. The correct edit had already landed in round 1; rounds 3-4 were pure harness-induced churn.
findings.fix: The data needed is ALREADY tracked: the run_code env-block path does `ctx.setdefault("_failed_imports", set()).add(_mod)` (line 2716). Extend the verify-gate skip to treat an env-blocked verify as satisfied: change `... and not ctx.get("_run_code_verified")` to also skip when `ctx.get("_failed_imports")` is non-empty (verification was attempted but is environmentally impossible). Accept the first `done` instead of forcing a round that can only end in a forbidden workaround.

findings.title: `new` as an empty STRING in an `edits` array leaves a residual blank line instead of deleting — inconsistent with `new=[]` and with the single-hunk old/new path
findings.where: core/native_tools.py:_do edit_file — `_as_list` helper (lines 1732-1737) used for the `edits` array path, vs the `_as` helper (line 1688) used for the single old/new shorthand
findings.evidence: Round 1 the model sent `edits` hunks with `"new": ""` for both removals (it reasoned: "For removal, old is the block, new is empty string? Probably new should be ''"). The resulting CUMULATIVE diff shows a stray ADDED blank line: `371:-         logger_obj.removeFilter(log_filter)` immediately followed by `364:+ ` (a `+` blank). The 10-line block became one blank line, not zero. In code, `_as_list("")` does `str("").split("\n")` → `[""]` (one blank), whereas the single-hunk `_as` does `[] if v in (None, "") else ...` → `[]` (full delete), and the reject message at line 1699 documents `new=[]` deletes ALL of old.
findings.why_bug: Two documented-equivalent ways to express the same edit diverge: `new=""` deletes fully via the old/new shorthand but leaves a residual blank line via the `edits` array. The model intended clean removal and silently got an extra blank between top-level defs. Low test-impact (Python tolerates extra blanks) but it is a genuine off-by-one inconsistency that produces an artifact the model did not ask for and could compound across multiple removals.
findings.fix: Make `_as_list` treat an empty/whitespace-only string the same as the single-hunk `_as`: map `new==""` (and `None`) to `[]` (full delete) rather than `[""]`. Keep both code paths sharing one normalizer so empty-new means delete-all everywhere.

findings.title: Verify nudge text suggests `run_code` right after the harness told the coder run_code can't import this module
findings.where: core/native_tools.py:_JSON_OPS_VERIFY constant (lines 3673-3677), emitted by the verify-gate at line 3862
findings.evidence: _JSON_OPS_VERIFY: "Before you finish: re-trace your edit on the spec's hardest case (or run_code a quick check). If it's correct, emit ... done ... again; otherwise emit the fixing edit_file op." This is appended in round 3 — one round after the harness told the model `import PyQt5 failed (ModuleNotFoundError) ... reason statically and move on`. The 'or run_code a quick check' clause points the model back at the exact action just declared impossible, and in round 4 the model duly tries another run_code (via a forbidden monkey-patch).
findings.why_bug: The nudge is generic and ignores per-step state the harness already has (`_failed_imports`). For an import-blocked file it should steer to a pure STATIC re-trace, not 'run_code a quick check', otherwise it reinforces the impossible-verification trap.
findings.fix: When `ctx.get("_failed_imports")` is non-empty (or, better, once the gate skips per finding #1), drop the 'or run_code a quick check' clause and ask only for a static re-trace; or skip the verify nudge entirely for env-blocked files.

findings.title: Cumulative diff mixes OLD and NEW line numbering, so the SAME line number renders as both a context line and a deleted line, and a context line gets interleaved inside a deletion block
findings.where: core/edit_diff.py:render_diff (deletions numbered `k+1` in BEFORE space, lines 57-58; context/additions numbered `j1+k+1` in AFTER space, lines 50-52, 60-61)
findings.evidence: After the round-1 insertion shifted the file +2, the round-1 result diff shows `362:  ` and `363:  ` (blank CONTEXT, new numbering) immediately followed by `362:- @contextlib.contextmanager` and `363:- def hide_qt_warning(...)` (deletions, old numbering) — i.e. line numbers 362 and 363 each appear TWICE with different content. In the QtWarningFilter block it is worse: `404:- class QtWarningFilter(logging.Filter):` then `397:  ` (a CONTEXT blank, new numbering) then `406:-     """Filter to filter Qt warnings.` — difflib matched the deleted blank old-405 to new-397 as 'equal', so a context line sits in the middle of the deletion at a non-monotonic number.
findings.why_bug: The coder is repeatedly instructed to anchor edits on the line number AND content from its view. A cumulative diff in which the same integer denotes two different lines, and context lines appear inside a deletion run, is misleading for a weak model. It is partly mitigated by the result text ('do NOT copy an old from it — that's not the editable form'), so impact is bounded, but the rendering is genuinely confusing across any step that inserts before a later deletion.
findings.fix: In render_diff, when before/after numbering has diverged, make the divergence legible: print BOTH old and new numbers on changed rows (e.g. `362→364:-`), or emit git-style `@@ -old,+new @@` hunk headers per group so the two numbering spaces are never silently conflated. At minimum, suppress 'equal' rows that are pure blank-line matches inside an otherwise-contiguous deletion so context lines don't interleave the removal.

findings.title: Verify-gate forces a wasted round-4 re-`done` after run_code was already attempted and env-blocked
findings.where: core/native_tools.py:call_with_json_ops (verify gate, L3850-3868) + _do_run_code (_run_code_verified flag, L2685-2690)
findings.evidence: Step header says '4 rounds'. R2 the coder runs run_code; it fails: 'import PyQt5 failed (ModuleNotFoundError)... this is an ENVIRONMENT limitation of the smoke box, NOT a bug in your edit... Reason about PyQt5 statically and move on.' R3 the coder emits `{"tool":"done",...}`. The step does NOT end — R4 appears with reasoning 'We need to verify behavior' and emits the IDENTICAL `done` again with no new op. Source: the gate at L3860 `if ctx.get('files_changed') and not _verify_nudged and not ctx.get('_run_code_verified')` fires the _JSON_OPS_VERIFY nudge and `continue`s; `_run_code_verified` is set True ONLY on a clean exit-0 (L2685-2690), so the R2 env-blocked run (sys.exit(1)) leaves it False.
findings.why_bug: An env-blocked run_code attempt (the package transitively imports PyQt5, so it can NEVER pass in the smoke box) is indistinguishable from 'never tried' to the gate, so the gate guarantees one wasted streaming round (R4 = byte-identical re-`done`, zero new action). It also directly contradicts the run_code message the harness itself just emitted ('reason statically and move on'). The 'verify-gate skip when run_code already passed' fix doesn't cover 'run_code was RUN but failed only on an environmental ModuleNotFoundError'. On the timeout-prone ansible-style steps this is exactly the wall-clock waste the gate-skip was meant to prevent.
findings.fix: In the verify gate, also skip when a run_code was attempted this step whose ONLY failure was an environmental ModuleNotFoundError for a third-party dep (the harness already records this set in ctx['_failed_imports'] at L2716). Treat 'verify was attempted but impossible in the sandbox' as satisfying the gate (accept the first `done`), or set a ctx['_run_code_attempted'] flag the gate can read.

findings.title: ModuleNotFoundError run_code message says 'reason statically and move on' but offers no static syntax-check command, while the verify-gate demands a verify pass
findings.where: core/native_tools.py:_do_run_code ModuleNotFoundError branch (L2717-2726)
findings.evidence: The message (L2717-2726) ends 'Reason about PyQt5 statically and move on.' It gives no concrete command to verify the edited file WITHOUT triggering the package import. The sibling __pycache__ branch (L2736-2741) does hand a `python -B -c` recipe and even notes 'py_compile/compileall write bytecode... will still fail here'. The coder spent R2-R4 wanting to verify but had no viable path.
findings.why_bug: The harness simultaneously (a) blocks the only verification the coder tried, (b) tells it to 'move on', and (c) via the verify-gate (finding 1) demands a verification pass before accepting `done`. With no offered way to actually verify, the coder loops on re-justification. A static AST parse imports nothing and avoids the read-only __pycache__ OSError.
findings.fix: Append to the ModuleNotFoundError branch: 'To syntax-check your edit WITHOUT importing the package, run `python -B -c "import ast; ast.parse(open(\'<edited file>\').read())"`.' (Note: NOT py_compile/compileall — they write .pyc and fail in the read-only box, as the __pycache__ branch already documents.) This lets the coder satisfy the verify-gate.

findings.title: Post-edit message discourages re-reading, but just-inserted lines have no editable-form anchor anywhere
findings.where: core/native_tools.py edit-result message builder (L2023-2033)
findings.evidence: The R1 result message says: 'You do NOT need to read_file again — your start-state + this diff IS qtlog.py's current state (read a range only for a region you've truly never seen).' and 'The diff below is in `LINENO:+/- ` format — read it to SEE what changed, but do NOT copy an `old` from it (that's not the editable form).' The 18 newly-inserted lines (class QtWarningFilter / hide_qt_warning) exist ONLY in the diff's non-editable `LINENO:+/-` form; they are NOT in the start-state view the coder was given.
findings.why_bug: If the coder needs to edit code it JUST added (e.g. fix the docstring it flagged in R4), it has no `LINENO ⇥INDENT|` editable anchor: the start-state view predates the insertion, and the message forbids copying `old` from the diff AND discourages re-reading ('region you've truly never seen' is ambiguous for lines it wrote but never saw in editable form). A latent stale-anchor/reject trap. It did not bite here only because the coder finished without a second edit.
findings.fix: Carve out the just-inserted region explicitly: either (a) re-emit the newly added lines in editable `LINENO ⇥INDENT|` form in the result, or (b) reword to 'to change a line you just ADDED, read_file that range first — it isn't in your original view in editable form.'

findings.title: Budget-nudge round counter is off-by-one: the model is told "Round 4/8" on its 5th turn, and the hard "NO ROUNDS LEFT" final-push is never delivered
findings.where: core/tool_call.py: call_with_tools — budget_msg block (rounds_used = round_num at ~5375; templates ~5382-5429), prompt assembled at ~5854 and consumed at the TOP of the next loop iteration (for round_num in range(1, max_rounds+1) at 3237)
findings.evidence: Round-5 prompt, trace line 4438: "⚠ Round 4/8 — past halfway, 4 round(s) left. START YOUR DRAFT PLAN NOW...". The [YOUR TOOL INDEX] right above it (lines 4427-4431) lists 4 completed rounds (R1 LS, R2 CODEx2, R3 REFS, R4 CODE) — so this is the model's 5th turn, yet it is labeled "Round 4". Planner max_rounds=8 (workflows/code.py:8756).
findings.why_bug: The budget message is computed with rounds_used=round_num = the round that JUST finished, then embedded into the prompt shown on the NEXT turn. So every budget nudge is mislabeled by one round, and "past halfway" is emitted exactly AT the halfway point (round_num==max_rounds//2==4) rather than past it — internally inconsistent with the displayed "4/8". Worse consequence (same root cause): the strongest pressure branch (rounds_left<=0 → "⛔ NO ROUNDS LEFT. This is your FINAL response. Write your COMPLETE plan...") only triggers at round_num>=max_rounds, i.e. the last loop iteration (round_num=8), whose continuation prompt is discarded because the loop then exits — so the planner NEVER sees the hard final-round stop on its actual last turn (round 8 sees only the round_num=7 "1 round left" message). All budget pressure thus arrives one round late.
findings.fix: Label and gate the budget message against the round the model is ABOUT to take, not the one that just finished: use rounds_used = round_num + 1 (and rounds_left = max_rounds - (round_num+1) + 1) when building the continuation prompt, OR move the budget computation to the top of the loop keyed on the current round_num before sending. Verify the rounds_left<=0 "FINAL response" branch actually reaches the model on its last executable round.

findings.title: Assembled USER REQUEST contains a direct contradiction: the issue's stated remedy ("relocate the tests") versus RULE 2 ("Do NOT add or modify any test files")
findings.where: swe_bench task-prompt assembly (RULES block + ISSUE/REQUIREMENTS injected into the planner prompt); rendered in the trace USER REQUEST section
findings.evidence: RULE 2 (trace line 1647/881): "Only edit source files. Do NOT add or modify any test files." ISSUE (trace line 891/1657): "The hide_qt_warning function and its associated tests have been moved... The tests need to be relocated to ensure they continue validating...". The model burned its entire Round-1 reasoning reconciling them (lines 1190-1193): "Wait, the issue says 'tests need to be relocated'. But the rules say 'Do NOT add or modify any test files'."
findings.why_bug: The harness presents an issue whose described fix (move the tests) is forbidden by an injected rule, with no reconciliation. A capable model resolves it (the real contract is the source-only INTERFACE TO IMPLEMENT + the eval's own new test), but the contradiction wastes a reasoning round and could steer a weaker planner to either edit a test file (violating RULE 2) or conclude the task is unsatisfiable. This is the "contradictory instruction" class explicitly in scope.
findings.fix: When the issue text describes test relocation but the rules forbid test edits, the task wrapper should strip/neutralize the 'tests need to be relocated' clause from the injected issue, or add a one-line reconciliation note (e.g. 'the test move is handled by the evaluation harness; only move the source symbols').

findings.title: INTERFACE TO IMPLEMENT under-specifies a MOVE: it lists only the destination symbols (qtlog.py) and says 'create/modify EXACTLY these', omitting the required removal from log.py and the caller/import update
findings.where: swe_bench INTERFACE-TO-IMPLEMENT synthesis (injected into planner prompt); rendered trace lines 914-927
findings.evidence: Trace 914-927 lists only "Name: hide_qt_warning / Path: qutebrowser/utils/qtlog.py" and "Name: QtWarningFilter / Path: qutebrowser/utils/qtlog.py" with the prefix "Create/modify EXACTLY these symbols at these paths". It does NOT name the deletion from qutebrowser/utils/log.py nor the caller update at qutebrowser/browser/qtnetworkdownloads.py:124 (which the model had to discover itself via REFS in R3/R4).
findings.why_bug: For a relocation, the contract isn't just 'add to qtlog.py' — it's 'add to qtlog.py AND remove from log.py AND rewire callers', otherwise the symbol is duplicated/imports break. The word 'EXACTLY' framed around the add-targets can anchor a weaker planner to an add-only plan that leaves log.py defining the same symbols and qtnetworkdownloads.py importing from the old location → broken move. The harness handed an incomplete map of the change's real reach.
findings.fix: When the interface is derived from a move/rename gold patch, include the removal site and any caller/import sites (or at least a note 'this is a relocation — remove from the old module and update callers'), rather than only the destination signatures.

findings.title: Verify-gate forces a wasted extra `done` round; an environment-blocked run_code is never credited as a verify attempt
findings.where: core/native_tools.py — call_with_json_ops verify-gate (~line 3860) + _do_run setting ctx['_run_code_verified'] only on exit-0 (~line 2689)
findings.evidence: Gate: `if ctx.get("files_changed") and not _verify_nudged and not ctx.get("_run_code_verified"): _verify_nudged = True; messages.append(... _JSON_OPS_VERIFY); continue`. _run_code_verified is set ONLY on a clean exit-0 (line 2690 `ctx["_run_code_verified"] = True`). In coder_step_1 the model DID verify: round 2 ran run_code, which returned `⚠ run_code: \`import PyQt5\` failed (ModuleNotFoundError) ... this is an ENVIRONMENT limitation ... NOT a bug in your edit`. Because that exit was non-zero, _run_code_verified stayed False, so round 3's `done` was rejected by the verify-nudge and the step burned round 4 re-emitting `done`. coder_step_3 (a 1-line whitelist edit, run_code impossible/pointless) likewise spent round 2 just re-emitting `done` after the gate fired.
findings.why_bug: The harness itself classifies the run_code failure as 'ENVIRONMENT, NOT YOUR BUG', yet refuses to count that same run as a verification attempt — so for ANY edit to a module that imports an absent 3rd-party dep (PyQt5/django/jinja2 — the common qutebrowser case) the coder can never satisfy the gate and is forced into an extra streaming round. Wall-clock per step is the documented timeout wall, so a guaranteed extra round on the import-heavy steps is a real drag.
findings.fix: In _do_run, when the result hits the ModuleNotFoundError/ImportError env branch (it already populates ctx['_failed_imports']) or the read-only-__pycache__ branch, set a per-step flag (e.g. ctx['_run_code_attempted']=True). In the verify-gate, accept the first `done` when EITHER _run_code_verified OR that env-blocked-attempt flag is set — the coder verified as far as the smoke sandbox allows.

findings.title: Assembled prompt contradicts itself on the `batch` tool — base prose says 'Do it in ONE round with batch()', the JSON-OPS override says there is no batch tool
findings.where: core/native_tools.py — call_with_json_ops assembly `system = finalize_coder_system(system) + _JSON_OPS_PROMPT` (line 3705); base text in core/prompts_v8.py IMPLEMENT_NATIVE_PROMPT (tool list + GATHER, prompts_v8.py:1985)
findings.evidence: prompt_round0.txt line 11 fully documents `- batch(calls) — run SEVERAL read-only lookups in ONE round ...`, and line 25 (GATHER) orders: `Do it in ONE round with batch(): name what each lookup answers ...`. Then line 72 reverses it: `there is NO \`batch\` tool here — emitting several ops (one per line) in a round IS the batch, so never wrap them.`
findings.why_bug: A weak gpt-oss reading top-to-bottom is told to USE batch() by the GATHER discipline, then told batch doesn't exist. If it obeys the earlier imperative it emits the nested `{"tool":"batch","args":{"calls":[...]}}` array — which is exactly the nested-bracket structure the JSON-OPS mode was created to avoid because gpt-oss mis-closes it (per the _coalesce_edit_ops comment) → json.loads fails → 0 ops parsed → empty-turn nudge → wasted round. Project doctrine treats a model-facing instruction that contradicts the live format as a P0.
findings.fix: When building the JSON-OPS system prompt, scrub the batch references from the reused native prose (or swap GATHER's 'Do it in ONE round with batch()' for 'emit all your lookup ops together, one JSON object per line'). Don't rely on a single override sentence 60 lines later to undo an imperative the model already read.

findings.title: Stale 'read on demand' boilerplate emitted even when the step has zero read-on-demand / overflow files
findings.where: workflows/code.py — nat_user assembly (~line 11125, the unconditional trailing sentence) + core/prompts_v8.py GATHER (prompts_v8.py:1985)
findings.evidence: prompt_round0.txt closing line 374: `Read the 'read on demand' files only when you reach them.` and GATHER line 25: `Some of the step's files are already loaded above; others are listed "read on demand".` But STEP 3 loads exactly one fully-shown file (run_vulture.py, 230 lines, no overflow) — there is no 'read on demand' list anywhere in the prompt. In code, the `_overflow_clause` was correctly made conditional (ckpt-220 #6) but the trailing `Read the 'read on demand' files only when you reach them.` sentence was left unconditional.
findings.why_bug: The prompt references a category of files that does not exist for this step, inviting the coder to go hunting for non-existent 'read on demand' files (a wasted lookup round) and contradicting the immediately-preceding 'already loaded — edit THOSE directly'. Misleading on every single-file, no-overflow step.
findings.fix: Gate the 'Read the read-on-demand files only when you reach them' sentence on `if overflow:` (same condition as `_overflow_clause`), and make GATHER's '...others are listed read on demand' clause conditional on there actually being deferred files.

findings.title: Prompt steers the coder to verify with run_code, but run_code can never load a module that imports an absent 3rd-party dep
findings.where: core/prompts_v8.py — IMPLEMENT_NATIVE_PROMPT system line ('verify ideally with run_code') + GATHER ideal-shape 'VERIFY (run_code or re-trace)'; surfaced in prompt_round0.txt lines 5 and 25
findings.evidence: prompt_round0.txt line 5: `you are the last gate, so verify (ideally with run_code, which runs your live sandbox) before you finish.` and JSON-OPS rule (5) line 71: `round 3 = a run_code check`. In coder_step_1/2 the resulting run_code (`importlib.import_module('qutebrowser.utils.qtlog')`) dies at `from PyQt5.QtCore import *` → ModuleNotFoundError before reaching the edited code at all.
findings.why_bug: For any edit inside a qutebrowser module (which transitively imports PyQt5) a full-module import can NEVER succeed in the stdlib-only smoke box, so the 'ideally run_code' steering reliably spends a round producing a scary traceback the harness then has to relabel as environmental. The prompt offers 're-trace' only as a parenthetical alternative; it never tells the coder that when the target module imports an unavailable dep, run_code is futile and re-trace is the correct verify.
findings.fix: Add a carve-out to the verify guidance: 'If the file you edited imports a 3rd-party package (PyQt5/django/…), a full import/pytest run will ModuleNotFoundError in the smoke box — don't bother; re-trace the edit by hand or run_code only a stdlib-only `python -c` exercising the isolated changed logic.'

findings.title: Merger tool-call results are never returned to the model — every read is a black hole, all 5 rounds wasted, no plan produced
findings.where: workflows/code.py merger/planner round loop (next-round prompt assembly for the plan-FINALIZER role) — the step that should append the VIEW/CODE result + YOUR TOOL INDEX before re-prompting
findings.evidence: The model requests the SAME range every round and never advances: R1 line 1114 `[VIEW: lib/ansible/utils/collection_loader/_collection_finder.py 840-860]`; R2 line 1811 the identical `[VIEW: ... 840-860]` now correctly wrapped in `[tool use]…[/tool use]` + `[STOP][CONFIRM_STOP]`; R3 line 2511 re-requests `840-860` AGAIN plus `1-25`; R4 lines 3216/3220 again `840-860` + `1-25`; R5 lines 3923/3927 again `1-30`/`840-860`. If the R2 well-formed read had fired and its output reached the model, it would not re-request 840-860 in R3/R4/R5. Each round prompt is labelled `showing last 40000 chars = newest tool results + manifest` (e.g. line 1117) yet the displayed tail is the static base prompt ending at INPUT PLAN #3 (`whether str.isidentifier() is available on a`) with ZERO tool-result content anywhere — the file the model read is never present in the prompt it sees next round.
findings.why_bug: A merger that can never see what it reads cannot ground or write a plan; it loops on identical reads until the round budget is spent, then the run falls back to a weaker raw draft (matches the known f327 planner regression). The harness mishandled the tool OUTPUT: it dropped the read result instead of feeding it into the next round.
findings.fix: In the merger/planner round driver, after executing the round's tool calls, append the rendered results to the next-round prompt in a dedicated section that survives truncation (place it AFTER the static [INPUT PLANS] block, not before it). Verify offline by rendering round 3's assembled prompt and confirming the 840-860 VIEW output text is actually present.

findings.title: Static [INPUT PLANS] re-appended verbatim each round while dynamic read-state/manifest is dropped; promised CONTEXT MANIFEST re-read guard never renders
findings.where: core/prompts_v8.py merger prompt assembly + the round loop that builds CONTEXT MANIFEST / YOUR TOOL INDEX sections
findings.evidence: The 3 full input plans are reproduced identically in every round (R1 ~899-1109, R2 ~1597-1807, R3, R4, R5 ~3705-3916) — large and unchanging — but no `CONTEXT MANIFEST` or `YOUR TOOL INDEX` section ever appears, even after the model has read files. The prompt promises (lines 328-330) `CONTEXT MANIFEST ── files already loaded into context (⛔ no re-reads on these)`, and reflexes warn against `Re-reading a file already in [CONTEXT MANIFEST] (marked ⛔)` (line 92), but that guard is structurally absent for the merger.
findings.why_bug: Because the harness retains only the static input and never builds the manifest/tool-index of what the model already read, the anti-re-read guard can never engage, so nothing breaks the identical-read loop; the model has no record it already asked for 840-860.
findings.fix: Render a CONTEXT MANIFEST (and YOUR TOOL INDEX) for the merger role listing files/ranges already read this session, marked ⛔, so the re-read guard fires; this is the structural backstop for the dropped-result bug above.

findings.title: Tool tag emitted without a [tool use] wrapper or without [STOP] fires nothing AND produces no diagnostic — model gets no learning signal and loops
findings.where: core/tool_call.py / the planner round driver's tool-extraction + the missing-signal SYSTEM NOTE path
findings.evidence: R1 (line 1114) the model emits a bare `[VIEW: …840-860]` with no `[tool use]` wrapper and no `[STOP]`; per line 879 `Tags outside a [tool use] block are ignored`, so nothing fired — but the model received NO note that its read was ignored. By R3/R5 the model has degraded to un-bracketed `tool use` / bare `VIEW` with JSON-array args (R5 lines 3921-3927: `tool use` / `VIEW` / `["lib/ansible/utils/collection_loader/_collection_finder.py", 1, 30]`), none of which fire, still with zero feedback. The harness DOES inject a `[SYSTEM NOTE]` for a half-`[STOP]` (lines 47-49) but gives silence for an unwrapped/no-STOP tool tag.
findings.why_bug: The model cannot self-correct a malformed tool call it gets no feedback on; the silent no-op directly feeds the wasted-round loop and the syntax drift into JSON-array form.
findings.fix: When a response contains a tool tag (e.g. [VIEW:]/[CODE:]) that is NOT inside a [tool use]…[/tool use] block or lacks [STOP], inject a one-shot SYSTEM NOTE: 'A tool tag was detected outside [tool use]/without [STOP]; nothing fired — wrap it and end with [STOP][CONFIRM_STOP].' mirroring the existing half-STOP note.

findings.title: Merger round-3 'STOP reading and WRITE the plan' hard rule is not enforced — model reads through rounds 4-5 and emits no plan
findings.where: planner/merger round loop — round-budget enforcement for the FINALIZER
findings.evidence: Prompt sets a HARD RULE (lines 387-390): `by your THIRD round, STOP reading and WRITE the === PLAN ===, marking any still-open point in ## CONFIDENCE`. The model is still issuing read tool calls in R3 (line 2511), R4 (line 3216), and R5 (line 3923), and never produces a `=== PLAN ===` block.
findings.why_bug: The stated round-3 stop-and-write discipline exists only as prose; the harness never forces it, so a stuck merger burns rounds 4-5 reading and ships nothing — exactly the failure the rule is meant to prevent (`if you never write one, the run falls back to a weaker draft`).
findings.fix: At round 3+ for the merger, if no `=== PLAN ===` has been emitted, suppress further tool execution and inject a forcing nudge ('Investigation budget exhausted — write the === PLAN === now from the drafts; note open points in ## CONFIDENCE'), guaranteeing a committed plan over a fallback.

findings.title: Glued-into-prose ops are silently dropped with no "no-op parsed" nudge → model spirals and nearly false-aborts a solvable task
findings.where: core/native_tools.py JSON-OPS op extraction loop (the line-by-line {"tool":"...","args":"..."} parser / _salvage_inline_tool_call) + the zero-op turn handler
findings.evidence: Line 674 is a SINGLE reasoning turn in which the coder emits ~8 ops concatenated into prose, e.g. `...Let's try reading a small range around line 10.{"tool":"read_file","args":{... "start_line":5,"end_line":15}}No output. Possibly the tool requires quoting?...{"tool":"read_file",..."start_line":1,"end_line":10}}No output...{"tool":"search_text",..."pattern":"import sys"}}...{"tool":"list_dir","args":{"path":"."}}No output again.` NONE of these surface a result; the model concludes `Possibly the system only shows view when new content is added... the system suppressed output due to length`. It nearly kills the task at line 1082: `{"tool":"done","args":{"summary":"Unable to retrieve file contents due to tool response issues; cannot apply required changes."}}`.
findings.why_bug: When a turn's {tool,args} JSON is glued to surrounding prose (not on its own line) the JSON-OPS parser extracts ZERO ops and the harness returns nothing actionable — and crucially gives NO feedback ('no op parsed; emit one {"tool":...} per line'). A weak model interprets the silence as 'the tools are broken' and rationally moves to abort. Rounds were wasted and a fully solvable 2-hunk task came one op away from a false done/give-up.
findings.fix: (a) Salvage {tool,args} substrings even when concatenated with prose / inside an unclosed <think> (regex-extract balanced JSON objects, not just whole-line ops). (b) When a turn yields prose but zero parseable ops, ALWAYS send an explicit nudge ('I parsed no tool op this turn — emit exactly one {"tool":...,"args":...} per line, outside any prose') instead of returning silence. (c) Optionally suppress a `done` whose summary admits failure (e.g. contains 'unable'/'cannot apply') when no edit has been applied this step, and re-prompt.

findings.title: `keep` is an ineffective no-op that falsely reports success ('the rest of its view is dropped')
findings.where: core/native_tools.py _do_keep / tools/codebase.py view-state 'kept ranges' application (the revealed-set is not actually pruned)
findings.evidence: Round 5 op `{"tool":"keep","args":{"path":"...","ranges":[[1,200]]}}` returns (line 834): `✓ keep: keeping lines 1-200 ...; the rest of its view is dropped from your context (read_file a range to bring any of it back).` But the GROWING-VIEW revealed-count is 332 immediately BEFORE keep (line 684) and still 332 immediately AFTER, on the next read (line 846) — and lines 660-683 (revealed in earlier rounds, supposedly 'dropped') still render in that post-keep view (lines 1052-1076). The count only falls to 200 later at line 1086, caused by a full `read 1-200` supersession, not by keep. The model itself notes at line 1080 'the keep operation doesn't display content' and works around it.
findings.why_bug: keep's whole purpose is context management — dropping revealed regions to free budget. Here it changed nothing (332→332) yet emitted a ✓ success claiming the rest was dropped. The model spent a full round on a tool that did nothing and was given a misleading confirmation, deepening the round-4–6 navigation spiral.
findings.fix: Make keep actually prune the revealed-set to the kept ranges (recompute the revealed-count and re-render with the now-collapsed regions), OR — if keep is intentionally deprecated under JSON-OPS — stop advertising/accepting it and return a clear redirect. The success string must reflect the real post-state (e.g. report the new revealed-count).

findings.title: Edit-success message buries the diff under ~900 chars of meta-instruction prose on every successful edit
findings.where: workflows/code.py _apply_extracted_code — the cumulative-diff success banner
findings.evidence: Line 1554 success banner: `✓ Applied 2 edit(s) ... The diff below is the CUMULATIVE change ... that start-state + this diff = ...'s CURRENT, live state; everything NOT in the diff is UNCHANGED. TRUST that — your view is NOT stale (your old was anchored on its line number AND content, so a shifted view self-corrects). For your next change here, COPY the relevant line from your VIEW VERBATIM as old ... You do NOT need to read_file again ...` — several sentences of repeated 'trust the diff / don't re-read' guidance precede the actual 8-line diff.
findings.why_bug: On a SUCCESS, the actionable content is the diff; the wall of repeated reassurances ('TRUST that', 'NOT stale', 'do NOT read_file again', 'COPY ... VERBATIM') is noise that a weak coder must parse every edit. It dilutes the signal and — given this same model just spent rounds doubting tool output — risks the meta-text being misread as a new instruction.
findings.fix: Trim the success banner to one line plus the diff (e.g. '✓ Applied N edit(s) (step S, round R). Cumulative diff vs step start:'). Move the 'anchored on line#+content / no need to re-read' explanation into the system prompt once, not onto every edit result.

findings.title: PRE-LOADED RESEARCH for wikidata.py is truncated to 19 lines mid-file, hiding every anchor line the merger must reason about
findings.where: workflows/code.py — merge/finalizer prompt assembly that injects the PRE-LOADED RESEARCH / research-cache block (the cached [CODE:] result from draft #3)
findings.evidence: Lines 1172-1192: `=== Code: openlibrary/core/wikidata.py (158 lines) ===` then only lines 1-19 are shown, ending at `19:0|WIKIDA` — cut off at line 19, RIGHT before the WikidataEntity class. Yet the drafts and the merged plan all cite lines the merger never sees: class `at line 24`, `statements` annotation `at line 35`, insert `after line 55, before the @classmethod at line 57`. The merger's own [think] (line 1215) concludes `No tool calls needed — the research is complete and all plans agree`, and the final plan asserts `PRECISION = 10 — All file:line citations are grounded in the pre-loaded research` (line 1298) — but the research contains NONE of lines 24/35/55/57.
findings.why_bug: The merger prompt's own contract (lines 222-226) says files ≤1500 lines `return in full`; wikidata.py is 158 lines so the full file should be present. Instead 139 of 158 lines were silently dropped. The merger therefore cannot verify a single edit anchor, while the framing `do NOT re-search these` + the truncated body presented as complete actively traps it into shipping unverified line citations (35/55/57) as if grounded. If any of those line numbers are off, the coder gets a wrong anchor with no upstream catch.
findings.fix: When injecting the research cache into the merge prompt, emit the FULL cached file body for files ≤ the documented full-return cap (the merger needs anchors); if a hard char budget forces truncation, (a) keep the region the drafts cite, not just the file head, and (b) append an explicit `… N lines truncated, re-read with [CODE:]` marker so the merger knows the body is partial and `research is complete` is false.

findings.title: Misleading "(158 lines)" header with a mid-identifier cut and no truncation marker
findings.where: workflows/code.py / tools.codebase view renderer used for the cached research block
findings.evidence: Header line 1172 advertises `(158 lines)`; body stops at line 1191 `19:0|WIKIDA` — a corrupt last line cut in the middle of the `WIKIDATA…` identifier, with no `… truncated` notice anywhere before the `--- model response ---` separator at 1193.
findings.why_bug: A weak model takes the `(158 lines)` count at face value (the merger did — it called the research `complete`). The last rendered line `19:0|WIKIDA` is a half-token that, if ever copied as an `[edit]`/anchor, matches nothing. Even when truncation is intentional, the count must reflect what is shown and a marker must flag the cut.
findings.fix: Truncate on a line boundary, set the header count to the lines actually shown (or `showing 1-19 of 158`), and append a one-line `⚠ truncated — N lines omitted` marker the same way the SKELETON path already does.

findings.title: Input plan #3 is injected raw with unstripped [tool use]/[CODE:]/[think] protocol tags and ~50 lines of reasoning noise
findings.where: workflows/code.py — merge-input assembly (the same place that strips native <think>/[SYSTEM NOTE] from merge inputs)
findings.evidence: Lines 1050-1102: INPUT PLAN #3 begins with live-looking protocol tags `[tool use]` / `[CODE: openlibrary/core/wikidata.py]` / `[/tool use]` then a ~50-line `[think]` reasoning dump (1052-1102: "Wait, the type hint for `statements`…", "Let's double check the requirements again…") before its actual `=== PLAN ===` at 1104. Drafts #1 and #2 (927-1046) are clean plan-only. The `[think]` opened at 1052 has no visible `[/think]` before `=== PLAN ===` at 1104.
findings.why_bug: The recently-shipped fix strips native `<think>`/`[SYSTEM NOTE]` from merge inputs, but bracket `[think]` and `[tool use]`/`[CODE:]` blocks slip through. This bloats the merger prompt with one draft's raw scratchpad, presents live-looking tool/think tags as if they were plan content, and the apparently-unclosed `[think]` is exactly the swallow-hazard the prompt warns about — confusing a weak merger about where draft #3's plan actually starts.
findings.fix: Extend the merge-input sanitizer to strip bracket `[think]…[/think]` and any `[tool use]…[/tool use]` / bare tool tags from each input plan, and slice each draft to its `=== PLAN === … === END PLAN ===` span before injection (drafts #1/#2 already arrive clean — #3 should too).

findings.title: Duplicate "Verify claims against real code" block redefines [PURPOSE:] and [SEMANTIC:] in contradiction with the TOOL TABLE
findings.where: core/prompts_v8.py — the planner/merger CORE prompt's appended post-PROJECT-CONTEXT verification block (lines 910-920)
findings.evidence: TOOL TABLE: `[PURPOSE: path]` = `file gist — module docstring + each public def/class's signature` (lines 173-178) and `[SEMANTIC: query]` = `embedding search over the CODE itself (functions/classes) — returns the top matching file:line units` (179-187). The later block contradicts both: `[PURPOSE: cat]  expand a Phase-1 purpose category.` (916) and `[SEMANTIC: q]   fuzzy match over purpose categories.` (917). Same prompt, two incompatible argument shapes (path vs category / code vs categories).
findings.why_bug: Per project doctrine, a model-facing instruction that contradicts the live tool semantics silently degrades a weak model — here the merger is told PURPOSE takes a path AND a 'Phase-1 purpose category', and that SEMANTIC searches code AND 'purpose categories'. The `cat`/'purpose categories' wording is stale leftover from an older design. It also near-duplicates the TOOL TABLE (REFS/DEPENDENCY/CODE) as pure bloat in a prompt that elsewhere demands token discipline.
findings.fix: Delete the stale 910-920 mini-block (it duplicates the TOOL TABLE), or rewrite its PURPOSE/SEMANTIC lines to match the authoritative TOOL TABLE definitions (path-gist / code-embedding), removing the 'Phase-1 purpose category' phrasing.

findings.title: Empty-turn rounds are never written to the round-trace → the offline artifact silently drops the #1 failure mode and shows a confusing round-number gap
findings.where: core/native_tools.py:call_with_json_ops (empty-turn path ~3777-3785 vs round_trace at ~3843)
findings.evidence: The slice header says '=== CODER STEP 1 — 2 rounds ===' yet the body jumps from '========== ROUND 1 ==========' (line 3) straight to '========== ROUND 3 ==========' (line 102). Round 2 ran but is entirely absent. In the loop, the empty-turn branch does `messages.append(... _JSON_OPS_NUDGE ...); continue` at line 3782, which is BEFORE the `round_trace({... 'round': rnd ...})` call at line 3843. So any round where no JSON ops parse (the empty turn that the nudge exists for) emits NO trace event.
findings.why_bug: This very trace tooling exists to surface failure modes round-by-round (doctrine: 'Capture the WHOLE story'). Empty turns are a ~28% documented failure mode, and they are exactly the rounds the harness silently omits. The reader cannot tell round 2 was an empty/nudged turn vs lost data vs a counter bug, and the header count (2) undercounts the 3 rounds actually executed.
findings.fix: Emit a round_trace event for the empty-turn path too (e.g. round_trace with reasoning=content, io=[], event='empty-turn-nudge') before the `continue` at line 3782, so the artifact shows every round the loop consumed and labels stay contiguous.

findings.title: Environmentally-failed run_code (ModuleNotFoundError) does not credit the verify-gate, contradicting the run_code message's own 'environmental, move on' guidance → unsatisfiable verify loop for any module that imports a 3rd-party dep at top level
findings.where: core/native_tools.py:_do_run_code (exit-0-only `_run_code_verified` set, ~2685-2690) vs verify-gate in call_with_json_ops (~3860)
findings.evidence: The run_code result tells the model the failure is fine: '⚠ run_code: `import requests` failed (ModuleNotFoundError)... this is an ENVIRONMENT limitation of the smoke box, NOT a bug in your edit... Reason about `requests` statically and move on.' (lines 111-113). But `_run_code_verified` is set True ONLY on `code == 0` (line 2685-2690); a ModuleNotFoundError is non-zero, so it stays False, and the gate `if ctx.get('files_changed') and not _verify_nudged and not ctx.get('_run_code_verified')` (line 3860) treats the run as unverified and nudges for another verify pass. wikidata.py imports `requests` at module level (traceback line 117-119), so any run_code that imports the edited module ALWAYS fails this way — the gate's 'green run_code' path is unreachable for the whole instance.
findings.why_bug: The harness gives the coder two opposite signals about the same run: 'environmental, move on' from the run_code message, and 'not verified, do another pass' from the verify-gate. It burns a round nudging the model toward a re-verification that is structurally impossible to satisfy.
findings.fix: When run_code fails with an import/ModuleNotFoundError classified as environmental (the same condition that produces the 'NOT a bug' message), treat the verify-gate as satisfied for that attempt (set a `_run_code_env_attempted` flag the gate honors) instead of forcing another verify round into the same unavoidable failure.

findings.title: run_code message asserts the smoke box has 'the standard library only; third-party packages... NOT installed', contradicting the ckpt-166 venv-site-packages-bind the code comments rely on
findings.where: core/native_tools.py:_do_run_code result string (line shown at trace 111) vs comment at ~2700-2703
findings.evidence: The result message (trace line 111) states: 'The smoke-check sandbox runs a MINIMAL python — the standard library only; third-party packages and test plugins are NOT installed.' The adjacent code comment says the opposite: 'A ModuleNotFoundError/ImportError is, AFTER the venv site-packages bind (ckpt-166), almost always a genuinely-absent 3rd-party dep' — i.e. site-packages ARE supposed to be bound. For an openlibrary instance `requests` is a core project dependency that would be present in the project venv, yet it is absent here.
findings.why_bug: Either the user-facing message is stale (it predates the venv bind and now mis-describes the box as stdlib-only) or the venv bind is mis-scoped (it binds jarvis's own venv, which lacks the instance's deps, not the instance venv). Either way the message and the design comment disagree, and run_code cannot import any edited module that pulls a common 3rd-party dep — making run_code verification dead for such instances.
findings.fix: Reconcile the two: if site-packages are intentionally NOT the instance's, change the message to say so accurately ('only jarvis's own deps + stdlib are available; project deps are not'); if the bind is supposed to provide them, fix the bind to point at the instance venv so `requests`-class deps resolve.

findings.title: edits-array vs flat-op contradiction: body says 'one call, edits=list of hunks (else REJECTED)', JSON-OPS override forbids the edits array
findings.where: core/prompts_v8.py:IMPLEMENT_NATIVE_PROMPT_V8 (edit_file desc) vs the JSON-OPS override block appended in core/native_tools.py
findings.evidence: Body line 19: "Put ALL changes to a file in ONE call — `edits` = a list of small {old,new} hunks ... A multi-site refactor MUST be one call ... or it's REJECTED (a half-done refactor never lands)."  JSON-OPS override line 71 rule(2): "edit_file is FLAT: `old` and `new` are STRINGS, not arrays. ... Do NOT use an `edits` array and do NOT nest `[...]` — that is the one structure you keep mis-closing."  rule(4): "ONE edit_file op = ONE change. To change a file in SEVERAL places ... emit SEVERAL edit_file ops for that file in the SAME round."
findings.why_bug: The live JSON-OPS coder must emit several FLAT edit_file ops (one per line) and the harness merges them into an internal `edits` array (visible in coder_step_1.txt tool-call #1: it reconstructed `"edits":[...]`). But the verbatim body prose still imperatively commands the opposite — 'ALL changes in ONE call', '`edits` = a list of hunks', 'MUST be one call or REJECTED'. A weak model anchoring on the body (which is longer and reads first) will emit exactly the nested `edits` array the override calls 'the one structure you keep mis-closing'. This is model-facing prompt drift between the native-tool body and the format override, which the doctrine treats as P0.
findings.fix: When JARVIS_JSON_OPS is active, scrub or rewrite the native-tool body's edit_file paragraph so it does not mention an `edits` array / 'one call' at all — state the rule once, in flat-op terms ('one edit_file op = one change; multiple changes = multiple ops in the same round, harness merges them'). Don't leave the negated convention in the prompt for the override to walk back.

findings.title: Residual batch() imperatives in the body for a path that has no batch tool
findings.where: core/prompts_v8.py (tools list + HOW-TO-THINK/GATHER) vs JSON-OPS override line 72 in core/native_tools.py
findings.evidence: Line 11: "batch(calls) — run SEVERAL read-only lookups in ONE round". Line 25: "Do it in ONE round with batch(): name what each lookup answers ... → batch the ranges you need." Then line 72 negates it: "there is NO `batch` tool here — emitting several ops (one per line) in a round IS the batch, so never wrap them."
findings.why_bug: The body issues direct commands to 'use batch()' / 'batch the ranges' in two places, naming a tool the override says does not exist. The reconciliation lives 47 lines later. A weak model that follows the imperative may try to emit a batch(...) op (which has no handler) and waste a round, or be confused about whether to wrap ops. Same prompt-drift class as the edits-array issue, lower blast radius.
findings.fix: In the JSON-OPS prompt assembly, replace the literal 'batch()' calls in the GATHER/tools text with the flat-op phrasing ('emit several read ops, one per line, in one round'), so the only mention of 'batch' is the override that explains it doesn't exist.

findings.title: Prompt references a 'read on demand' file list that does not exist for this single-file step
findings.where: workflows/code.py (coder prompt assembly: 'read on demand' wording) — emitted in prompt lines 25 and 303
findings.evidence: Line 25: "Some of the step's files are already loaded above; others are listed 'read on demand'." Line 303 (after the lone FILE(S) block): "Read the 'read on demand' files only when you reach them." The FILE(S) block (lines 142–302) contains only openlibrary/core/wikidata.py, fully loaded; there is no 'read on demand' list anywhere in the prompt.
findings.why_bug: The prompt twice promises a 'read on demand' file list that is absent when the step touches a single fully-loaded file. A literal-minded weak model may hunt for / wait on a list that never appears, or assume files are being withheld. Stale boilerplate that contradicts the actual rendered artifact.
findings.fix: Make the 'read on demand' sentences conditional — only render them when at least one file is actually deferred. For a single fully-loaded-file step, drop both phrases.

findings.title: File line-count label off-by-one: header says '(159 lines)' but the file is 158 lines and the view renders only through line 158
findings.where: tools/codebase.py file-view renderer (line-count label = len(text.split('\n')) overcounting the trailing-newline element)
findings.evidence: Prompt line 143: "=== openlibrary/core/wikidata.py (159 lines) ==="; the view's last rendered line is line 158 (prompt line 301: "158 ⇥8|        return oldb.insert(...)"). On disk `wc -l` = 158 and the last content line is 158; the file ends with a trailing newline, so split('\n') yields a phantom 159th empty element that the label counts but the renderer (correctly) does not show.
findings.why_bug: The declared line count exceeds the highest line actually shown by one. Harmless for a mid-file edit like this step, but it is a systematic off-by-one (any trailing-newline file is mislabeled by +1), and it can make a coder believe a final line exists/was withheld, or mis-judge end-of-file edits and def-index gap ranges on large files.
findings.fix: Compute the line count from the same list the renderer enumerates (drop the empty trailing split element), or use the count of rendered lines, so the header equals the max line number shown.

findings.title: Round counter gap — step reported as '2 rounds' but rounds are labeled ROUND 1 then ROUND 3 (round 2 missing)
findings.where: core/native_tools.py JSON-OPS loop round counter / JARVIS_ROUND_TRACE dumper (round labeling)
findings.evidence: coder_step_1.txt line 1: "CODER STEP 1 — 2 rounds"; line 3: "========== ROUND 1 ==========" (edit); line 102: "========== ROUND 3 ==========" (run_code + done). No ROUND 2 is dumped, yet the edit-result feedback (line 75) labels the turn 'step 1, round 1'.
findings.why_bug: The internal round counter and the dumped/labeled round numbers disagree: the summary counts 2 substantive rounds, the labels jump 1→3, and round 2 is never shown. Either a round (likely an empty/nudge turn) was silently consumed between the edit and the run_code turn — a wasted round the 'empty-turn storm' fix is supposed to prevent — or the counter increments inconsistently. Both are harness-side and make round-by-round auditing (and any round-budget accounting shown to the model) unreliable.
findings.fix: Make the round counter monotonic and contiguous, and have the trace dump every round including empty/nudge/salvage turns (label them as such). If round 2 was an empty turn, surface it so the wasted round is visible rather than hidden by a numbering gap.

findings.title: run_code verify path is structurally impossible for this module, yet the prompt makes run_code the primary verify gate and only reveals the failure after a wasted round
findings.where: core/prompts_v8.py (system: 'verify (ideally with run_code...)') + tools/sandbox.py run_code (no pre-flight on heavy module imports)
findings.evidence: System line 5: "you are the last gate, so verify (ideally with run_code, which runs your live sandbox) before you finish." Line 20 urges "a targeted `python -c '…'` exercising the edited logic." But wikidata.py line 8 is `import requests`, so the model's smoke test (coder_step_1.txt round 3) dies: "ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'requests'" — any import of the edited module fails. The harness only tells the model it's environmental AFTER the run (lines 111–113).
findings.why_bug: The prompt pushes run_code as the way to satisfy the 'verify before done' gate, but for any file whose module-top imports a 3rd-party dep (the common case), the suggested `python -c 'from <module> import ...'` cannot run in the stdlib-only smoke box. The coder spends a round on a doomed check and gets no real verification of get_statement_values. The harness handles it gracefully but reactively.
findings.fix: Detect statically that the target module's top-level imports include known-absent 3rd-party deps and, before the model tries, suggest an isolated check that avoids importing the whole module (e.g. exec the edited method against a stub), or down-weight the run_code-verify instruction for such files so the model doesn't burn a round on an unrunnable import.

findings.title: Round-trace records prompt[:60000] (HEAD cap) → the dynamic tool-result tail is never captured, defeating the offline round-by-round audit
findings.where: core/tool_call.py :: call_with_tools (round_trace call, line 3325: "prompt": (current_prompt or "")[:60000])
findings.evidence: Every recorded planner prompt in the underlying trace is EXACTLY 60000 chars (verified: `python3` over /tmp/ansible2_trace_ckpt216.jsonl prints `promptlen 60000` for round 2/3/4 of owl-alpha, nemotron, gemma-4-31b-it), and a substring search for `=== Code: lib/ansible/galaxy/dependency_resolution/dataclasses.py` returns char -1 — the file-read result the planner consumed is NOT in the recorded prompt. The code line is literally `"prompt": (current_prompt or "")[:60000]`. Because the planner's static base prompt is ~50KB (lines 1-1145 of the rendered artifact), a HEAD cap at 60000 keeps the (identical-every-round) base and discards the past-thinking tail where the actual tool results live. This is exactly why the gemma dump (f12all) shows the round-1 dataclasses.py result truncated mid-file (line 35→32→27 across R2/R3/R4) and NEVER shows the R2 [SEARCH:] results or the R3 1038-line _collection_finder.py read at all.
findings.why_bug: The inline comment at tool_call.py:3319-3322 claims `prompt = exactly what THIS round's model saw ... prompt[N+1] holds round N's tool results — the pair across rounds reconstructs the whole planner conversation.` That promise is FALSE: a head cap keeps the static base (worthless to re-record every round) and throws away the dynamic tool-result tail — the one thing an auditor needs. The project doctrine (CLAUDE.md: 'Capture the whole story... dump the COMPLETE trace') depends on this recording, and it is silently lossy. An auditor cannot see what results the planner actually reasoned from.
findings.fix: Record the TAIL, not the head: `current_prompt[-60000:]` (the dynamic content is always at the end), or better record only the delta (past-thinking + newest tool results) since the static base is constant. Then split_trace's `_pr[-40000:]` would actually reach the fresh results. Also fix the misleading comment.

findings.title: Blast-radius annotation fires on the `TYPE_CHECKING = False` compat-shim assignment: `|appears 20 (#575, shared name)` — noise on a non-def line that trips the N>=20 mandatory-drill-in threshold
findings.where: core/dependency_index.py :: annotate_view (line 488; default threshold=1 at line 512 annotates every indexed symbol incl. module-level assignments; shared-name count from idx.by_name at 541-542)
findings.evidence: In the round-1 dataclasses.py read the planner saw: `19:4|TYPE_CHECKING = False |appears 20 (#575, shared name)`. That line is the `try: from typing import TYPE_CHECKING / except ImportError: TYPE_CHECKING = False` Python-2/3 typing guard — a boilerplate compat fallback, not a meaningful API symbol. The `appears 20` count comes from `len(idx.by_name.get('TYPE_CHECKING'))` (the same idiom repeated in ~20 modules), and `threshold=1` (line 512, set deliberately so 'every def emits its tag') causes module-level assignments to be annotated too.
findings.why_bug: (a) Misleading: it presents a 20-callsite shared-name blast radius for a name no edit in this task touches, polluting the view the planner AND the downstream coder read. (b) It contradicts the rendered prompt doc, which states the annotation appears on `def` lines: 'The `|appears N (#tag, ...)` annotation on a `def` line means the symbol is shared' (rendered-artifact line 228-229; example at line 211 is `def call_with_retry(`). Here it sits on a plain assignment. (c) `appears 20` lands exactly on the BLAST RADIUS table's `N >= 20 -> call [DEPENDENCY: #tag] first to drill in` rule (rendered line 132) — a documented mandatory action — so the harness is steering the model to waste a DEPENDENCY round on a typing shim. The planner happened to ignore it, but the trap is real.
findings.fix: In annotate_view, skip annotating module-level simple-assignment symbols (and known compat shims like TYPE_CHECKING), or only annotate def/class def_lines to match the documented contract; alternatively suppress the `shared name` / tag when the symbol is a same-name compat idiom replicated across modules rather than a real polymorphic def.

findings.title: split_trace shows _pr[-40000:] of an already-head-capped prompt; its comment claims 40000 'reliably reaches the fresh tool-result tail' which is false
findings.where: behavioral_audit/split_trace.py :: main (line 85: fh.write((_pr[-40000:] ...)); comment at lines 80-82)
findings.evidence: split_trace.py:85 dumps `_pr[-40000:]` and the comment (lines 80-82) asserts '15000 was often entirely consumed by the static base ... 40000 reliably reaches the fresh tool-result tail + manifest.' But `_pr` is the upstream-recorded `prompt[:60000]` head cap, so `_pr[-40000:]` = chars 20000-60000 = still inside the static base + only the head of past-thinking. In the gemma audit file this manifests as the round-1 file result cut at line 35/32/27 and rounds 2-3 results entirely absent.
findings.why_bug: The audit tool's stated guarantee is unmet because of the upstream head-cap (finding #1); taking the tail of a head-capped string cannot recover content that was already dropped. The auditor is shown a confusing slice that looks like a truncated/stale file read but is actually missing data, inviting wrong conclusions (e.g. 'the harness truncated the file the model read').
findings.fix: Primarily fix finding #1 (record the tail upstream). Secondarily, in split_trace detect when `len(_pr)` hit the 60000 cap and emit a loud banner ('prompt recording was HEAD-capped at 60000 chars; dynamic tool-result tail NOT captured') instead of implying the dynamic tail is shown.

findings.title: Edit-reject recovery hint sizes its "ACTUAL current lines" window to 1 line when `old` is a multi-line STRING — shows only the matching TOP of a block, so the coder misdiagnoses a last-line mismatch as an off-by-one and burns its budget
findings.where: core/native_tools.py:_actual_region_hint (n calc ~line 1493) fed by _old_not_found_msg string-coercion (lines 1535-1538)
findings.evidence: json-coder step2 R1 emitted hunk #2 `old` = the 13-line block 42-54 as ONE string, but truncated the final line to `...tested_str)` (one `)` short of the real `...tested_str))`). The reject (coder_step_2.txt L99-104) showed only `40 ⇥0|`,`41 ⇥0|`,`42 ⇥0|try:`,`43`,`44` — lines 40-44. _old_not_found_msg does `if isinstance(old_raw, str): old_raw=[old_raw]`, so the 13-line string becomes a 1-element list; _actual_region_hint then computes `n = len([o for o in old_list if str(o).strip()])` = 1, and `hi = anchor + n + 2` = 42+1+2 → window 40-44. The lines it showed (42,43,44) all MATCH the model's `old`, so R2 reasoned `"The first edit removed line 14, shifting subsequent lines... line numbers off by 1"` (coder_step_2.txt L293) — a false theory — and spent R2,R3 re-reading instead of fixing the dropped `)` on line 54, which the window never revealed.
findings.why_bug: For a multi-line `old` the recovery window must span the whole attempted block (so the diverging line is visible). Counting a `\n`-joined string as 1 line shrinks the window to the block's head, which by construction matches — actively steering the coder to the wrong (off-by-one) conclusion while the reject also tells it "do NOT re-read". This is the proximate cause the JSON-OPS primary produced 0 edits on a trivial step and fell through to native gpt-oss (7 rounds) AND native medium (3 rounds) — 3 coder passes for one import swap.
findings.fix: In _actual_region_hint count newline-separated non-blank lines across elements: `n = sum(1 for o in old_list for ln in str(o).split('\n') if ln.strip()) or 1`. Then the window (anchor-2 .. anchor+n+2) covers all 13 lines + context and surfaces line 54 where the truncation actually is.

findings.title: FILE(S) preload header overcounts lines by 1 (`count('\n')+1`) for newline-terminated files — header says "452 lines" while the view ends at 451 and every range view says "of 451"
findings.where: workflows/code.py:build_implement_native_prompt line 11091 (and the overflow note, line 11098)
findings.evidence:
prompt_round0.txt L97: `=== lib/ansible/galaxy/dependency_resolution/dataclasses.py (452 lines) ===`, but the full view ends at `451 ⇥4|    """A concrete collection candidate...` and every on-demand view says `lines 40-70 of 451`. The file is 451 lines (wc -l = 451, ends in `
`). Header uses `c.count(chr(10)) + 1` → 452; the inject-decision two lines above (11073) and _file_line_count (native_tools.py:547) correctly use `c.count('
') + (0 if c.endswith('
') else 1)` → 451.
findings.why_bug: The same function reports two different counts for the same file; the coder is told there is a line 452 it never received, inviting a wasted read to "find" it. Inconsistent line numbering between the preload header and every later range view erodes trust in the harness's numbers.
findings.fix: Use the endswith-aware count in the header: `({c.count(chr(10)) + (0 if c.endswith(chr(10)) else 1)} lines)` at lines 11091 and 11098 (reuse the `_nlines` already computed at 11073).

findings.title: Assembled JSON-OPS coder prompt describes edit_file with TWO conflicting shapes — the native body teaches an `edits` LIST of {old,new} hunks; the appended override says edit_file is FLAT string old/new and "Do NOT use an edits array". The model followed the upper form and truncated a hunk
findings.where: core/native_tools.py:_JSON_OPS_PROMPT (lines 3652-3661) vs IMPLEMENT_NATIVE_PROMPT edit_file description + INDENTATION section (mirrored in prompt_round0.txt lines 19, 51-56)
findings.evidence: prompt_round0.txt L19: "`edits` = a list of small {old,new} hunks"; the JSON-OPS override (native_tools.py:3652) says "edit_file is FLAT: `old` and `new` are STRINGS, not arrays... Do NOT use an `edits` array and do NOT nest `[...]`". In the live json-coder step2 R1 the model emitted the UPPER form: `args: {"path":..., "edits": [{"old":"14 ⇥0|...","new":""},{"old":"42 ⇥0|try...\n43..."}...]}` (coder_step_2.txt L97), and one hunk in that array was truncated → reject.
findings.why_bug: A weak coder anchors on the longer, more detailed description (the upper edit_file/INDENTATION sections) over a one-line late override. The two halves of its actual prompt give incompatible edit_file schemas; relying on a trailing 'this OVERRIDES …' line to undo the dominant teaching is fragile and demonstrably failed here.
