findings.fix: For the JSON-OPS path, render the upper edit_file description and INDENTATION examples in the FLAT single-op string form too (or strip the `edits`-array language), so both halves of the prompt agree; don't depend on a late override to retract the primary description.

findings.title: find_callers `#tag` annotations are present in range-read views but absent from FULL-preloaded files — a coder holding a fully-loaded ≤1000-line file has no tags to pass to find_callers
findings.where: workflows/code.py:build_implement_native_prompt (uses add_line_numbers(display_mode='prefix_ws'), line 11074/11091) vs the native read-view renderer that appends `|appears N (#tag)`
findings.evidence: In prompt_round0.txt the fully-loaded file shows e.g. `52 ⇥4|    def _is_py_id(tested_str):` with NO annotation; the on-demand range read of the SAME unchanged file shows `52 ⇥4|    def _is_py_id(tested_str): |appears 1 (#948, central)` (coder_step_2.txt L317) and `128 ⇥0|def _is_fqcn(tested_str): |appears 1 (#8c19, central)` (L359). find_refs/find_callers in the prompt say to pass the `#tag`, but the full preload exposes none.
findings.why_bug: Same file, two renderers, two annotation sets. A coder told to use find_callers(tag) on a high-fanout symbol cannot, because the full-preload view never shows a tag — it must first do a discouraged re-read to obtain one.
findings.fix: Run the same fanout/`#tag` annotation pass over the full-preloaded `file_block` that the native range-view uses, so tags are consistent whether a file is preloaded or read on demand.

findings.title: Unconditional "Read the 'read on demand' files only when you reach them" trailer (and the GATHER paragraph) reference files that don't exist for a single fully-loaded-file step
findings.where: workflows/code.py:build_implement_native_prompt nat_user trailer (line 11125) and IMPLEMENT_NATIVE_PROMPT GATHER text (prompt_round0.txt line 25)
findings.evidence: This step preloads its only file in full (overflow empty — no 'TOO LARGE TO PRELOAD' block in prompt_round0.txt), yet L550 still says "Read the 'read on demand' files only when you reach them." and L25 says "others are listed 'read on demand'". The `_overflow_clause` right before it IS correctly gated on `overflow`, but this sentence is not.
findings.why_bug: References a category of files that doesn't exist for single-file steps; mildly invites the coder to look for nonexistent deferred files and contradicts the 'everything is already loaded' framing.
findings.fix: Gate the 'read on demand' trailer on `overflow` (emit only when overflow is non-empty), parallel to the already-conditional `_overflow_clause`.

findings.title: split_trace.py overwrites prompt_round0.txt for every coder-prompt record, so the rendered 'JSON-OPS coder prompt' is actually the LAST fallback's (native, model=medium) prompt and lacks the JSON-OPS protocol block; step-1's prompt is also lost
findings.where: behavioral_audit/split_trace.py lines 39-45 (open(p,'w') per record)
findings.evidence: The trace has 5 coder prompt records (json/native gpt-oss step1, json/native gpt-oss step2, native medium step2 — confirmed via per-(phase,model,step) counts). split_trace writes all of them to one `prompt_round0.txt` with `open(p, 'w')`, so only the last survives. prompt_round0.txt is headed `(phase coder, step 2, model medium)` and contains NO `## ⚠ OUTPUT FORMAT — JSON OPS` block, while coder_step_2.txt is `model gpt-oss-120b`. The artifact handed to this audit does not match the JSON-OPS coder it is meant to study.
findings.why_bug: The auditor (and any consumer of this dump) is shown the wrong coder's prompt and cannot see the actual JSON-OPS system prompt the studied run received; multiple steps' prompts are silently collapsed to one.
findings.fix: Write per (phase,step,model) prompt files, e.g. `prompt_step{N}_{phase}_{model}.txt`, instead of a single overwritten prompt_round0.txt.

findings.title: Round-2 prompt embeds the planner's own round-1 file read TRUNCATED to 43 of 158 lines (cut mid-line), while [YOUR TOOL INDEX] still claims 158
findings.where: planner round-prompt assembly — the builder that embeds prior tool results into [YOUR PAST THINKING]/[YOUR TOOL RESULTS] (best guess workflows/plan.py or core/prompts_v8.py round-prompt assembly)
findings.evidence: The round-2 prompt ENDS mid-line at line 2056: `43:4|def get_wikipedia_link(self, language: str = 'en') -> tuple[str, ` then `--- model response ---`. The trace dumps `prompt[-40000:]`, which ends at the prompt's TRUE end — so the embedded round-1 [CODE: wikidata.py] result is cut at line 43. Yet line 1972 [YOUR TOOL INDEX] asserts `[CODE: openlibrary/core/wikidata.py] (R1 — 158 lines)` and line 2012 header says `(158 lines)`. wikidata.py is 158 lines — well under the 1500-line full-return threshold, so it should be shown in FULL.
findings.why_bug: In the very round it commits the plan, the planner re-reads its own prior file read and gets a silently chopped file (cut mid-token at `tuple[str,`). It never saw lines 44-158: any existing USAGE of `statements`, the rest of the class, the exact insertion point, or any type/helper clarifying whether `value.content` is dict-access vs attribute-access. It guessed dict access (`statement.get('value', {}).get('content')`). Here the guess happened to be plausible, but the harness chopped a small file the planner explicitly loaded — a context-integrity bug that bites silently. The [YOUR TOOL INDEX] '158 lines' vs the 43-line body is internally contradictory.
findings.fix: Do not truncate embedded prior tool results for files under the full-return threshold; render the past-read at the same length the tool originally returned. If a global round cap forces trimming, trim oldest/largest results FIRST and never mid-line, and make [YOUR TOOL INDEX] line-count agree with the embedded body (or annotate 'TRUNCATED — re-[CODE:] to see the rest'). Confirm by rendering the real round-2 planner prompt offline.

findings.title: Prompt references a `[CONTEXT MANIFEST]` section and a `⛔` re-read marker that the live prompt never renders (the actual section is `[YOUR TOOL INDEX]`, no ⛔)
findings.where: core/prompts_v8.py — planner system prompt text vs the live loaded-files renderer
findings.evidence: Line 92: 'Re-reading a file already in [CONTEXT MANIFEST] (marked ⛔).' Line 328-330 lists `── CONTEXT MANIFEST ──── files already loaded into context (⛔ no re-reads on these)` AND `── YOUR TOOL INDEX ──` as two separate sections. Lines 569-572: '## No re-reading — Files in [CONTEXT MANIFEST] are already loaded. [CODE:] on them is rejected (⛔).' But the live round-2 prompt renders only `[YOUR TOOL INDEX]` (line 1968) with 'HARD RULES' prose and NO ⛔ symbol and NO [CONTEXT MANIFEST] section anywhere.
findings.why_bug: Stale prompt / format drift: the model is told (3×) to recognize ⛔-marked files in a [CONTEXT MANIFEST] section to avoid re-reads, but neither that section name nor the ⛔ marker exists in the assembled artifact. A weak planner looking for the promised marker finds nothing, weakening the re-read gate the instructions lean on.
findings.fix: Pick one name. Either rename the live section to [CONTEXT MANIFEST] and add the ⛔ markers, or update all prompt prose (lines 92, 328-330, 569-572) to reference [YOUR TOOL INDEX] and drop the ⛔ language. Remove the duplicate two-section listing at 328-330.

findings.title: PROJECT CONTEXT's partial 200-file alphabetical dump is re-sent verbatim every round, padding the prompt and helping push real content past the tail
findings.where: per-round prompt assembly that prepends [PROJECT CONTEXT] (swe_bench/planner context injection)
findings.evidence: Lines 1747-1948 (round 2) duplicate lines 977-1177 (round 1) char-for-char — the same 'first 200, alphabetical — PARTIAL' file list ending '… and 686 MORE files NOT listed'. The full ~600-line planner system prompt is also re-sent. The only artifact the planner actually needs (wikidata.py) lands at the absolute tail (line 2010+) and is the thing that gets truncated.
findings.why_bug: The 200-file alphabetical slice is mostly irrelevant macros/*.html and doesn't even contain most files a planner would want ('686 MORE NOT listed'); re-injecting it every round is pure token cost that competes with the real file content for the tail of a length-bounded prompt. Combined with finding 1, the noise is what the model keeps while the signal (the file body) is what gets chopped.
findings.fix: Drop the alphabetical partial file dump after round 1 (the tools [LS:]/[SEARCH:] cover discovery), or move volatile per-round sections (tool results) to the END and trim the static boilerplate first under any cap.

findings.title: Injected REQUIREMENTS (#4) contradicts the code's own `statements: dict[str, dict]` annotation; both are presented as ground truth, so the planner marks it MET with a hedge and writes no reconciling STEP
findings.where: swe_bench task-injection (REQUIREMENTS block) vs faithfully-fed source — no harness reconciliation
findings.evidence: REQUIREMENTS line 909/1680: 'The `statements` field … should represent a mapping from property IDs to a LIST of structured statement objects.' Source line 2047 (=file line 35): `35:4|statements: dict[str, dict]`. The model flagged the conflict in [think] (line 2074: 'the type hint … is dict[str, dict]. However, the requirements say it's a mapping to a list') then in the plan (line 2096) marked it: '— MET (defined as dict[str, dict] …, though requirements specify the values are lists of objects).'
findings.why_bug: The harness hands the planner two mutually-inconsistent 'facts' as equally authoritative and offers no signal which one the hidden test pins. The planner resolves it by marking MET with a parenthetical caveat and emits NO step to update the annotation/structure — if the gold patch changes the annotation, the plan under-covers requirement #4. This is contradictory input, not model incapacity.
findings.fix: When a REQUIREMENT describes a data-shape that conflicts with a fed annotation, the requirement (test contract) should win; consider a planner note that requirement-vs-annotation conflicts default to the requirement, prompting a reconciling STEP rather than a silent MET.

findings.title: Edit-reject "ACTUAL current lines" window is sized by COUNT of old-list elements, not text lines — a newline-joined multi-line `old` echoes only a 5-line window for a 13-line failed hunk
findings.where: core/native_tools.py:_actual_region_hint (n at ~line 1493; window `hi = min(len(cur_lines), anchor + n + 2)` at ~line 1514)
findings.evidence:
json-coder R1 (jsonl idx42) sent hunk #2 as a single newline-joined string spanning lines 42-54. The reject result echoed ONLY 5 lines: "40 ⇥0|
41 ⇥0|
42 ⇥0|try:  # NOTE: py3/py2 compat
43 ⇥4|    # FIXME: put somewhere into compat
44 ⇥4|    # py2 mypy can't deal with try/excepts" — it stops at 44, omitting 45-54. Cause: `n = len([o for o in old_list if str(o).strip()]) or 1`; a joined-string `old` is ONE list element → n=1 → `hi = anchor+1+2` → a ±2 window. The SAME message says "your view is STILL CURRENT — do NOT re-read", so the model cannot recopy the full 13-line hunk verbatim from the reject and cannot re-read either.
findings.why_bug: The hint is meant to let the coder recopy the failing `old` verbatim so it can resend without re-reading. Counting list elements instead of physical lines makes the hint cover ~3 lines of a 13-line hunk whenever `old` arrives as a joined string (a supported JSON-OPS form), defeating the hint and forcing the very re-read the message forbids (idx43 immediately re-reads 40-70).
findings.fix: Compute n as total physical line count across `old` elements: `n = sum(max(1, str(o).count(chr(10))+1) for o in old_list if str(o).strip()) or 1`, so the echoed window always spans the entire rejected hunk regardless of joined-string vs array form.

findings.title: gpt-oss burned two full coder sessions (~10 rounds) on step 2 with ZERO edits before mistral-medium fallback succeeded in one round — read/view state is discarded across the fallback and a read-only no-progress session runs to the round cap
findings.where: workflows/code.py coder fallback orchestration (_CODER_CHAIN) + core/native_tools.py round-budget/no-progress backstop
findings.evidence: Step 2 ran THREE sessions: (1) json-coder gpt-oss-120b — rounds 1-3, ops [edit_file(FAIL), read, read], 0 edits, abandoned; (2) coder gpt-oss-120b native — rounds 1-7, ops [search, read, read, read, EMPTY, EMPTY, read], 0 edits, hit budget; (3) coder mistral-medium — round 1 edit_file → "✓ Applied 5 edit(s)", done. The native session (2) re-read lines 120-150 and 124-140 that session (1) had ALREADY read — every session restarts the look-phase from zero.
findings.why_bug: The whole 5-hunk edit was trivially applicable (mistral did it in 1 round from the clean initial view), yet the primary coder wasted ~10 rounds because each fallback throws away the file views already gathered and there is no detector for a session that only reads/searches without ever landing an edit. This is a large per-step time/round sink across the chain.
findings.fix: Carry the accumulated view/edit ctx (viewed ranges, files_changed) into the next coder in the chain so it doesn't re-read; add a no-progress backstop that fails a session over early once it has spent N rounds with reads-only and zero successful edits (instead of running to the full round cap).

findings.title: Inline-salvage drops a recoverable tool call on two consecutive rounds, turning them into empty-turns even though `_salvage_inline_tool_call` recovers a valid op from each
findings.where: core/native_tools.py call loop, salvage gate `if not tcs and _salvage_count < 4:` (~line 3343); _salvage_inline_tool_call (~3124)
findings.why_bug: This gpt-oss session leaks EVERY op as harmony text; the `_salvage_count < 4` cap (intended to stop salvage loops) is exhausted by the earlier productive salvages, so rounds 5+ become wasted empty-turns despite salvage succeeding — directly contributing to the zero-edit native session above.
findings.fix: Don't count PRODUCTIVE salvages toward the cap; cap only consecutive salvages that fail to advance state (no files_changed / repeated identical op), or raise the cap substantially for providers that habitually emit calls as text.

findings.title: Atomic N-hunk reject names only the FIRST failing hunk and never states all-or-nothing, so the coder invents a phantom 'earlier hunk applied → line numbers shifted' theory and wastes rounds
findings.where: core/native_tools.py:_old_not_found_msg / edit-reject text ("The file is UNCHANGED ...", ~line 1524+)
findings.evidence: json-coder R1 reject named only "✗ edit_file hunk #2". In R2 (idx43) the model reasoned: "we attempted to delete line 14 first, that succeeded? It didn't error... The first edit removed line 14, shifting subsequent lines up by 1. The edit's old line numbers must match current view." It then re-read to chase a non-existent shift. The 5-hunk call was atomically rejected (nothing applied), but the message only said the file is "UNCHANGED" without explicitly stating hunks #1/#3/#4/#5 also did NOT apply and that line numbers are unchanged.
findings.why_bug: A weak coder reads 'hunk #2 failed' as 'hunks before it succeeded' and constructs a phantom line-shift, then burns rounds re-reading to 'realign' numbers that never moved — exactly the R2/R3 waste here.
findings.fix: Make the reject explicit: 'ALL k hunks were rejected together (all-or-nothing) — NONE applied, line numbers are UNCHANGED from your view.' Optionally validate every hunk and report all mismatches at once so the coder fixes them in one resend.

findings.title: read_file RANGE views append a ` |appears N (#tag, central)` symbol annotation onto copy-verbatim lines, confusing the coder about where the real line ends (the initial full view has no such tail)
findings.where: tools/codebase.py range-view renderer (def-line blast-radius annotation); strip side core/native_tools.py:_APPEARS_TAIL_RE (~line 1605)
findings.evidence: Range view (idx43/idx44) rendered "52 ⇥4|    def _is_py_id(tested_str): |appears 1 (#948, central)". In R3 (idx44) the model reasoned: "Note line 52 has trailing space after colon? It shows `def _is_py_id(tested_str): ` with a space before newline. We'll include exactly as view." The model misread the space before `|appears` as a trailing space in the code line. The round-0 full view of the same line had NO annotation ("52 ⇥4|    def _is_py_id(tested_str):").
findings.why_bug: The coder is told to copy view lines verbatim into `old`; an undelimited trailing annotation makes it unsure where code ends and nearly copies a phantom trailing space. It is defused only because _APPEARS_TAIL_RE strips the tail at match time, but it still costs reasoning and risks malformed `old`; the inconsistency (full view omits it, range view adds it) compounds the confusion.
findings.fix: Either omit the ` |appears` annotation from lines in the copy-source RANGE view (keep it only in a separate locator/find_refs surface), or mark it with an unmistakable non-code delimiter and tell the coder it is stripped — and keep full-view and range-view annotation behavior consistent.

findings.title: run_code env-limit message blanket-forecloses isolated verification of a pure-stdlib helper, leaving the whole qutebrowser family edit-verify-blind
findings.where: core/native_tools.py:_do_run ModuleNotFoundError branch (lines 2717-2726)
findings.evidence: Coder command: `from qutebrowser.utils import urlutils; print(list(urlutils.widened_hostnames('a.b.c')))`. Result: "⚠ run_code: `import PyQt5` failed (ModuleNotFoundError)... Reason about `PyQt5` statically and move on." The traceback (line 107-109) shows the failure is the MODULE-LEVEL `from PyQt5.QtCore import QUrl` at urlutils.py:31, not anything the new function needs.
findings.why_bug: The function under test (widened_hostnames) is pure-stdlib (only str.find / slicing) and IS smoke-testable in isolation — but because it lives in a module that imports PyQt5 at top level, importing the module is structurally impossible in the stdlib-only smoke box. Since EVERY qutebrowser source file imports PyQt5, run_code can never runtime-verify any edit in this entire repo family, and the message's blanket 'reason statically and move on' steers the coder away from the one path that WOULD work (defining/pasting the helper body inline, no module import). run_code is the project's headline verify lever; here it is a guaranteed wasted round with no fallback offered.
findings.fix: When the missing module is NOT named in the coder's command (i.e. a transitive import of the module being imported), add a line to the message: 'your function itself does not need `PyQt5` — to smoke-test it, paste its body/def directly into the command instead of importing the whole module.' This converts a doomed import into a runnable isolated check for pure-stdlib helpers.

findings.title: run_code error mis-attributes the failing import: says `import PyQt5 failed` when the coder never imported PyQt5
findings.where: core/native_tools.py:2717 (f"⚠ run_code: `import {_mod}` failed")
findings.evidence: Coder command imported `qutebrowser.utils.urlutils`; the message reports "`import PyQt5` failed (ModuleNotFoundError)" because `_mod = _imp.group(1).split('.')[0]` extracts PyQt5 from the transitive failure. The coder's text contains no `import PyQt5`.
findings.why_bug: Phrasing it as `import PyQt5 failed` implies the coder wrote that import, which a weak model may try to reconcile by hunting for / editing a PyQt5 import line (the exact rabbit-hole the message elsewhere forbids). The naming is honest about the missing module but wrong about WHO imported it; the only thing that disambiguates is the raw traceback below.
findings.fix: Phrase as 'a (possibly transitive) import of `PyQt5` failed while importing your target — the missing dep is `PyQt5`' rather than '`import PyQt5` failed', so the coder doesn't look for a literal `import PyQt5` it never wrote.

findings.title: Trailing `\n` in edit `new` is expanded into a spurious extra blank line, leaving two blank lines at EOF
findings.where: core/native_tools.py:_expand_indent_lines (1634-1658) via _do_edit batch apply
findings.evidence: Model new ends with `...8|hostname = hostname[dot_idx + 1:]\n` (line 65). The cumulative diff (lines 95-97) shows `638:+ hostname = hostname[dot_idx + 1:]` then `639:+ ` and `640:+ ` — two added blank lines at end of file (639 from the trailing \n's empty split element, 640 the original EOF blank shifted down).
findings.why_bug: Splitting `new` on \n yields a trailing '' element which _expand_indent_lines emits verbatim as a blank line (no match → out.append('')). A single trailing newline in `new` is a near-universal model artifact, not an intended blank; the harness turns it into real source, producing two consecutive EOF blank lines which a flake8/W391 lint gate in some repos would flag. Borderline model-authored (the model did type the \n), but the applier could safely trim exactly one trailing empty element.
findings.fix: In _do_edit / _expand_indent_lines, drop a single trailing empty-string element of `new` before applying (only the final one, never interior blanks), so a stray trailing newline doesn't inject an EOF blank line.

findings.title: Stale code comment: ModuleNotFoundError branch justifies itself by 'AFTER the venv site-packages bind (ckpt-166)' which ckpt-169 reverted
findings.where: core/native_tools.py:2700-2702 comment
findings.evidence: Comment reads: "A ModuleNotFoundError/ImportError is, AFTER the venv site-packages bind (ckpt-166)... almost always a genuinely-absent 3rd-party dep". But core/safe_exec.py:199-208 states "REVERTED Fix A (ckpt-169): we bind only the venv BIN dir... NOT the venv site-packages. Binding site-packages (ckpt-166)... BACKFIRED".
findings.why_bug: Not model-facing, but it is exactly the kind of stale-instruction drift the project doctrine flags as P0: the comment asserts the opposite of the live sandbox behavior (stdlib-only, no site-packages). It nearly caused this audit to mis-report the model-facing 'standard library only' message as stale — a future debugger reading only this comment would draw the wrong conclusion about what run_code can import.
findings.fix: Update the comment to reference the ckpt-169 stdlib-only revert (bin-dir bind only, no site-packages) so it matches safe_exec.py and the _do_run docstring.

findings.title: _looks_like_plan admits a pure-reasoning draft via its numbered-list branch — degenerate draft #3 fed to merger as a peer plan and chosen as baseline
findings.where: workflows/code.py: phase_plan / _looks_like_plan (lines 8782-8786, gate at 8821)
findings.evidence: INPUT PLAN #3 (gemma-4-31b-it) in the trace is raw [think] reasoning with NO '=== PLAN ===' and NO '### STEP', truncated mid-sentence at line 1130 ('  - `a.b.c`'). Yet it is presented as a full INPUT PLAN #3 (line 1054) and the merger anchors on it: 'Plan #3 ... the most complete plan' (1145) and 'Baseline: Plan #3' (1914, 1959). _looks_like_plan returns True because gemma's body contains numbered enumerations like '1. Implement `widened_hostnames`' (1060) and '1. Investigate ...' (1070) that match the regex branch `^\s*\d+\.\s`. Since drafts #1 and #2 ARE valid plans, the degenerate fallback at 8842 ('if not plans and _degenerate') does NOT apply — so a correctly-flagged degenerate draft would have been held back; its appearance proves it passed the gate.
findings.why_bug: The plan-structure gate is supposed to hold back pure-reasoning drafts (8830-8832), but the `^\s*\d+\.\s` alternative matches any ordinary numbered list inside reasoning prose. This lets a no-plan, truncated CoT dump masquerade as a real plan, and the merger spends its baseline decision on it instead of on the one genuinely complete draft (#2).
findings.fix: Drop the bare `^\s*\d+\.\s` numbered-list branch from _looks_like_plan, or require it to co-occur with a real plan header ('=== PLAN', '## TASK SHAPE', '## STEPS', or '### STEP'). A list of numbered sentences is not plan structure.

findings.title: Unclosed [think] in a draft defeats _cap_plan's _strip_think — the entire raw reasoning channel (plus <channel|> tokens) leaks into the merger prompt
findings.where: workflows/code.py: _cap_plan (8868-8876) calling core/tool_call.py:_strip_think / _THINK_BLOCK (164-166)
findings.evidence: INPUT PLAN #3 as RENDERED in the merger prompt still contains live '[think]' (1056), '<channel|>[tool use]' (1076, 1087) and three '[think]' opens with ZERO closing '[/think]'/'</think>'/'</longcat_think>' (the draft is truncated before any close). _THINK_BLOCK = `\[think\].*?` + _THINK_CLOSE requires a close token; with none present the regex matches nothing, so _cap_plan strips nothing and the full CoT rides into the merger. The recently-added lenient '</longcat_think>' close (tool_call.py:164) cannot help when there is NO close at all.
findings.why_bug: A draft that times out / truncates mid-think emits an unbalanced '[think]' with no closer. _strip_think only removes BALANCED think blocks, so the unbalanced case leaks 70+ lines of private reasoning and harmony channel garbage as if it were plan content — exactly the 'private CoT mixed in as plan' failure the ckpt-222 strip was meant to prevent.
findings.fix: In _cap_plan, after _strip_think, detect a dangling '[think]'/'<think>' with no matching close and either truncate the draft at that point, or route it through _salvage_plan_from_think to extract only the plan body. Treat an unbalanced think marker as 'reasoning from here on' and cut it.

findings.title: Layer-1 [ERROR: ...] failure traces and empty [tool use] blocks not stripped from draft bodies; merger derives false confirmation from an errored stub
findings.where: workflows/code.py: _cap_plan (8868-8876) — strips <think> and [SYSTEM NOTE:] but not [ERROR:]/empty tool blocks
findings.evidence: INPUT PLAN #1 (nemotron-super) body still contains '[ERROR: Agent failed (Function process_single_item_agent timed out after 90.0 seconds), API failed (API request returned None after all retries)]' (907) and '[ERROR: Max turns (10) exceeded]' (914), plus a trailing empty '[tool use]\n\n[/tool use]' (975-977). _cap_plan only strips `<think>` and `\[SYSTEM NOTE:...\]`. The merger then treats this errored draft as corroboration: PART C 'From Plan #1, I confirm the two-file scope is correct' (1963).
findings.why_bug: These are internal runtime/diagnostic artifacts, not plan substance. Feeding them as plan content adds noise and, worse, lets the anti-consensus machinery count a draft that never actually investigated as an independent confirmation of scope.
findings.fix: Extend _cap_plan's sanitizer to strip `\[ERROR:.*?\]` lines and empty `[tool use] ... [/tool use]` blocks (same pass as [SYSTEM NOTE:]). Optionally down-weight a draft whose body is dominated by error traces before the anti-consensus reflexes treat it as a vote.

findings.title: <channel|> harmony channel-delimiter tokens have no strip rule — leak into merge inputs
findings.where: core/tool_call.py: _strip_think / merge-input sanitizer (no rule for `<channel|>` / `<|channel|>`-style tokens)
findings.evidence: INPUT PLAN #3 contains '<channel|>[tool use]' at lines 1076 and 1087, and the merger's own R1 response shows '</longcat_think>' at 1166. _THINK_CLOSE (tool_call.py:164) covers '</think>'/'</longcat_think>' as think CLOSERS, but the bare harmony channel marker '<channel|>' is a distinct token class with no removal rule, so it survives into the prompt even after think-stripping.
findings.why_bug: Half-rendered model-internal channel delimiters are meaningless to the merger and degrade a weak model's parse of the draft. They are a known family (harmony commentary/analysis channels) the sanitizer doesn't recognize.
findings.fix: Add the harmony channel tokens (`<channel|>`, `<|channel|>`, `<|start|>`/`<|message|>` and similar) to the merge-input strip set alongside the think markers.

findings.title: Stated 'No code in the plan' rule is unenforced — merger ships fenced code bodies verbatim to the coder
findings.where: workflows/code.py: merger output path (current_plan = _strip_think(_body), ~line 3593) — no code-fence strip
findings.evidence: The merger prompt mandates 'No code bodies.' (line 431) and a whole section '## No code in the plan ... Strip code from any input plan when integrating' (443-446). The shipped FINAL plan nonetheless contains a full Python `widened_hostnames` implementation in a fenced block (1999-2016) and a quoted `_is_blocked` body (1894-1906). The merger output is only think-stripped, not code-fence-stripped, so this ships to the coder as written ('it ships exactly as you write it').
findings.why_bug: A model-facing rule that the harness states but never enforces is a contradiction; per repo doctrine that silently degrades weak consumers. Worse, the embedded impl bakes in the merger's invented trailing-dot algorithm (STEP 2 point 4), which the coder may copy verbatim instead of reasoning from the real file.
findings.fix: Either strip fenced ``` code blocks from the final plan before it ships (matching the stated rule), or relax the prompt rule to permit short illustrative snippets — but make the stated contract and the actual output path agree.

findings.title: finish does not terminate the coder loop — accepted finish is followed by another model round (wasted round + API call)
findings.where: core/native_tools.py JSON-OPS run loop — the loop-termination check after a 'finish'/'done' op (off-by-one: flag set, loop re-enters and calls model once more before breaking)
findings.evidence: ROUND 4 → `--- tool call #1: finish ---` ... `result: Task marked finished.` then ROUND 5 → `--- tool call #1: finish ---` (identical summary) ... `result: Task marked finished.` The header says `6 rounds`; round 5's reasoning is empty — the model only re-emitted finish because the harness re-prompted it after already accepting the round-4 finish.
findings.why_bug: A 'finish' (or 'done') op must break the loop immediately. Here round 4's finish was accepted ('Task marked finished.') yet the harness ran another full model turn (round 5) and accepted a second identical finish. The termination flag is evidently checked at the TOP of the next iteration instead of right after dispatching finish, costing one wasted model round/API call every step that finishes.
findings.fix: Break out of the dispatch loop in the same iteration that processes finish/done (return as soon as the finish op is handled), rather than setting a flag that is only tested at the next loop head.

findings.title: Gap def-index truncates the defs IMMEDIATELY ABOVE the viewed region (Request/__init__/open), the most relevant ones — forcing a wasted full-file read
findings.where: tools/codebase.py (growing-view gap label renderer) / tools/code_index.py — the gap def-index list truncates from the bottom of the gap instead of prioritizing defs nearest the requested range
findings.evidence: Round-1 view of 1600-1700 shows the `lines 1-1599 not read` gap label ending at `...862:class RedirectHandler; 869:def redirect_request …` — it stops at 869 and drops `1306: class Request`, `1307: def __init__`, `1359: def open`, which are the defs the model needs to wire use_netrc and which sit just above the viewed region. The model then had to issue a no-range whole-file read (the second ROUND 1: `args: {"path": "lib/ansible/module_utils/urls.py"}` → `TOO LARGE ... use read_file with a line range`) purely to surface `1359: def open` / `1307: def __init__`.
findings.why_bug: For a gap that ends right above the requested lines, the defs adjacent to the view (here Request.open at 1359, Request.__init__ at 1307) are the most relevant, yet the cap discards exactly those by truncating from the top-down and cutting the tail. That mis-selection cost an entire extra read round and still left the model never having seen Request.open's signature before editing.
findings.fix: When truncating a gap def-index, bias selection toward defs nearest the requested range (e.g. keep the last K defs immediately preceding the view, not just the first K), or always include the enclosing class + its methods when the requested region is inside/after a class body.

findings.title: finish accepted with zero verification — no run_code/test executed this step, so an incomplete patch ships silently
findings.where: finish handler / verify-gate in the JSON-OPS coder loop (core/native_tools.py) — no finish-gate requiring a test/run_code before accepting finish
findings.evidence: Across all 6 rounds run_code is never called; ROUND 4 `finish` is accepted immediately (`result: Task marked finished.`). The shipped edit forwards `use_netrc=use_netrc` into `Request().open(...)` (round-3 diff line `1670:+ ...ciphers=ciphers, use_netrc=use_netrc)`) but Request.open's signature (line 1359) was never read or edited, so the forwarded kwarg has no receiver — a TypeError that a single run would have surfaced.
findings.why_bug: The harness has no gate forcing at least one verification when run_code never ran (the known 'verify-gate skip when run_code already passed' implies a gate exists for the not-passed case). Here it did not fire, letting a structurally incomplete/broken patch be marked finished with no execution feedback — the exact #1 lever the doctrine flags.
findings.fix: On finish, if no run_code/test has executed this step, intercept with a 'run the failing test or the touched module once before finishing' nudge (one re-prompt), rather than accepting finish outright.

findings.title: Round counter prints 'ROUND 1' twice — turns are mislabeled 1,1,2,3,4,5 for a 6-round step
findings.where: JARVIS_ROUND_TRACE round-numbering / split_trace.py (the per-round header emitter)
findings.evidence: Header `=== CODER STEP 2 — 6 rounds ===`, then `========== ROUND 1 ==========` at the top AND a second `========== ROUND 1 ==========` for the next (whole-file no-range read) turn, after which numbering resumes at ROUND 2,3,4,5. Six distinct model turns are labeled 1,1,2,3,4,5.
findings.why_bug: The round index is not incremented between the first and second model turn (likely the salvage/first-read turn shares a counter value with the following turn), making the trace labels off-by-one versus the actual round and the '6 rounds' header — misleading for round-by-round audits.
findings.fix: Increment the round counter once per model turn at a single point, independent of the salvage/inline-tool path, so every emitted ROUND header is unique and matches the declared round count.

findings.title: FILE(S)-block header line count is off-by-one (says "622 lines", view shows 621) — same function uses the CORRECT formula 18 lines earlier
findings.where: workflows/code.py:build_implement_native_prompt (header at line 11091)
findings.evidence:
Prompt header: `=== qutebrowser/utils/urlutils.py (622 lines) ===` but the rendered view's last line is `621 ⇥4|    return code` — there is no line 622. Source: line 11091 builds the header with `{c.count(chr(10)) + 1}`, while `add_line_numbers(..., 'prefix_ws')` DROPS the trailing empty element (codebase.py:330-331 `if lines and lines[-1] == '' and content.endswith('
'): lines = lines[:-1]`). I reproduced it: for `'line1
line2
line3
'`, `count(chr(10))+1` = 4 but the view renders 3 lines ending `3 ⇥0|line3`. Tellingly, the SAME function computes the count correctly at line 11073 (`_nlines = c.count(chr(10)) + (0 if c.endswith(chr(10)) else 1)` = 621) and only the display header uses the naive `+1`.
findings.why_bug: The coder is told the file is 622 lines but can only see 621. Per the 'BE THE INTERPRETER / read every line' doctrine a careful coder may waste a round on `read_file(622,622)` chasing the phantom last line, and it is exactly what made the STEP text need the awkward workaround `Add ... after line 621, before any trailing newline`. It mislabels essentially EVERY file (almost all end in a trailing newline). The overflow note at line 11098 has the identical `count(chr(10)) + 1` bug.
findings.fix: Make the header use the same count the view produces. Reuse the already-correct `_nlines` (line 11073) for the header instead of `c.count(chr(10)) + 1`, and likewise for the overflow note at 11098. Equivalently: header = number of lines `add_line_numbers` actually emits.

findings.title: JSON-OPS coder prompt documents AND prescribes a `batch` tool that does not exist; the negation is buried 60 lines later
findings.where: core/prompts_v8.py:IMPLEMENT_NATIVE_PROMPT_V8 (lines 1971 tool desc, 1985 GATHER) reused for the JSON-OPS coder; override at core/native_tools.py:3664
findings.evidence: The reused native system prompt fully documents the tool at line 11 of the rendered artifact: `- batch(calls) — run SEVERAL read-only lookups in ONE round ... Use it for your opening LOOK` (prompts_v8.py:1971), and the GATHER move at line 25 actively INSTRUCTS the coder to use it: `Do it in ONE round with batch(): name what each lookup answers` and `→ batch the ranges you need` and `LOOK (one batch)` (prompts_v8.py:1985). Only at line 72 — after the entire toolkit, INDENT section, and reflexes — does the JSON-OPS override say `there is NO 'batch' tool here — emitting several ops (one per line) in a round IS the batch, so never wrap them` (native_tools.py:3664).
findings.why_bug: A weak gpt-oss coder reading top-to-bottom sees `batch` documented as a real tool and is explicitly told to gather `in ONE round with batch()` before the negation appears. This is a live prompt/format contradiction — exactly the prompt-drift class the project doctrine flags as P0 — and risks the coder emitting a `{"tool":"batch", ...}` op or wrapping ops in a batch array, the one structure the override repeatedly warns against.
findings.fix: For the JSON-OPS (and native) coder, strip `batch` from the toolkit list and rewrite the GATHER prose to say 'emit all your lookups as separate ops in one round' rather than 'with batch()'. The override at the end should not be the FIRST place the no-batch rule is stated when the body prescribes batch().

findings.title: Opening system-prompt clause 'working through NATIVE function calls' contradicts the JSON-OPS output mode
findings.where: core/prompts_v8.py:1965 (IMPLEMENT_NATIVE_PROMPT_V8 first sentence); override at core/native_tools.py:3635
findings.evidence: Line 5 (first sentence of the system prompt): `You are the CODER in a multi-step coding agent, working through NATIVE function calls.` But line 59 (the appended JSON-OPS override) says the opposite: `You do NOT make function/tool calls. You RESPOND WITH OPERATIONS as a sequence of FLAT JSON objects`. The toolkit also still says `finish(summary) — call when the step is done` (line 21) / GATHER ends with `→ finish` (line 25), only reconciled at line 72 (`wherever the instructions above say "call finish" ... you instead emit the done op`).
findings.why_bug: The single most prominent framing sentence the coder reads tells it to use native function calls, which is false for this JSON-OPS path. Stale mode framing degrades a weak model; the correct mode is only asserted in an appended block far below. Same root cause as the batch issue: the native prompt is reused verbatim and patched at the tail instead of being mode-correct up front.
findings.fix: Parameterize the opening sentence and the finish/tool-call phrasing by coder mode, or have the JSON-OPS finalizer rewrite the first sentence (e.g. 'working by emitting flat JSON operations') rather than only appending a contradicting override at the end.

findings.title: Prompt references 'read on demand' files that do not exist in this single-file step, and the term mismatches the actual block label
findings.where: workflows/code.py:11125 (nat_user trailer, unconditional) + core/prompts_v8.py:1985 (GATHER)
findings.evidence: Trailer at line 753 of the artifact: `Read the 'read on demand' files only when you reach them.` and GATHER (line 25): `Some of the step's files are already loaded above; others are listed "read on demand".` This STEP 1 loads only urlutils.py — there is NO 'read on demand' list and no overflow block in the prompt. The trailer at code.py:11125 emits this sentence unconditionally (outside the `if overflow:` guard). Moreover the only place such files DO appear is titled `=== STEP FILE(S) TOO LARGE TO PRELOAD — open these with read_file ===` (code.py:11097), so even when present they are never labelled 'read on demand'.
findings.why_bug: It is a dangling reference: the coder is told to read files from a list that does not exist, and the terminology ('read on demand') never matches the actual section header ('TOO LARGE TO PRELOAD'). For a self-contained single-file step this is pure noise that can make a literal-minded weak coder hunt for a nonexistent file list.
findings.fix: Gate the 'Read the read-on-demand files...' sentence behind `if overflow:` (like `_overflow_clause`), and align its wording with the real label ('files listed under TOO LARGE TO PRELOAD'). Drop the 'read on demand' phrasing from GATHER when no such list is injected.

findings.title: finish op marks task done but does NOT break the JSON-OPS loop → duplicate finish, wasted round(s)
findings.where: core/native_tools.py JSON-OPS op loop (finish/done terminal handling) — the dispatch that prints "Task marked finished." vs the loop break condition
findings.evidence: ROUND 6: `--- tool call #1: finish ---` … `result: Task marked finished.` then ROUND 7 emits the byte-identical `{"tool":"finish","args":{"summary":"Added `use_netrc=True` …"}}` and again `result: Task marked finished.` The header says "8 rounds" — the model already finished at round 6.
findings.why_bug: The task spec says the loop runs "until {\"tool\":\"done\"}", but the coder emitted {"tool":"finish"}. The harness RECOGNIZES finish enough to print "Task marked finished." (so a finish/terminal flag was set) yet the loop kept going and re-prompted the model, which had nothing left to do and re-emitted the same finish. A finish op that is acknowledged must immediately terminate the coder loop. The terminal-token the prompt advertises (done) and the token the model actually uses/that gets the 'finished' message (finish) are not the same as the token the loop break checks → every finished step burns at least one extra round (and risks running to max-rounds).
findings.fix: Make the finish/done handler set loop_done=True and break the op loop on the FIRST recognized terminal op (whatever string prints "Task marked finished."). Unify the terminal-op token across the prompt, the dispatch that prints the message, and the loop-exit check so finish==done==exit. Also collapse multiple terminal ops in one streamed turn to a single break (stop processing ops after the first finish).

findings.title: Round counter mislabels: two consecutive "ROUND 1" headers (counter fails to increment after first op)
findings.where: behavioral_audit round-trace dumper / split_trace.py (per-round group labeling for JSON-OPS coder)
findings.evidence: Line 3 `========== ROUND 1 ==========` (list_dir) then line 37 `========== ROUND 1 ==========` again (read_file). Subsequent groups are 2,3,4,5,6,7 — so labels run 1,1,2,3,4,5,6,7 for 8 groups while the header claims "8 rounds".
findings.why_bug: The round index does not advance between the first list_dir turn and the next read_file turn, so the trace is internally inconsistent (max label 7, header 8, one number duplicated). This makes round-by-round auditing ambiguous — you cannot tell whether two ops were one model turn or two, and off-by-one round attributions propagate into any per-round timing/cost analysis.
findings.fix: Increment the round counter once per model turn (per op-group) consistently; if multiple ops share one turn, label them ROUND N op #k rather than reusing/duplicating the round number. Reconcile the "N rounds" header count with the max printed round label.

findings.title: Parse-gate reject appends a FIXED "nested-block indent slip / +4 deeper" diagnosis that is wrong for a non-indent SyntaxError (argument-order error), costing a wasted round
findings.where: workflows/code.py:_apply_extracted_code — parse-gate SyntaxError reject-message builder
findings.evidence: Both rejects (line 50 and line 131) carry: "SyntaxError at line 548: non-default argument follows default argument. ... Most often this is a nested-block indent slip — a body line not deeper than its `if`/`for`/`def`/`else` keyword. Re-issue with each nested level +4 deeper than its parent (keyword at INDENT N → body at N+4)." The actual error has NOTHING to do with indentation — it is a function-signature parameter ordering problem (`use_netrc=True` placed before the non-defaulted `ciphers`). The model's round-2 reasoning (lines 56–130) re-commits the same `, use_netrc=True, ciphers` ordering and is given the same irrelevant indent hint; it only self-corrects on the 3rd attempt by moving the param after `ciphers`.
findings.why_bug: The harness hard-codes the indent-slip remedy onto EVERY parse-gate reject regardless of the SyntaxError text. For 'non-default argument follows default argument' the advice is not merely unhelpful — it points the weak coder at indentation (which is correct here) and away from the real fix (move the defaulted parameter after all non-defaulted ones). A targeted, error-text-branched hint would very likely have fixed attempt 2 instead of burning a round.
findings.fix: Branch the reject hint on the SyntaxError message. When the text contains 'non-default argument follows default argument', emit a parameter-ordering hint ('a parameter with a default must come AFTER all parameters without one — move use_netrc=True to the end of the signature'), and only append the indent-slip boilerplate for IndentationError / 'unexpected indent' / 'expected an indented block' cases.

findings.title: Loop did not terminate after `finish` marked the task done — a second identical `finish` round was solicited and executed
findings.where: core/native_tools.py call loop / JSON-OPS coder loop — finish handler break (JARVIS_JSON_OPS path)
findings.evidence: ROUND 2 (line 165): tool call finish → "Task marked finished." (line 167). ROUND 3 (line 173): tool call finish AGAIN with the byte-identical summary → "Task marked finished." (line 175). No verify/run_code output appears between them.
findings.why_bug: `finish` at round 2 already set the done flag, yet the loop ran another full model turn (round 3) that produced a duplicate finish. That is a wasted round/model call. On a timeout-prone instance like a26c325b, every needless round is wall-clock the harness cannot afford; the loop should break immediately when finish marks the task finished.
findings.fix: After dispatching `finish` and setting the finished flag, break the round loop unconditionally (or, if a post-finish verify gate is intended, run it once and exit — never re-prompt the model for another finish).

findings.title: Round counter does not advance across rejected-edit turns: header says "5 rounds" but turns are labeled 1, 1, 1, 2, 3 (three consecutive ROUND 1)
findings.where: core/native_tools.py / JSON-OPS loop round-increment (also surfaced by the JARVIS_ROUND_TRACE renderer / split_trace.py)
findings.evidence: Line 1: "CODER STEP 7 — 5 rounds". Round headers: line 3 ROUND 1, line 54 ROUND 1, line 135 ROUND 1, line 161 ROUND 2, line 169 ROUND 3. The two rejected-edit model turns (lines 50, 131) reuse round number 1; the counter only advances after the edit APPLIED.
findings.why_bug: Five distinct model turns are numbered 1,1,1,2,3, so the displayed round is stale/misleading. More important than the label: if the loop's max-round budget keys off this same counter, rejected-edit spins are FREE — they consume wall-clock but not budget, which is exactly the reject-loop-to-timeout failure mode seen on this instance (clean budget-exhaust never triggers; the run just times out).
findings.fix: Increment the round counter on every model turn including rejected tool results, so the displayed number and the budget both reflect reject spins; reconcile the header's '5 rounds' with the per-turn labels.

findings.title: A no-range full read of a >1000-line file RESETS the growing-view accumulator, discarding ranges a prior batch read already revealed
findings.where: JSON-OPS read_file view accumulator — workflows/code.py view-assembly + core/native_tools.py read_file (the growing-view / supersession path)
findings.evidence: Turn 2 (batch read_file 1200-1600): header `=== VIEW: ... GROWING VIEW (401 revealed; ...)` (line 44) — 1600-1200+1 = 401, so 1200-1600 was revealed. Turn 3 the model did `{"tool":"read_file"...}` with NO range → `TOO LARGE to show in full (>1000)` def-index, zero content (line 241). Turn 4 (read_file 1300-1400) header is `GROWING VIEW (101 revealed; ...)` (line 341) — RESET to just the 101 lines of 1300-1400, not 401+101 — and its footer says `⋯ lines 1401-2066 not read — read_file(start_line=1401, end_line=2066) to reveal ⋯` (line 448) even though the batch had already requested+revealed 1401-1600. Turn 5 (read 1401-1500) then shows `201 revealed` (101+100), confirming the accumulator restarted at turn 4. The batch's 1200-1299 and 1401-1600 reveals were thrown away.
findings.why_bug: The harness charged the model a full round for the batch read of 1200-1600, then silently wiped those revealed lines when the model asked for the def index via a no-range read. The model had to re-read 1300-1400 and 1401-1500 (turns 4 and 5) to recover lines it had already paid to see — 2 wasted rounds. On this exact instance (a26c325b) the documented failure mode is budget-exhaust from re-read churn (memory: 're-read skeleton trap' / 'trim-trap spiral'), so this reset feeds directly into the known wall. A zero-content TOO-LARGE response should overlay the def index on top of already-revealed ranges, not reset them.
findings.fix: When read_file is called with no range on an already-partially-viewed large file, return the def index for the UNREAD gaps but PRESERVE the previously revealed ranges in the growing view (don't let a zero-content open supersede them). Equivalently: ensure ranges revealed inside a batch read_file merge into the same per-file growing-view accumulator that standalone read_file uses, and guard the supersession logic so a degenerate (no-content) read never clears richer prior reveals.

findings.title: First `finish`/`done` op did not terminate the JSON-OPS loop — an extra empty-arg round was spent
findings.where: JSON-OPS op loop terminal-op handling (finish vs done) — workflows/code.py JSON-OPS dispatcher
findings.evidence: Round 5: `{"tool":"finish","args":{"summary":"Added use_netrc parameter ..."}}` → result `Task marked finished.` (lines 693-695). Round 6: model is invoked AGAIN and emits `{"tool":"finish","args":{}}` (empty args) → result `Task marked finished.` AGAIN (lines 701-703). Two separate model turns both 'finishing'.
findings.why_bug: The terminal op should break the coder loop on the first successful finish; instead the harness ran another model round. The JSON-OPS protocol in the prompt specifies the terminal op is `done` (`{"tool":"done","args":{"summary":...}}`, prompt line ~70), but the model emitted `finish` — the harness salvaged it (accepted as terminal, good) yet only marked-finished without breaking, so it took a second `finish` (with empty args, indicating a re-prompt) to actually stop. One wasted API round per step.
findings.fix: In the JSON-OPS loop, treat a salvaged `finish` op identically to `done`: mark finished AND break immediately on the first occurrence. Confirm the `finish`-alias path sets the same loop-exit flag as `done`, not just the 'finished' summary.

findings.title: Round counter stuck at 1 for the opening look turns — 8 turns are labelled with max 'ROUND 6'
findings.where: JARVIS_ROUND_TRACE round labelling / op-loop round increment (workflows/code.py)
findings.evidence: Title says `CODER STEP 1 — 8 rounds` (line 1), but the per-turn headers are: list_dir `ROUND 1` (line 3), batch read `ROUND 1` (line 37), no-range read `ROUND 1` (line 235), then read 1300-1400 `ROUND 2` (line 335), read 1401-1500 `ROUND 3` (line 450), edit `ROUND 4` (line 638), finish `ROUND 5` (line 689), finish `ROUND 6` (line 697). Eight distinct model turns labelled 1,1,1,2,3,4,5,6.
findings.why_bug: The round index does not advance for the first three look turns (list_dir, batch, no-range read all share 'ROUND 1'), so round-by-round audit and any per-round budget accounting are off by 2 for the opening look phase. It also correlates with the supersession path in finding A (the superseded no-range read appears not to advance the counter), masking exactly the rounds that are being wasted.
findings.fix: Increment the round counter once per model turn (one API call = one round) regardless of which tool was used, so the label matches the 8 turns the title counts; or, if look-phase coalescing is intentional, document it and make the budget logic use the true per-turn count.

findings.title: Always-on coder prompt hardcodes this benchmark instance's answer symbols (`use_netrc`, `elif use_netrc:`, `web.data`/`parse_qs`)
findings.where: core/prompts_v8.py:2008 (MATCH THE TEST'S EXACT PATH reflex, injected into every coder step)
findings.evidence: prompts_v8.py line 2008: "... a flag/condition that turns a behaviour OFF must SKIP the work ... `elif use_netrc:` that never runs the netrc lookup when disabled, NOT `if login and use_netrc:` ... if the data arrives via `web.data()` ... parse THAT (e.g. `parse_qs(web.data())`), don't substitute ... `web.input()` ...". The live instance a26c325b IS the `use_netrc` ansible issue, and a sibling step's exact required change is literally `elif use_netrc:` guarding the netrc lookup.
findings.why_bug: Embedding a specific SWE-bench instance's literal answer (the `use_netrc` netrc-guard change, and a separate web.py instance's data path) as a 'general principle' in the always-on prompt is over-fit prompt content. When that exact instance runs, the worked example doubles as a concrete instruction for a sibling step's edit, which can lure the coder to implement the example rather than (or in addition to) its assigned step — a latent over-scope/wrong-step risk on multi-step instances of these issues. (On coder_step_1 here it happened to align with the Request change, so it was benign this run, but the coupling of prompt example to live task symbol is fragile.)
findings.fix: Replace the instance-specific literals in the reflex examples with neutral, synthetic symbol names (e.g. `use_feature`/`fetch_body()`), so the always-on prompt never restates a benchmark instance's actual answer. Keeps the pedagogical shape without colliding with live task symbols.

findings.title: SEARCH results render absolute sandbox paths, not repo-relative — contradicts the documented format and floods context
findings.where: tools/codebase.py: search_code() (rg is run with an absolute `root`) → _parse_rg_output() → format_search_results()
findings.evidence:
Every [SEARCH:] hit in the trace prints the full absolute path, e.g. (R1 result, lines 1932/1937):
`── /home/pguilp25/.swebench_work/instance_qutebrowser__qutebrowser-c580ebf0801e5a3ecabc54f327498bb753c6d5f2-v2ef375ac784985212b1805e1d0431dc8f1b3c171/.jarvis_sandbox/tests/end2end/test_adblock_e2e.py ──`. The TOOL TABLE documents the clean form `── /path/to/file.py ──` (line 264) and the PROJECT TREE / manifest use repo-relative paths (`qutebrowser/components/hostblock.py`). format_search_results just emits `r["file"]` verbatim (codebase.py:752) and search_code passes the absolute sandbox `root` to ripgrep (codebase.py:560,588) without relativizing.
findings.why_bug: ~150 noise chars per hit (the instance id alone is ~90 chars) consume the context window every round and obscure that these are ordinary repo-relative files; it also leaks the internal `.jarvis_sandbox` working dir (which is otherwise in PRUNE_DIRS, codebase.py:99). It is inconsistent with how every other section names files, forcing the weak model to mentally strip the prefix on every result.
findings.fix: In format_search_results / _parse_rg_output, strip the project_root prefix and render `── <repo-relative path> ──` (the same relativization the tree/manifest already use). Pass `--path-separator` or `os.path.relpath(file, root)` before display.

findings.title: Plan-in-reasoning salvage cannot recover an UNCLOSED/truncated [think], and only runs on [PLAN DONE] — a planner cut off mid-think loses everything
findings.where: core/tool_call.py: _salvage_plan_from_think() (regex requires a closing tag, ~L208-211) + the salvage call site gated entirely on a detected [PLAN DONE] (~L3702) / the final no-plan break (~L4279-4294)
findings.evidence: Across rounds 3, 4, 5 the planner writes its ENTIRE solution inside `[think]` (the complete widened_hostnames algorithm + the _is_blocked rewrite, derived correctly — e.g. lines 2730-3007, 3775-4016, 4784-5031) and the visible body is empty. _salvage_plan_from_think matches `\[think\](.*?)<close>` (L209) — it needs a closing `[/think]`; an unclosed/truncated think yields `reasoning=''` → returns ''. And the salvage is only ever invoked inside the `_pd_valid` ([PLAN DONE]-detected) branch (L3702-3707); the no-signal/no-plan final break at L4293 just `break`s with empty current_plan and never attempts salvage.
findings.why_bug: The repo doctrine names this exact failure ('an unclosed [think] that swallowed a correct plan') as a known costly one, and a salvage path exists — but it is unreachable for the precise case that triggers it (model never emits [PLAN DONE] because it is still inside think). A fully-correct plan sitting in the reasoning channel is discarded with zero recovery.
findings.fix: (1) Make _salvage_plan_from_think tolerate an unclosed opener: if there are more `[think]`/`<think>` opens than closes, salvage from the last unclosed opener to EOT (mirror the unclosed-think masking already done for signals at L760-772). (2) On the final no-plan break for a plan role with empty current_plan, attempt _salvage_plan_from_think(full_response) before giving up, not only on [PLAN DONE].

findings.title: No runtime guard catches a planner that burns whole rounds inside [think] with no plan block — all anti-ramble guards key on VISIBLE plan headers that never appear
findings.where: core/tool_call.py: verbatim-restatement guard (~L4008, uses _detect_preamble_sections on the raw result) and the prose-only '>400 tokens of think then commit' rule (prompts_v8, unenforced)
findings.evidence: R3, R4, R5 re-derive the SAME widened_hostnames trace almost verbatim (compare lines 2758-2831 vs 3819-3890 vs 4806-4883 — identical step-by-step `.c`/`c.`/`.c.` walkthroughs). The restatement detector calls _detect_preamble_sections(result), which scans for `## GOAL`/`## REQUIREMENTS`-style headers; the model's reasoning lives inside `[think]` as prose ('Key requirements:', 'Let's trace') with no `##` headers, so the detector finds nothing and never fires. The model thus loops with no intervention.
findings.why_bug: A weak reasoning model that keeps everything in [think] (which the prompt actively tells it to do: 'keep that in [think]') evades every loop guard, so it can consume its entire round budget re-deriving the same logic and contribute nothing to the merge. The harness has the levers (it already injects empty-turn, bare-signal, restatement, partial-view nudges) but none cover the think-only-no-commit pattern.
findings.fix: Add a guard: if a plan-role round produces no plan block, no tool tags, and a large [think] body, AND the prior round was the same shape, inject a one-shot 'close your [think] and emit `=== PLAN ===` NOW — you have re-derived the same reasoning; commit it' nudge (and/or run the salvage). Detect repetition on the raw/think text, not only on visible ## headers.

findings.title: Leaked `<channel|>` control token stored verbatim in [YOUR PAST THINKING] and replayed every round
findings.where: core/tool_call.py: response capture into _round_texts / the [YOUR PAST THINKING] renderer (no stray-channel-token sanitization before storing/replaying)
findings.evidence: The model emits a harmony-style channel token right before its tool block, and it is stored and re-fed each round: R1 thinking shows `Let's start by searching for "block" and "host" in the components.<channel|>[tool use]` (line 1921, replayed at 2710 and 4764). The harness preserves `<channel|>` verbatim in [YOUR PAST THINKING].
findings.why_bug: Stray protocol/channel tokens in replayed history are exactly the 'leaked protocol tokens' the doctrine warns about; they are noise that can re-prime the model to emit the same malformed token and confuse a weak model about where its visible output vs. tool block begins.
findings.fix: Strip stray harmony/channel control tokens (`<channel|>`, `<|channel|>`, `<|...|>`) from model output before appending to _round_texts / rendering [YOUR PAST THINKING], the same way [think] and [SYSTEM NOTE] are already sanitized from replayed context.

findings.title: JSON-OPS `finish` op is accepted but does NOT terminate the loop — every round after the real work is wasted to the round cap
findings.where: core/native_tools.py — JSON-OPS op driver loop (JARVIS_JSON_OPS path) terminate check / _do_finish; the break condition keys on the `done` op, not the `finish` op the model actually emits
findings.evidence: Step header: `=== CODER STEP 4 — 6 rounds ...`. The real edit lands in ROUND 3 (`✓ Applied 1 edit(s)`). Then ROUND 4: `--- tool call #1: finish ---` ... `result: Task marked finished.` — and the loop does NOT stop. ROUND 5 runs an IDENTICAL call: `--- tool call #1: finish ---` ... `result: Task marked finished.` with empty reasoning and no intervening prompt/work. The task brief says the JSON-OPS loop runs "until {\"tool\":\"done\"}", but the model emits `{"tool":"finish"}` (its native-coder tool name). The harness recognizes it enough to print "Task marked finished." yet never breaks — so the loop ran to its 6-round cap (labels 1,1,2,3,4,5) instead of ending at the first finish.
findings.why_bug: finish/done terminator mismatch: the model was trained/prompted with the native `finish` tool and uses it, but the JSON-OPS loop only treats `done` as the terminator. A clean completion can never short-circuit the loop — it always burns rounds to the cap after the work is done. Here only 2 rounds were lost because the cap was near; with a larger budget it would waste every remaining round. On a TIMEOUT-prone instance like a26c325b this is exactly the avoidable wall-clock burn that pushes a step past the 1800s limit.
findings.fix: Make the JSON-OPS loop treat `finish` and `done` as the SAME terminator: in op dispatch, when the op name is `finish` (or `done`), set the terminate flag and break immediately after recording the summary. Alternatively alias `finish`→`done` at parse time. Make the result string unambiguous ("Task finished — ending step").

findings.title: Growing-view gap-footer def-index is truncated for large gaps, hiding the target symbol → forces a redundant whole-file re-read
findings.where: tools/codebase.py — gap-footer renderer (the `⋯ lines X-Y not read … — contains …` line); the per-gap def cap truncates before reaching the symbol the coder needs
findings.evidence: ROUND 1 first read (lines 1-200, growing view) ends with the gap footer: `⋯ lines 201-2068 not read — read_file(start_line=201, end_line=2068) to reveal ⋯ — contains 208:class HTTPGSSAPIAuthHandler; ... 1135:def validate_proxy_response; 1148:def detect_no_proxy …`. It truncates at line 1148 with `…` and OMITS the actual target `1800: def url_argument_spec` (the function the whole step is about). The model could not locate the target from this footer, so in the next round it issued a no-range read whose result (`TOO LARGE to show in full (>1000)`) finally lists `1800: def url_argument_spec`. That second read existed only to recover the index the footer truncated.
findings.why_bug: The footer's purpose is to point the coder at WHERE to read, but it caps the def list well before the file end (1148 of 2068) and the named target is past the cut. The `…` signals truncation, but the only hint (`read_file(start_line=201,end_line=2068) to reveal`) points at revealing CODE, not at how to recover the full index — so the model wastes a round guessing a no-range read. (May partially overlap the recent 'def-index gap cap scaled to gap size' fix; this trace shows the cap is still short of the needed symbol for a ~1868-line gap.)
findings.fix: For a large gap, raise the cap so it reaches the file end, or when truncating append a targeted hint ('def list truncated — read_file with NO range for the full index'). Better: never truncate out a symbol matching the plan/target, or surface the full def index on the FIRST read of a >1000-line file so no recovery round is needed.

findings.title: Round counter duplicates 'ROUND 1' — two distinct API rounds share the same label (sequence 1,1,2,3,4,5 for 6 rounds)
findings.where: round-trace dumper / loop round-increment (JARVIS_ROUND_TRACE split + native_tools loop): the counter isn't incremented between the salvaged-inline-call round and the following round
findings.evidence: `grep ROUND` yields headers at lines 3 (`ROUND 1`), 220 (`ROUND 1` again), 320 (`ROUND 2`), 364 (`ROUND 3`), 380 (`ROUND 4`), 388 (`ROUND 5`) — six round blocks but labels run 1,1,2,3,4,5, while the header claims '6 rounds'. The first ROUND 1 has reasoning with a leaked inline tool-call JSON that was salvaged (`First, read that file.{ "path": ...}`); the second ROUND 1 has empty reasoning and a clean structured read.
findings.why_bug: When a round's tool call is recovered via inline-salvage, the next genuine round re-uses the same round number, so two separate model turns both print 'ROUND 1'. This corrupts round-by-round audits (off-by-one against the budget; '6 rounds' header vs max label 5) and can mask a wasted round when reconciling rounds-used against the cap.
findings.fix: Increment the round counter on every model turn regardless of whether the tool call came from a structured tool_call or from inline salvage; assert the printed max round label equals the step's round count.

findings.title: Two inconsistent def-index renderings for the same file (flat semicolon footer vs nested indented full list)
findings.where: tools/codebase.py — view renderer: the gap-footer index and the >1000-line no-range index are produced by different code paths/formats
findings.evidence: Gap footer (ROUND 1 first read) renders the index flat and semicolon-joined: `208:class HTTPGSSAPIAuthHandler; 214:def __init__; 219:def get_auth_value; ...`. The no-range read (second ROUND 1) renders the SAME symbols hierarchically indented by nesting: `  208:   class HTTPGSSAPIAuthHandler` / `  214:     def __init__` / `  219:     def get_auth_value`. Same file, same symbols, two visually different layouts in consecutive rounds.
findings.why_bug: Presenting the identical structural artifact in two different shapes within adjacent rounds is mild model-facing drift — a weak coder must re-parse the index format it just learned. Low harm, but gratuitous inconsistency and a divergence-maintenance risk (a fix to one renderer won't touch the other).
findings.fix: Render the def-index through one shared formatter (the nested/indented form is more readable) for both the gap footer and the no-range full-index, so the coder sees a single stable layout.

findings.title: Cumulative diff numbers a removed line with its OLD number while context/added use NEW numbers — an earlier in-file INSERT makes them collide (duplicate line number)
findings.where: core/edit_diff.py:render_diff (lines 57-61, removed-line numbering); surfaced via the CUMULATIVE diff in core/native_tools.py:_do_edit_file (~line 2016) and _do_replace (~1429)
findings.evidence:
The edit_file result diff in the trace shows two consecutive lines BOTH numbered 674:
  `674:                    dict_headers, socket_timeout, ca_path, unredirected_headers,`  (context)
  `674:-                   decompress, ciphers)`  (removed)
  `675:+                   decompress, ciphers, use_netrc=use_netrc)`  (added)
render_diff numbers removed lines with their OLD index (`{k+1}:- {a[k]}`, k into old_src) but context/added with NEW index (`{j1+k+1}`). Because this diff is CUMULATIVE against the step-START baseline and the FIRST hunk inserted a line (`631:+ use_netrc = module.params.get('use_netrc', True)`), every line below is shifted +1, so the second hunk's removed line keeps OLD number 674 while its neighbours use NEW numbers — colliding with the context line above (also 674). Reproduced offline: a +1 earlier insert makes context `48: code_673` and removed `48:- code_674` share number 48.
findings.why_bug: The diff is handed to the coder as authoritative 'CURRENT, live state' line numbers ('That start-state + this diff = file's CURRENT state — TRUST it'), yet it emits two different content lines under the SAME line number 674. A weak coder reading this can conflate dict_headers and decompress as the same line, or distrust the numbering it's told to trust. It is a self-contradictory artifact of exactly the kind the doctrine flags (stale/misleading model input). Impact is partly mitigated because the same message tells the coder 'do NOT copy an `old` from the diff' and real anchors come from the read view, so it did not break THIS one-round step — hence medium not large.
findings.fix: When the diff is cumulative (spans an earlier insert/delete that shifts later lines), number removed `-` lines in the SAME post-edit coordinate frame as context/added lines (or drop the number on `-` lines, or prefix them like `old N`). Concretely, give render_diff an option to number deletions by their position in the new/current numbering (e.g. j1-based, the spot just before they were removed) so a `-`/`+` replacement pair reads as `675:- … / 675:+ …` and never collides with the `674:` context line above it.

findings.title: Coder edits an entirely UNSEEN region (get_url.py main/argument_spec, lines 478–602) in one round with no read_file, and the harness applies it blind
findings.where: core/native_tools.py:call_with_native_tools loop / step dispatcher — no "unseen-region, read first" guard before applying edits
findings.evidence: Across coder steps 1–5 the only get_url.py region ever read was url_get's signature at line 382 (step 5: "Line 382: def url_get(...)"). Steps 1–4 read only urls.py. In step 6 there is ZERO read_file, yet edit_file targets lines 499/523/602 in main()/argument_spec — a region the coder had never viewed this run. The applier matched them by content at 478/524/602 (off by 21 / off by 1) and applied all 3 without any prompt to read. The model literally fabricated the view ("We have lines displayed. We'll edit." line 34) for code it never saw.
findings.why_bug: The harness lets the coder mutate a region it has demonstrably never rendered, with line anchors 21 lines stale, and has no signal/guard that this is an unread region. This is the direct enabler of the misplaced, de-indented edit. A "you're editing lines you haven't viewed this step — read_file first" gate (or auto-injecting the target region) would have prevented it.
findings.fix: Track per-step which line ranges of each file the coder has actually been shown; if an edit's anchor falls outside any viewed range, reject with a forced read of that range instead of applying on a hallucinated view.

findings.title: Applier silently DE-INDENTS the matched line (ciphers 8→4 spaces) by trusting the model's stale INDENT token even though the line-number anchor missed by 21
findings.where: workflows/code.py:_apply_extracted_code + native_tools.py:_expand_indent_lines (INDENT| re-emission)
findings.evidence: edit #1 old = "499 ⇥4|    ciphers=dict(type='list', elements='str')," (claims indent 4). Diff shows it matched at line 478 where the real line was indent 8: "478:-         ciphers=dict(...)" vs the applied "478:+     ciphers=dict(...)" — 8 spaces collapsed to 4. The model's line number (499) missed the content match (478) by 21 lines, a strong staleness signal, yet the harness re-emitted the model's `4|` indent verbatim and corrupted the line's indentation.
findings.why_bug: On a self-replace whose `old` content matches but whose line-number anchor is far off, blindly honoring the model's INDENT| over the matched line's actual indent corrupts the file (here it de-indented an argument_spec entry). The harness already knows the matched line's true indent and the size of the line-number miss.
findings.fix: When a content match lands >N lines from the stated line number, warn the coder of the drift; for a self-replace, default to preserving the matched line's existing indent unless the model's new content clearly intends a re-indent.

findings.title: Plan STEP 7 anchors the insert on "line 499 (`ciphers = module.params['ciphers']`)" — a same-token alias trap that lands the new statement INSIDE the argument_spec dict
findings.where: planner/merger plan-step generation (planner_merging_plans__final__owl-alpha.txt STEP 7)
findings.evidence: Plan line 950: "In main() at line 461, after line 499 (`ciphers = module.params['ciphers']`), add `use_netrc = module.params.get('use_netrc', True)`." The live file has no `ciphers = module.params['ciphers']` near line 499; the nearest `ciphers` token is the argument_spec entry `ciphers=dict(...)` at line 478. The no-view coder fused the plan's line-number 499 with that aliased token and inserted a main-body statement inside `argument_spec.update(dict(...))`: diff line 88 "479:+     use_netrc = module.params.get('use_netrc', True)" sits between `ciphers=dict(...)` and the closing `)` at line 480 — i.e. it became a dict kwarg referencing `module` before `module = AnsibleModule(...)` exists.
findings.why_bug: The plan presents a stale line number paired with a token (`ciphers`) that appears twice in the file (argument_spec entry vs param-read), creating an aliasing trap. A coder without a fresh view resolves it to the wrong occurrence, breaking the patch. (Distinct from the known generic plan-line-number caveat: the issue is the duplicate-token collision at the cited number.)
findings.fix: Have the plan anchor on a unique, full-line content quote (e.g. the exact `ciphers = module.params['ciphers']` line) rather than a line number plus an ambiguous bare token; or strip absolute line numbers from plan steps for files the coder must re-read.

findings.title: run_code import-only smoke test mislabeled "This output IS your edit's real behaviour" — none of the 3 edits were exercised
findings.where: core/native_tools.py:_do_run_code result wrapper (the canned "This output IS your edit's real behaviour" suffix)
findings.evidence: tool call #2 runs `importlib ... spec.loader.exec_module(mod); print('import ok')`. exec_module runs only module top-level; main() (where ALL 3 edits live, under `if __name__=='__main__': main()`) never executes. Result (line 113): "✓ ran in your edited sandbox — exit 0 (success). This output IS your edit's real behaviour: / import ok". The broken `use_netrc = module.params.get(...)` inside argument_spec.update() would NameError at runtime but is never reached by an import.
findings.why_bug: The unconditional "This output IS your edit's real behaviour" + "exit 0 (success)" stamps a green light on a test that validated none of the edited code path, giving false confidence so the coder finishes the step in 1 round shipping a broken patch.
findings.fix: Don't claim the output IS the edit's real behaviour for a smoke test that never reaches the edited function; detect import-only/no-call tests and qualify the message ("this only confirms the module imports — main() was not exercised").

findings.title: Post-edit success message contradicts the actual state: asserts "your view is NOT stale" / "You do NOT need to read_file again" when anchors were 21 lines off on an unread region
findings.where: workflows/code.py — post-edit success/diff message template
findings.evidence: Success message (line 81): "TRUST that — your view is NOT stale (your `old` was anchored on its line number AND content, so a shifted view self-corrects)... You do NOT need to read_file again... read a range only for a region you've truly never seen." In this step the model's numbers were off by 21 (499→478) and it WAS editing a region it had truly never seen, yet the message reassures the opposite.
findings.why_bug: The unconditional reassurance actively suppresses the corrective re-read precisely in the case (large anchor drift / unviewed region) where a re-read is needed, reinforcing the blind-edit failure mode.
findings.fix: Make the reassurance conditional: when a match landed far from the stated line number, replace "your view is NOT stale" with an explicit drift warning and a nudge to read the affected range.

findings.title: Cumulative-diff `⋮` elides a SINGLE line for no gain (latent blind-hole on def-index files)
findings.where: core/edit_diff.py:render_diff (difflib.get_grouped_opcodes with context=3)
findings.evidence: In the step-5 apply result the cumulative diff renders `386:  ` / `    ⋮` / `388:      """` (trace coder_step_5.txt lines 81-83). The two hunks (lines 383 and 391) are separated by exactly 7 equal lines (384-390); with context=3, difflib splits them and the `⋮` hides exactly ONE line (387). The `⋮` marker itself occupies a line, so the elision saves nothing — it strictly removes information (line 387's content) at zero vertical-space gain.
findings.why_bug: A `⋮` that hides <2 lines is a no-gain elision. It is harmless HERE only because get_url.py is 697 lines and was preloaded in FULL (the model still holds line 387 from the file view, which the diff does not supersede). But render_diff is the SAME renderer used for >1000-line files opened as a def/class index where the body is NOT held — there a 1-line `⋮` punches a genuine blind hole the coder must spend a whole round to re-read, contradicting the message's own 'you do NOT need to read_file again'.
findings.fix: Post-process get_grouped_opcodes (or bump the split threshold): when the equal run separating two hunks is small enough that the hidden remainder after trimming context is <2 lines, render those lines instead of emitting `⋮` — never replace 1 hidden line with a 1-line marker.

findings.title: After an edit, a CHANGED line has no copy-able `old` form — message says 'copy from your VIEW' but the VIEW is stale for that line and the diff is forbidden
findings.where: core/native_tools.py:_do_edit apply-result message (lines ~2022-2034)
findings.evidence: The result message instructs: 'For your next change here, COPY the relevant line from your VIEW VERBATIM as `old` (keep its `LINENO ⇥INDENT|` so it anchors)' and then 'The diff below is in `LINENO:+/- ` format … but do NOT copy an `old` from it (that's not the editable form).' For the two CHANGED lines (383, 391) the preloaded `LINENO ⇥INDENT|` VIEW still shows the PRE-edit content (`383 ⇥12|            decompress=True, ciphers=None):`), while the only fresh copy of the new content lives in the `383:+ …` diff — which the same message forbids as an `old` source.
findings.why_bug: For an UNCHANGED line the instruction is fine, but for a just-CHANGED line it is self-contradictory: 'copy from your VIEW' points at content that is now stale, and the only fresh representation (the diff's `:+` form) is explicitly off-limits and uses a different gutter (`:+ ` vs `⇥INDENT|`).
