To re-edit a line it just changed the coder must either reconstruct the `INDENT|` form from the diff (error-prone) or re-read (a wasted round on a timeout-bound instance). Not triggered in step 5 (no re-edit of a changed line was needed), but it is a real model-facing contradiction the doctrine flags as a P0 class.
findings.fix: In the apply-result message, render each CHANGED line additionally in its copy-able `LINENO ⇥INDENT|code` form (the editable anchor), or state explicitly that for a line shown as `:+` in the diff the coder should anchor on its NEW content using that line number — so a changed line always has a fresh, copy-able `old`.

findings.title: Per-step injection under-covers the plan: the required `use_netrc` DOCUMENTATION option block in url.py is dropped from STEP 9, so the coder ships a get_option() against an undeclared option (broken feature)
findings.where: per-step IMPLEMENT prompt assembly (workflows/code.py building the 'Implement ONLY this step' block) fed from the merger's structured step-list (core planning/merge); not the coder rounds in coder_step_9.txt
findings.evidence: Injected step (prompt_round0.txt:100-102): "STEP 9: Forward `use_netrc` in url lookup plugin / Files: lib/ansible/plugins/lookup/url.py / In `LookupModule.run()`, retrieve `use_netrc` from the lookup options (with default `True`). Pass `use_netrc=use_netrc` to the `open_url()` call." But the merged plan it derives from (planner_merging_plans__final__owl-alpha.txt:962, STEP 10) requires BOTH: "In `LookupModule.run()` ... ADD `use_netrc` TO THE DOCUMENTATION OPTIONS (add a `use_netrc` option block similar to other boolean options, default `True`). In the `open_url()` call ... add `use_netrc=self.get_option('use_netrc')` ...". The documentation sub-task was dropped. The coder did only what the step said and reasoned (coder_step_9.txt:9,11): "We need to add an option in documentation? Not required for this step. ... We'll assume it's defined." It inserted only `use_netrc=self.get_option('use_netrc')` (diff line 231:+). url.py is the ONLY step touching the lookup plugin; no later step adds the doc option.
findings.why_bug: In an Ansible lookup plugin, `self.get_option('use_netrc')` only resolves options DECLARED in the plugin's DOCUMENTATION options block; the view (prompt_round0.txt:116-135) shows validate_certs/split_lines/use_proxy declared but no use_netrc. Without the declaration, get_option('use_netrc') has no validated value/default — it raises/returns nothing at runtime, so the lookup-plugin path of the feature is broken and the hidden lookup test fails. This is CORRECT model behavior on UNDER-COVERING harness input (merger emitted the doc requirement, the step-splitter lost it) — exactly a 'plan that under-covers'.
findings.fix: When splitting the merged prose plan into per-step injected instructions, do not summarize away sub-tasks: STEP 9 must retain 'add a `use_netrc` option block to the DOCUMENTATION options (default True)' alongside the open_url forwarding. Verify each emitted step is a superset (not a lossy paraphrase) of its source step — diff emitted-vs-source and fail loud when a named action (the documentation edit) is missing.

findings.title: Over-scope guard compounds the dropped doc-option: it discourages editing the unnamed DOCUMENTATION block even though it lives in the same step file
findings.where: IMPLEMENT prompt over-scope warning (prompts_v8 IMPLEMENT_NATIVE_PROMPT / per-step header in workflows/code.py)
findings.evidence: prompt_round0.txt:96: "You are assigned ONLY the STEP below ... do NOT edit symbols or files the step doesn't name (a later step handles those — editing them here causes conflicts and over-scope)." Injected STEP 9 names only `LookupModule.run()` and `open_url()`; the DOCUMENTATION options block is an unnamed symbol in the same file. The coder's "Not required for this step" (coder_step_9.txt:9) is consistent with this guard suppressing the doc edit.
findings.why_bug: Because the step text already dropped the doc requirement (finding 1), this guard makes self-recovery impossible: even a coder noticing the missing `use_netrc` declaration is told the DOCUMENTATION block is out-of-scope ('a later step handles those'), when no later step touches url.py. The lossy split + this guard together guarantee the doc option is never added.
findings.fix: Scope the over-scope guard to the named FILE region/intent rather than a literal symbol allowlist, OR have the step text enumerate every symbol the step must legitimately touch (here, the DOCUMENTATION options block). Tie this to the finding-1 fix so the step owning url.py also owns its doc declaration.

findings.title: Merger's tool-call results never reliably reach it — 4 of 5 planner rounds wasted, final plan ships unverified
findings.where: workflows/plan.py (merger/finalizer loop assembling tool-result feedback into the next-round prompt) + core/tool_call.py (run/append tool results)
findings.evidence: Round 3 model: "Let me re-issue the tool calls properly to gather the remaining evidence" then `[VIEW: lib/ansible/module_utils/urls.py 1358-1510]`. Round 4 issues the IDENTICAL `[VIEW: lib/ansible/module_utils/urls.py 1358-1510]` again. Round 5 [think]: "I still need to see: `open_url`, `fetch_url`, `url_argument_spec`, `get_url.py`, `uri.py`, and `url.py` lookup plugin. But I'm at round 5/6 and need to commit." Final CONFIDENCE: "PRECISION = 8 ... other file line numbers from research cache (consistent across 3 drafts)" — i.e. the merger never verified them. No `=== Code:` / `=== VIEW:` tool-result block appears anywhere in rounds 2-5 of the rendered prompt.
findings.why_bug: The same `[VIEW: urls.py 1358-1510]` is re-issued in two consecutive rounds and the urls.py region only lands by round 5 (≈2 rounds after first request in round 3); the 6 files requested in round 2 (open_url/fetch_url/url_argument_spec/get_url/uri/url.py) are still unseen at the final round. A reasoning model does not re-issue an identical read if the result was fed back. The harness is dropping or lagging tool-result feedback into the merger prompt, so the planner burns its whole budget re-reading and emits a plan whose line citations it itself flags as unverified.
findings.fix: In the merger loop, append each round's tool results to a dedicated, always-present `YOUR TOOL INDEX` / results section that survives prompt trimming, and verify the just-executed call's output is in the NEXT round's prompt before advancing. Add an assertion/log that round N's emitted reads produce a result block visible in round N+1; if a result is dropped by the context window, prioritize it over re-emitting the static input plans.

findings.title: Malformed `[tool use]` wrapper (missing leading `[`) silently drops every enclosed tool tag with no warning
findings.where: core/tool_call.py — the `[tool use]…[/tool use]` block extractor (extract_tool_calls / equivalent)
findings.evidence: Round 2 model emitted 11 reads each wrapped as `tool use]` (no leading bracket), e.g. line 1728 `tool use]` / `[VIEW: lib/ansible/module_utils/urls.py 1649-1700]` / `[/tool use]`, repeated for get_url.py, uri.py, url.py etc. None fired (round 5 confirms those files were never seen). Round 3 model self-diagnoses: "Let me re-issue the tool calls properly". No `[SYSTEM NOTE]` / "N tool tags ignored" warning appears, unlike the documented warnings for a bare `[STOP]` (line 47-49) or an unterminated edit block (line 51-52).
findings.why_bug: The protocol documents feedback for a bare-half STOP and for unterminated edit blocks, but a tool tag emitted with a broken `[tool use]` opener is dropped silently — the model gets zero learning signal and has to guess what went wrong, costing it the whole round-2 batch (6 distinct files) that it never recovers.
findings.fix: Make the extractor tolerant of a missing leading `[` on the `tool use` opener (the closer `[/tool use]` and the inner `[VIEW:]` tags are unambiguous), OR when ≥1 recognized tool tag (`[VIEW:`/`[CODE:`/`[SEARCH:`…) is present but no valid `[tool use]` block is found, surface an explicit one-shot warning: "N tool tags seen outside a valid [tool use] block — wrapper malformed, nothing fired; re-wrap as [tool use]…[/tool use]."

findings.title: Merger told it receives "3 draft plans" but only 2 are supplied — plan #3 absent every round, plan #2 truncated mid-word — model hallucinates the third
findings.where: workflows/plan.py / core/prompts_v8.py — the merge prompt builder that concatenates Layer-1 plans into the `[INPUT PLANS]` section and writes the "3 draft plans"/"3 independent Layer-1 plans" framing
findings.evidence: System prompt line 350-351: "You receive 3 independent Layer-1 plans"; section header line 898: "[INPUT PLANS] — 3 draft plans to merge". grep shows only `[INPUT PLAN #1] — by owl-alpha` and `[INPUT PLAN #2] — by gemma-4-31b-it` in every round; there is NO `[INPUT PLAN #3]`. Plan #2 is cut off mid-word at line 1065/1722/2408/3066/3721: "RISK: 3 / (Precisi". Round 5 model then asserts a phantom third: "All three drafts agree on the file set and the approach" and "## C ... All 3 drafts agree".
findings.why_bug: The count and the anti-consensus / GROUNDED-MINORITY-WINS reflexes are built on having 3 real, independent inputs. With only 2 present (and one truncated), the model fabricates consensus from a draft it never saw, exactly the failure those reflexes are meant to prevent. The hard-coded "3" plus a missing/empty third plan is a stale-count assembly bug.
findings.fix: Make the count dynamic ("You receive N draft plans") and label only the plans actually present; when a Layer-1 planner returns empty/failed, drop its slot AND adjust the count, or emit an explicit "[INPUT PLAN #3]: (unavailable — planner produced no output)" so the model doesn't invent agreement. Also fix the per-plan body truncation that severs plan #2 at "(Precisi".

findings.title: Round-header claim "newest tool results + manifest" is false — the salient tail is static input-plan text, and the entire base is re-emitted verbatim each round
findings.where: workflows/plan.py — merger prompt assembler / context-trim ordering (and the trace dumper's round-header label)
findings.evidence: Every round 2-5 header reads "showing last 40000 chars = newest tool results + manifest", but the rendered last-40000-char tail in each round ends with `[INPUT PLAN #2] … (Precisi` then immediately `--- model response ---` (lines 1722-1724, 2408-2410, 3066-3068, 3721-3723). The full doctrine + full 200-file `[PROJECT CONTEXT]` list + both full input plans repeat identically each round; no `CONTEXT MANIFEST` / `YOUR TOOL INDEX` / tool-result block appears in the tail.
findings.why_bug: Tool results (the only genuinely new information across rounds) are NOT positioned at the recency-salient end of the prompt; the static, already-known input plans occupy the tail. Combined with any last-N-char window this is exactly why fresh results get pushed out and the model re-reads. The header label asserting results are in the tail is misleading and masks the ordering defect.
findings.fix: Place the newest tool results + manifest AFTER the input plans (at the recency-salient tail), and exempt them from the char-window trim; trim the repeated static doctrine/project-context instead. Correct the round-header label to reflect what is actually in the shown window.

findings.title: Coder STEP 9 drops the 'add use_netrc DOCUMENTATION option block' sub-task that the merged plan contained → unconditional get_option on an undeclared option breaks the url lookup plugin
findings.where: plan→coder-step extraction/condensation (the step-splitter/merger that turned owl-alpha's 10-step merged plan into the 9 coder steps; surfaces in the STEP block built for the coder user-turn in workflows/code.py / planner step rendering)
findings.evidence: Coder-facing STEP 9 (prompt_round0.txt lines 100-103): "In `LookupModule.run()`, retrieve `use_netrc` from the lookup options (with default `True`). Pass `use_netrc=use_netrc` to the `open_url()` call." — but the project's own merged plan (planner_merging_plans__final__owl-alpha.txt line 962, STEP 10) explicitly said: "add `use_netrc` to the documentation options (add a `use_netrc` option block similar to other boolean options, default `True`). In the `open_url()` call ... add `use_netrc=self.get_option('use_netrc')`". The doc-option clause was lost. coder_step_9.txt lines 9-11 show the coder noticed: "We need to add an option in documentation? Not required for this step ... if not defined, it may raise error ... We'll assume it's defined." Final diff (coder_step_9.txt lines 68-75) adds ONLY `231:+ use_netrc=self.get_option('use_netrc'),` — no option block.
findings.why_bug: In an ansible lookup plugin `self.get_option('use_netrc')` reads from options validated against the DOCUMENTATION block; if `use_netrc` isn't declared there it raises (KeyError/AnsibleError). The inserted line is UNCONDITIONAL, so after this patch EVERY url-lookup invocation calls get_option('use_netrc') on an undeclared option — a regression that breaks the plugin entirely, not just the new feature. The step is also internally incoherent: it keeps the outcome "(with default True)" while removing the declaration that would actually provide that default. The harness fed the coder a step that under-covers vs. its own planning.
findings.fix: When condensing/renumbering the merged plan into coder steps, do not summarize away concrete sub-actions (esp. 'add the option/argument-spec declaration'). For lookup/module steps that call get_option/module.params on a NEW key, the step must explicitly require declaring that key (DOCUMENTATION options block / argument_spec) in the same step, or assign it to a sibling step. Diff each emitted coder STEP against the merged-plan source step and flag dropped imperative clauses.

findings.title: JSON-OPS rendered prompt contradicts itself: opens as 'NATIVE function calls' and fully documents a batch()/finish() toolkit, then retracts both at the end
findings.where: core/prompts_v8.py IMPLEMENT_NATIVE_PROMPT_V8 (native body) reused verbatim under the JSON-OPS override appended for json-coder (native_tools.py / the JSON-OPS prompt assembler)
findings.evidence: prompt_round0.txt line 5: "You are the CODER ... working through NATIVE function calls." Line 11 fully documents "batch(calls) — run SEVERAL read-only lookups in ONE round...". Line 21: "finish(summary)". Then line 72: "there is NO `batch` tool here ... wherever the instructions above say 'call finish'/`finish(summary)`, you instead emit the `done` op." Line 35/25 still tell it to LOOK via "one batch". In coder_step_1.txt the model actually emitted a `batch` op (logged `--- tool call #1: batch --- args: {"calls": [...]}`) despite line 72 — i.e. the override's claim "there is NO batch tool" is itself false, the harness ran it.
findings.why_bug: A weak coder receives a full native-tool identity and toolkit, then a late override that negates pieces of it (batch absent vs. actually supported; finish vs. done; native-call vs. flat-JSON). Reconciling contradictions wastes reasoning and invites wrong-format ops. Doctrine treats model-facing instruction that contradicts the live format as a P0 drift bug.
findings.fix: Render the JSON-OPS coder prompt as ONE coherent artifact: drop/neutralize the 'NATIVE function calls' framing and the batch()/finish() tool entries at the source for json-coder rather than retracting them in a trailer; OR make line 72 truthful (batch IS accepted) instead of asserting it doesn't exist.

findings.title: Trailing user-turn boilerplate references 'read on demand' files that don't exist in a single-fully-loaded-file step
findings.where: file-block / closing-instruction assembler for the coder user turn (workflows/code.py prompt build that appends the post-FILE(S) paragraph)
findings.evidence: prompt_round0.txt line 359: "Read the 'read on demand' files only when you reach them." But the FILE(S) block lists exactly one file, lib/ansible/plugins/lookup/url.py (249 lines), shown in FULL and labeled "(already loaded)" — there is no 'read on demand' list anywhere in this step's prompt.
findings.why_bug: Stale conditional boilerplate that names a category absent from this prompt; for a weak model it implies there are other files to fetch, nudging an unnecessary read/look round (the prompt's own 'ideal 4 rounds: LOOK(batch)→...' reinforces this) when the only file is already in view and the correct shape is just EDIT→VERIFY.
findings.fix: Only emit the 'read on demand' sentence when the step actually has deferred/not-loaded files; for a single fully-loaded file, render a closing line that says all needed files are loaded — edit directly.

findings.title: Served read-view line numbers are off by +1 vs the file's true numbers (view↔diff numbering disagree by exactly one line)
findings.where: core/native_tools.py — the served read-view numbering (_accumulated_view / the view the coder copied from) vs the render_diff baseline (_first_seen at native_tools.py:2015 / core/edit_diff.py:render_diff)
findings.evidence: The coder's edit_file `old` anchors (copied VERBATIM from its view — note the ⇥INDENT values 4/4/8/12/16/16/16/16/12/8 are all exactly right, so these are not hallucinated): "51 ⇥4|    @staticmethod", "52 ⇥4|    def from_input():", "53 ⇥8|        i = utils.unflatten(" … "60 ⇥8|        )". The harness's own returned diff (render_diff, deterministic k+1 1-based numbering) numbers the SAME physical lines one lower: " 50:      @staticmethod", " 51:      def from_input():", " 52:-         i = utils.unflatten(" … " 59:-         )". The +1 offset is uniform across all ten lines.
findings.why_bug: For the FIRST edit of a step there are no prior edits to remap, so the view the coder anchors on and the diff baseline (_first_seen, same step-start content) should number identical lines identically. They differ by exactly 1, which means the read-view handed to the coder numbered every line one too high relative to the live file (likely a stale view carried across steps without remapping for step-1 edits, or a baseline-capture mismatch). The edit only landed correctly because the applier is content-anchored and self-corrected; but the harness then returns a diff whose numbers (correct, 50/51/52…) silently contradict the view it just told the coder to keep trusting ("TRUST that — your view is NOT stale … keep its LINENO ⇥INDENT| so it anchors"). Any op that depends on absolute line numbers without content (a blank-line INSERT anchor, a range delete, or a read_file(start,end) re-read of a region) would land one line off.
findings.fix: Assert that the served read-view numbering and render_diff's baseline (_first_seen) are derived from the SAME content snapshot for a file, and remap/refresh the view at step boundaries so its LINENO matches the live file. Add an offline check in render_prompt.py that renders the view AND the post-edit diff for the same file and asserts a shared anchor line (e.g. the def line) carries the identical number in both.

findings.title: Post-edit diff renders a newly-ADDED closing paren as unchanged context and stacks a later deletion after it (difflib paren-alignment), misrepresenting what changed
findings.where: core/edit_diff.py:render_diff (difflib.SequenceMatcher get_grouped_opcodes on paren-heavy code)
findings.evidence: Diff tail: " 70:+                 )", then " 71:              )" rendered WITHOUT a +/- (i.e. as unchanged context), then " 59:-         )" (a deletion) AFTER it, then " 72:  " context. New line 71 (the else-branch's `)` closing utils.unflatten) is brand-new code, but it is shown as unchanged; meanwhile old line 58 (the original close of web.input) is silently treated as surviving and old 59 deleted out of position.
findings.why_bug: SequenceMatcher matched non-corresponding `)` lines between old and new as "equal", so the diff tells the coder a line it just added is unchanged and interleaves a deletion (old 59) after a new-side context line (new 71) — an out-of-order, hard-to-read 'what changed' view for a weak coder trying to verify its edit before signalling done. Low impact (cosmetic, inherent to line-based diffing), but it muddies the verify step on every paren/bracket-heavy edit.
findings.fix: Optionally suppress matching pure-delimiter lines (`)`, `]`, `}`, `:`) as diff anchors (treat them as junk for alignment) or post-process the opcodes so an added block's own closing delimiters render as `+` rather than borrowing an old line's number. At minimum, do not let a `:+`/context interleave place an old-numbered deletion after a higher new-numbered context line.

findings.title: Budget nudge is one round behind; the terminal "NO ROUNDS LEFT" stop is unreachable
findings.where: core/tool_call.py — continuation/budget builder (budget_msg block ~5374-5429; rounds_left also at 416) + loop `for round_num in range(1, max_rounds + 1)` at 3237
findings.evidence: The ROUND 5 prompt (line 4241) shows: "⚠ Round 4/8 — past halfway, 4 round(s) left. START YOUR DRAFT PLAN NOW...". This is the model's 5th turn (file header: "5 rounds"; loop is 1-indexed) yet it is labeled "Round 4/8". The continuation prompt is built at the END of round N (with round_num=N, rounds_used=round_num, rounds_left=max_rounds-rounds_used) and consumed in round N+1 — so the round number the model sees always lags the round it is actually in by one.
findings.why_bug: Two harness defects: (1) the model is mislabeled — on its real 5th round it is told "Round 4", and "past halfway" prints at rounds_used==4 which is EXACTLY half of 8, not past it. (2) More importantly, the terminal `rounds_left <= 0` branch (lines 5382-5396: "NO ROUNDS LEFT. This is your FINAL response... Do NOT use any more tool tags") can never reach the model: the highest round_num that ever builds a continuation is max_rounds-1 (the continuation built at the end of the final round is discarded when the for-loop exits), so rounds_left is always >=1. The hard final-round stop — added specifically (comment at 5369-5373) to stop a model that keeps investigating and never emits === PLAN === — is dead. The model is never given the "no more tools, write the plan now" terminal push.
findings.fix: Build the continuation budget message for the round the model is ABOUT to execute (round_num+1), not the one that just finished; equivalently pass `rounds_left = max_rounds - round_num` where round_num is the upcoming round. Then the terminal branch fires on the real last round and the displayed "Round N/max" matches the model's actual round. Also change "past halfway" to fire strictly past max_rounds//2.

findings.title: INTERFACE-TO-IMPLEMENT block renders a contradictory contract when the interface field is a "none" sentinel
findings.where: swe_bench.py:222-228 (interface block assembly in the task-prompt builder)
findings.evidence: The injected prompt (lines 892-894) reads: "=== INTERFACE TO IMPLEMENT — exact names, paths, and signatures === / Create/modify EXACTLY these symbols at these paths with these signatures (this is the contract the tests check — match names and locations precisely): / \"No new interfaces are introduced.\"". The source unconditionally wraps the dataset `interface` string with this rigid framing whenever `if interface:` is truthy — and here the field is the prose sentinel "No new interfaces are introduced." rather than an empty string.
findings.why_bug: The assembled text is self-contradictory model-facing input: it commands the planner to "Create/modify EXACTLY these symbols ... match names and locations precisely" and then lists ZERO symbols while asserting none exist. Yet the REQUIREMENTS (lines 889-890) add a `use_netrc` parameter to 9 functions (changing signatures). A weak planner can read "this is the contract the tests check: no new interfaces" as "signatures are frozen, add nothing" and under-scope. (Observed downstream effect: the planner hedged on TASK SHAPE — it labeled the task ADD-EX at line 4254 despite the FIX-default doctrine.)
findings.fix: Detect sentinel values ("no new interfaces", "none", "n/a", empty after strip) and either skip the INTERFACE block entirely or replace the rigid "Create/modify EXACTLY these symbols ... match names precisely" framing with a neutral note (e.g. "No NEW public symbol is introduced; you are MODIFYING existing signatures per the requirements above").

findings.title: Stale/inaccurate context-section labels: "[YOUR TOOL RESULTS]" doesn't exist; "[YOUR TOOL INDEX]" claims to contain results it doesn't
findings.where: core/tool_call.py:5494 (HARD RULES string) and :5447 (manifest header), plus the prompt legend in core/prompts_v8.py (~lines 330-331 as rendered)
findings.evidence: HARD RULES (trace line 1897/2680/3461/4239): "• File IS listed → do NOT re-call. Reason from [YOUR TOOL RESULTS]." But no section titled "[YOUR TOOL RESULTS]" exists in the assembled prompt; the code comment at 5213 confirms it "Replaces the previous split between [YOUR TOOL RESULTS]...". The index header (line 1885) says "[YOUR TOOL INDEX] — every tool call you've fired and what it returned:" and the legend (line 330) says "YOUR TOOL INDEX — your past tool calls + results (FACT)", yet the rendered index (lines 4216-4234) lists ONLY the calls plus line-counts — the actual returned CONTENT lives under [YOUR PAST THINKING].
findings.why_bug: The harness directs the model (a HARD RULE) to reason from a section name that isn't present, and twice asserts the tool-index contains results/"what it returned" when it only contains the call list. A model that trusts the index to hold result bodies, or hunts for the named section, gets misled — stale prompt-drift left behind when the two sections were merged.
findings.fix: Point the HARD RULE at the real section: "Reason from [YOUR PAST THINKING]" (or whatever now holds result bodies). Fix the index header/legend to say it lists the calls you made (with line counts), and that result bodies are in [YOUR PAST THINKING] — don't claim the index contains the returned content.

findings.title: Native loop emits "Task marked finished." BEFORE the verify-gate decides to HOLD — contradictory completion signal + the self-check it forces is a no-op here
findings.where: core/native_tools.py — call_with_native_tools, the finish handler at line 3430 vs the verify-gate at lines 3595-3611
findings.evidence:
Trace rounds 5 AND 6 are IDENTICAL finishes:
ROUND 5: `tool call #1: finish` args `{"summary": "Removed the `if k not in data:` guard ..."}` → result `Task marked finished.`
ROUND 6: the SAME `finish` with the SAME summary → result `Task marked finished.` again.
In source, when finish fires with edits present, line 3430 unconditionally sets `result_str = "Task marked finished."` (appended as the tool result the model sees). But AFTER the tool loop, the gate at 3599 (`if ctx.get('files_changed') and not _verify_nudged and not ctx.get('_run_code_verified')`) sets `done = False`, appends the `_VERIFY_NUDGE` ("⚠ SELF-CHECK before you finish — you edited: …"), and `continue`s. So at round 5 the model is told it is FINISHED and is simultaneously handed a turn telling it to self-check before finishing.
findings.why_bug: The tool-result text states completion at a point where the harness has NOT accepted the finish — it is about to un-finish (done=False) and demand another round. That is a direct model-facing contradiction (the doctrine treats prompt/format contradictions as P0). It also defeats the gate's own purpose: the model, already told 'Task marked finished.', simply re-emits the identical finish in round 6 (the self-check pass produces no edit and no verification), so round 6 is a wasted streaming round on a timeout-bound step. On a weaker model the contradictory 'finished' can trigger a stall/empty-turn instead.
findings.fix: Do not assert completion at the per-tool site. When finish fires, set a pending flag and leave result_str neutral; let the post-loop gate be the single authority. When the gate HOLDS for the self-check, override the tool result to e.g. 'Got your finish — run ONE self-check pass first (below) before I accept it.' Only emit 'Task marked finished.' on the round the gate actually accepts and breaks.

findings.title: JSON-ops loop skips round_trace on no-op/empty rounds — the json-coder's failure (the very thing that triggered the fallover here) is INVISIBLE in the trace
findings.where: core/native_tools.py — call_with_json_ops, the no-ops handler at lines 3777-3785 vs the round_trace at line 3843
findings.evidence: Raw open_dbbd9d53.jsonl for step 1: there is `phase=json-coder step=1 round=0 event=prompt` and then NOTHING from json-coder — it jumps straight to `phase=coder step=1 round=0 prompt model=gpt-oss-120b`, then `round=1 tools=[batch]` (native gpt-oss), then `phase=coder step=1 round=0 prompt model=medium` (fallover). In source, the empty/no-op branch `if not ops and not want_done:` does `continue` (line 3782, nudge) or `break` (line 3785) — both BEFORE the per-round `round_trace({"phase": "json-coder", ... "io": _trace_io})` at line 3843. The native loop, by contrast, emits an explicit `round_trace(... event='empty-turn' ...)` at line 3380 for exactly this case.
findings.why_bug: The JSON-ops gpt-oss-120b primary is the link ckpt-217 added specifically to KILL the empty-turn class, yet on this instance it produced zero usable output and fell over (json → native gpt-oss → qwen → mistral-medium). Because no-op rounds emit no round_trace, there is no round-level record of WHY the primary coder failed — the most important thing to audit is silently dropped, defeating JARVIS_ROUND_TRACE's purpose (offline diagnosis). The asymmetry with the native loop (which traces empty-turns) means json-coder failures look like the coder 'never ran.'
findings.fix: Before the `continue`/`break` in the no-ops branch (3782/3785), emit a round_trace mirroring the native path: `round_trace({"phase":"json-coder", "step":..., "round":rnd, "model":short, "event":"no-ops", "reasoning":(content or '')[:5000], "io":[]})`. Same for the api-error break at 3747-3749.

findings.title: split_trace.py merges ALL coders that ran on a step into ONE file labeled with the FIRST model, producing a duplicate "ROUND 1" and mis-attributing a mistral-medium fallover's 6 rounds to gpt-oss-120b
findings.where: behavioral_audit/split_trace.py — coder grouping at line 47 (group by step only) and header at line 55 (recs[0].get('model'))
findings.evidence: coder_step_1.txt header: `=== CODER STEP 1 — 7 rounds, model gpt-oss-120b ===` and it contains TWO `========== ROUND 1 ==========` blocks (line 3 = batch read, line 89 = no-range read). But raw open_dbbd9d53.jsonl shows: `phase=coder step=1 round=1 ... model=gpt-oss-120b tools=[batch]` then a fresh `phase=coder step=1 round=0 prompt model=medium` and `round=1..6 model=medium tools=[read_file, read_file, edit_file, run_code, finish, finish]`. So rounds 1-6 (the read/edit/run/finish that actually solved it) were mistral-medium, NOT gpt-oss-120b. Source line 47 `coder_steps.setdefault(r.get('step'), []).append(r)` keys ONLY on step; line 52 sorts by round (so the two round-1s collide); line 55 labels the file with `recs[0].get('model')` (gpt-oss-120b).
findings.why_bug: An auditor reading only the .txt (the intended workflow) is told this was one coherent 7-round gpt-oss-120b loop. It actually hides a fallover and nearly induces a false diagnosis: the 'round 2' no-range read returning a content-less TOO-LARGE index right after 'round 1' revealed lines 250-320 looks like the harness LOST the growing-view state (a fake bug) — when in reality it is the mistral-medium coder starting from a freshly re-seeded ctx (the intentional _seed_view_state reset in workflows/code.py:12474). The mislabeling masks both the fallover and the wasted re-read. (Also: line 40 writes every prompt-event to the same prompt_round0.txt, so json-coder and native prompts overwrite each other.)
findings.fix: Group coder rounds by a (step, coder-invocation) key — start a new invocation whenever a `round=0 event=prompt` (or a model change / round counter reset) appears — and label each file/section with its OWN model. Write prompt dumps to per-invocation filenames (prompt_step<N>_<model>_<k>.txt) instead of a single overwritten prompt_round0.txt.

findings.title: Round-trace records current_prompt[:60000] (HEAD slice) — drops the [YOUR TOOL INDEX]+[YOUR PAST THINKING]+tool-results TAIL on long prompts; auditor never sees the merger's round-1 reads
findings.where: core/tool_call.py :: call_with_tools (round_trace call, line 3325), compounded by behavioral_audit/split_trace.py :: main (lines 81-85)
findings.evidence: Line 3325: `"prompt": (current_prompt or "")[:60000]` — a HEAD slice. But the continuation prompt is assembled (line 5854) as `current_prompt = f"""{prompt}\n...[YOUR TOOL INDEX]...{manifest_str}...[YOUR PAST THINKING]...{past_thinking}"""` — i.e. the manifest and the actual tool results (file contents) sit at the END, AFTER the full base merge_prompt (SYSTEM ~35k + context[:12000] + all_plans_text[:48000]). For this instance the base ≈60k chars, so `[:60000]` keeps the static base and truncates the entire dynamic tail. In the rendered trace, grep for `=== Code:`, `CONTEXT MANIFEST`, `YOUR TOOL INDEX`, `from sandbox`, or a `292:`/`51:4|` PREFIX line returns ZERO actual instances — they appear ONLY inside the system-prompt documentation. The round-2 window (labeled `showing last 40000 chars = newest tool results + manifest`, split_trace.py:84) actually ends with the re-rendered `[INPUT PLAN #3]` then `--- model response ---`; the model's two round-1 `[CODE: lists.py]`/`[CODE: utils.py]` results are nowhere in it. The comment at line 3319-3320 falsely asserts the recorded prompt is `exactly what THIS round's model saw (the accumulated tool results ... are folded into current_prompt)`.
findings.why_bug: It is a `mishandled output` bug in the audit harness: the LIVE model received the full current_prompt (the [:60000] cap is only inside the round_trace dict, not on the API call), but the captured trace silently omits exactly the dynamic content an auditor needs. Across any planner/merger round whose base prompt exceeds ~60k chars (typical), the trace shows re-rendered static base + input plans and zero tool results — making round 2 look like the merger got no new information and nearly causing a mis-diagnosis of model behavior. This directly defeats the project's core 'verify the rendered artifact offline' doctrine for the JARVIS_ROUND_TRACE tool. split_trace.py's ckpt-216 bump 15000->40000 treated the symptom; the root cause is the head-slice at record time, so the tail is already gone before split_trace runs.
findings.fix: Record the TAIL or the DELTA, not the head. Either change line 3325 to `current_prompt[-60000:]`, or (better) since split_trace already shows the full base from round 1, capture only the continuation delta for rounds 2+ (everything after the stable `{prompt}` block: the warning banners + [YOUR TOOL INDEX] + [YOUR PAST THINKING] + next-turn cue), which is the part that actually changes. Also raise/remove the cap so the tool-result tail is never clipped, and fix the misleading comment + the split_trace.py:84 label.

findings.title: Round-trace caps the model RESPONSE at [:12000], truncating the merger's final round mid-word so the auditor can't tell if a plan was emitted
findings.where: core/tool_call.py :: call_with_tools (round_trace call, line 3326)
findings.evidence: Line 3326: `"response": (result or "")[:12000]`. The round-2 response in the trace ends mid-word at line 1996: `3. Fix unflatten to use last-wr` with no `[/think]`, no `=== PLAN ===`, and no `[PLAN DONE]` visible — yet this is the LAST of '2 rounds'. The ~190 visible response lines are ≈12k chars, i.e. cut by the trace cap, not necessarily by the model.
findings.why_bug: Combined with finding #1 (prompt tail dropped), BOTH halves of the round are partially hidden, so an auditor cannot determine whether the merger actually committed a `=== PLAN ===`/`[PLAN DONE]` or rambled in `[think]` until max_tokens (the difference between a healthy merge and a forced-commit fallback). The cap makes the most outcome-determining part of the round unauditable.
findings.fix: Raise the response cap well above the model's max output tokens (e.g. 40000) or remove it; the response is the smallest part of the record and is precisely where the plan/signal lives.

findings.title: PROJECT CONTEXT 'first 200 alphabetical' file list excludes BOTH edit-target files yet is re-injected every round
findings.where: workflows/code.py — merge_prompt assembly, `context[:12000]` / PROJECT CONTEXT 'SOME FILES IN THE PROJECT (first 200, alphabetical — PARTIAL)' builder
findings.evidence: The injected list (round 1 lines 684-885, re-injected round 2 lines 1388-1589) ends at `openlibrary/olbase/tests/__init__.py` then `… and 658 MORE files NOT listed`. The two files the entire task is about — `openlibrary/plugins/openlibrary/lists.py` and `openlibrary/plugins/upstream/utils.py` — are alphabetically AFTER the 200-item cut (`plugins/` > `olbase/`/`mocks/`) and so are absent. Every input plan cites exactly those two files at file:line.
findings.why_bug: ~200 lines of static context that names NONE of the task-relevant files is re-rendered into every round's prompt (and counts against the prefix budget that finding #1 shows is already near the 60k mark). It is misleading low-value context: a weak merger could anchor on the listed `core/`/`macros/` files and treat the un-listed plugins dir as 'not present', the exact failure mode the list's own `do NOT assume a file is absent` caveat warns about.
findings.fix: For an existing large repo where drafts already cite concrete file:line paths, either drop the alphabetical first-200 dump from the merger prompt or seed it with the files the input plans actually name (union of draft file scopes) so the relevant targets appear instead of an arbitrary alphabetical prefix.

findings.title: Read-only planner/merger receives the full CODER edit-mechanics instructions it can never use
findings.where: core/prompts_v8.py — CORE_V8 / MERGE_PROMPT_TEMPLATE_V8 (shared CORE prose rendered for the read_only_role=True merger)
findings.evidence: The merger is called with `read_only_role=True` (workflows/code.py:8958) yet its system prompt carries: `Unterminated edit block ([edit] or === EDIT: without its closer)` (lines 50-52), `To keep a line in an [edit] block, copy it VERBATIM — gutter, count and all` (lines 200-203), `suitable for [REPLACE LINES N-M] edits` (line 235), and `Inside a === EDIT === block, all tags are treated as file content` (line 317). The role never emits an edit.
findings.why_bug: Stale/irrelevant instruction surface for the role — model-facing edit-format prose with no applicable action dilutes the planner's actual job (synthesize + emit `=== PLAN ===`) and adds tokens to a prompt that finding #1 shows is already brushing the 60k cap. Not a correctness break, but it is instruction drift against the role.
findings.fix: Gate the edit-format / [REPLACE LINES] / unterminated-edit-block prose behind an edit-capable role flag so read-only planner/merger/reviewer-route prompts omit it (or replace with a one-line 'you do not edit files in this role' note).

findings.title: Dual-channel indent slip (`0|    def`) ships SILENTLY as "✓ Applied" when the number-path result happens to parse — methods land at module scope, outside the class
findings.where: core/native_tools.py: _do_edit → _build_and_apply / _result_is_good / _has_indent_disagreement (gate at line 1928)
findings.evidence: The native coder's op (coder_step_1.txt round-2 args) declares the method lines with indent NUMBER 0 but 4 literal leading spaces in the code: `"63 ⇥0|    def __bool__(self) -> bool:"`, `"66 ⇥0|    def __eq__(self, other: object) -> bool:"`, `"71 ⇥0|    def matches_filter(self, filterstr: str) -> bool:"`. The harness honored the NUMBER (0); the success diff shows the method defs at COLUMN 0, OUTSIDE the class: `64:+ def __bool__(self) -> bool:` / `72:+ def matches_filter(self, filterstr: str) -> bool:` — while the enum members (written `4|`) correctly landed at indent 4. The result is a `VersionChange` enum with ZERO methods and three module-level dunder defs, reported as `✓ Applied 2 edit(s) ... step 1, round 1` with no warning.
findings.why_bug: The harness already owns the exact detector: `_has_indent_disagreement()` flags any `new` line where typed-leading-spaces > 0 and != the INDENT number (`typed > 0 and typed != int(m.group(1))`). But it is gated behind `if not _result_is_good(result) and _has_indent_disagreement():` (line 1928) — it runs ONLY when the number-path result FAILS to parse. Top-level `def __bool__`/`__eq__` are valid Python, so `_result_is_good` returned True, the disagreement check was never consulted, and the wrong (number-authoritative) expansion shipped. matches_filter/__bool__/__eq__ are now unreachable as methods — the patch fails its tests, yet the harness presented unambiguous success. The diff itself contains the proof (defs at col 0) but the harness draws no attention to it.
findings.fix: Run `_has_indent_disagreement()` UNCONDITIONALLY (not only on parse-fail). When a disagreement exists AND the number-path result parses, also build the trust_spaces result; if it parses and DIFFERS, surface a one-line note in the otherwise-success diff, e.g. `⚠ line 63 declared INDENT 0 but carried 4 leading spaces — applied at indent 0 (module scope). If you meant indent 4 (inside the class), re-send that line as 4|...`. Do NOT silently auto-flip (intentional dedent `4|        x` also disagrees and must keep the number), but a parse-valid disagreement must be told to the coder since the parse gate cannot distinguish 'intended top-level' from 'should be a class method'.

findings.title: IndentationError reject hint gives wrong-direction advice for 'unexpected indent' (over-indent) — tells the coder to go DEEPER when it must DEDENT
findings.where: workflows/code.py: _apply_extracted_code parse-fail branch, else-clause hint at lines 10501-10505
findings.evidence: The json-coder round-1 op put a TOP-LEVEL class at indent 4 (`new` began `0|import enum\n\n4|class VersionChange(enum.Enum):`). The reject the coder received: `IndentationError at line 56: unexpected indent. ... Most often this is a nested-block indent slip — a body line not deeper than its if/for/def/else keyword. Re-issue with each nested level +4 deeper than its parent (keyword at INDENT N → body at N+4).`  Displayed context: `L56:     class VersionChange(enum.Enum):` (4-space indented). The correct fix is to DEDENT the class to indent 0; the hint says go `+4 deeper`.
findings.why_bug: The else-branch generic hint only describes the 'expected an indented block' / under-indent failure mode and prescribes INCREASING indent. 'unexpected indent' is the OPPOSITE error (a statement indented more than its context allows — here a module-level `class` at indent 4). The code special-cases `_orphan` and `_vp` (leaked view prefix) but has NO branch for 'unexpected indent', so it falls through to advice that points the weak coder the wrong way — a model-facing contradiction (CLAUDE.md treats prompt/format drift as P0).
findings.fix: Add a branch before the generic else: `elif 'unexpected indent' in emsg:` with hint 'Line {lineno} is indented MORE than its context allows. A top-level statement must be at INDENT 0; a line cannot be deeper than the block it belongs to. REDUCE its INDENT number (and any lines that should sit at the same level).' Keep the `+4 deeper` text only for the under-indent / 'expected an indented block' case.

findings.title: Indent-reject context window (lineno-1, lineno) is too narrow to fix an over-indent — the parent line to align to is not shown
findings.where: workflows/code.py: _apply_extracted_code parse-fail branch, ctx builder at lines 10472-10476
findings.evidence: `ctx = "\n".join(f"    L{ln}: {body_lines[ln-1]}" for ln in range(max(1, lineno - 1), min(len(body_lines), lineno) + 1))`. For the round-1 reject (lineno=56) this rendered only `L55:` (blank) and `L56:     class VersionChange(enum.Enum):`. To realize the class must be at indent 0 the coder needs to see a sibling/parent at the intended level (e.g. the module-level `_SettingsType = ...` a few lines up), which the 2-line window omits.
findings.why_bug: An 'unexpected indent' is, by definition, indented relative to a reference line ABOVE it; showing only [lineno-1, lineno] hides that reference, so the coder cannot see what level the offending line should match. Together with the wrong-direction generic hint, the coder is given neither correct advice nor enough context to self-correct.
findings.fix: Widen the context window for IndentationError to about [lineno-3 .. lineno+1] and annotate each line with its real leading-space count (e.g. `L52 (indent 0): _SettingsType = ...`) so the coder can see the level the over-indented line must align to.

findings.title: Injected full-file view header over-reports line count by 1 for newline-terminated files
findings.where: workflows/code.py build_implement_native_prompt (line ~11091)
findings.evidence:
FILE(S) block in prompt_round0.txt: `=== openlibrary/plugins/openlibrary/lists.py (926 lines) ===` but the view renders only up to `925 ⇥8|        return delegate.RawText(image_bytes)` (max lineno 925, 925 lines rendered). I extracted the prompt from open_dbbd9d53.jsonl: header says 926, max rendered lineno 925, count rendered 925. Source: `f"=== {fp} ({c.count(chr(10)) + 1} lines) ==="`. Reproduced: for content 'a
b
c
', count('
')+1 = 4 but add_line_numbers(display_mode='prefix_ws') renders exactly 3 lines.
findings.why_bug:
Almost every source file ends in a trailing newline, so `c.count('
')+1` counts a phantom empty final line that the renderer (which drops the trailing empty split element) never shows. The coder is told the file is N lines but can only see/anchor N-1. It is also INCONSISTENT with the big-file growing-view counter, which is correct: native_tools._do_read uses `_bnl = _bcur.count('
') + (0 if _bcur.endswith('
') else 1)` and _accumulated_view uses `total = len(lines)` after stripping the trailing empty (so utils.py correctly shows '1369 lines'). A coder reasoning about EOF / appending at the last line (e.g. line 926) targets a line that does not exist in its view.
findings.fix: Count the same way the renderer does: `nlines = c.count(chr(10)) + (0 if c.endswith(chr(10)) else 1)` (or `len(c.splitlines())`) at code.py:11091, matching the big-file path so the header equals the number of rendered LINENO rows.

findings.title: split_trace.py mislabels a multi-coder step as the first model and hides the fallover boundary
findings.where: behavioral_audit/split_trace.py main() coder-step writer (lines 55-62)
findings.evidence: Header written: `=== CODER STEP 1 — 7 rounds, model gpt-oss-120b ===` (uses `recs[0].get('model')`). But the raw open_dbbd9d53.jsonl rows for step 1 are: round1 model=gpt-oss-120b tools=['batch'], then round0..round6 model='medium' (mistral) tools=[read_file,read_file,edit_file,run_code,finish,finish]. So 6 of the 7 displayed rounds were a DIFFERENT model, and the only on-page hint is the unexplained duplicate `========== ROUND 1 ==========`.
findings.why_bug: The per-round `model` field IS in the trace, but the splitter throws it away in the header and never marks where one coder fell over and the next took over. An auditor (this very task) is pushed to attribute mistral-medium's reads/edits/finishes to gpt-oss, and the re-seeded fresh ctx (which makes the second coder's no-range read return a bare def-index) looks like a read-view regression rather than an expected re-invocation. The fallover REASON is also absent from the trace (gpt-oss vanished after round 1 with no empty-turn row), so spurious vs. real provider failures can't be told apart offline.
findings.fix: In split_trace.py print the per-round `model` on each ROUND header, and when `model` changes or `round` resets to a lower value mid-step, emit a divider like `↳ FELL OVER to <model> (fresh ctx)`. Drop or qualify the single-model step header. Separately, have native_tools record the fallover reason (exception text / stuck / budget) in a round_trace row at the boundary so the gap is auditable.

findings.title: Finish verify-gate forces an extra streaming round on any module that imports a third-party dep at top level
findings.where: core/native_tools.py: _run_code_verified set only on exit-0 (~2685-2690); verify-gate (~3599, also 3358/3860)
findings.evidence: Step 1 round 4 run_code: `import web failed (ModuleNotFoundError)` — harness correctly says 'ENVIRONMENT limitation ... Reason about web statically and move on.' Round 5 finish → 'Task marked finished.' Round 6 finish AGAIN → 'Task marked finished.' The gate at line 3599 (`ctx.get('files_changed') and not _verify_nudged and not ctx.get('_run_code_verified')`) un-finished once because `_run_code_verified` is set ONLY on a clean exit-0 (line 2690), which an environmental ModuleNotFoundError can never reach.
findings.why_bug: In repos like openlibrary (every module does `import web`) or django, run_code's import fails at module load for essentially every edited file, so `_run_code_verified` is structurally unreachable and the verify-gate fires once on every step — a guaranteed extra streaming round per step on exactly the timeout-prone benchmarks the ckpt-221 `_run_code_verified` shortcut was added to protect. It is also mildly self-contradictory: the run_code message tells the coder to 'move on,' then the gate makes it re-finish. Bounded to one round per step, but systematic. (Trade-off: an env import-failure means the logic was never exercised, so blindly skipping the self-check loses real value — hence small, not larger.)
findings.fix: When run_code's only failure was a recognized environmental ModuleNotFoundError (the case that already populates ctx['_failed_imports']), record that verification was ATTEMPTED-but-environmentally-blocked and let the verify-gate accept the first finish after a re-trace nudge has fired once, instead of re-prompting on every web/django step; or gate the nudge on whether a non-environmental check is even possible for the edited file.

findings.title: Planner final-round budget escalation is unreachable; the round banner lags by one and stalls on the last round
findings.where: core/tool_call.py _build_continue_prompt (rounds_used=round_num, lines 5374-5396) + its only call site (line ~3481, round_num=round_num)
findings.evidence: Budget banners the model saw: ROUND 5 prompt='⚠ Round 4/8 — past halfway, 4 round(s) left' (line 4453); ROUND 6 prompt='⛔ Round 5/8 — 3 round(s) left' (5283); ROUND 7 prompt='⛔ Round 6/8 — 2 round(s) left' (6139); ROUND 8 prompt='⛔ Round 6/8 — 2 round(s) left' (6973). The run ran the full 8-round cap (header: '— 8 rounds ===') and the model wrote its plan on its ACTUAL last round while being told '2 round(s) left' and invited to 'refine ... later' with '=== PLAN_EDIT ===' that never comes. It never once saw the 'NO ROUNDS LEFT — FINAL response. Write your COMPLETE plan ... Do NOT use any more tool tags' message that exists at lines 5391-5396.
findings.why_bug: budget_msg is computed with rounds_used=round_num and baked into the continuation prompt for the NEXT round; there is no continuation after the final round, so the rounds_left<=0 branch (5382-5396) is dead — the strongest pressure the planner can ever see is structurally '1 round left' (and here only '2 left' due to an extra desync, likely the empty round 2 / the plan round not advancing the displayed counter). Two consequences: (a) the banner reads one round behind reality (round 5's prompt says 'Round 4/8'), and 'past halfway' is shown at exactly-halfway 4/8; (b) on its true last round the planner gets a non-final 'refine later' nudge — if it had fired one more tool instead of emitting the plan, the loop would end with NO plan.
findings.fix: Render the budget banner against the round the model is ACTUALLY on (round_num+1, since the prompt is for the next round), and explicitly fire the final-round message when the upcoming round is the last: when round_num+1 >= max_rounds, emit the rounds_left<=0 'NO ROUNDS LEFT — FINAL response, write your COMPLETE plan, no more tool tags' text. Ensure the displayed counter advances every round (don't let the empty/plan round freeze it).

findings.title: Prompt keys the no-reread rule on a phantom '[CONTEXT MANIFEST]' section the runtime never renders
findings.where: core/prompts_v8.py (section list + 'No re-reading' block; rendered at trace lines 328, 571-572, 92) vs core/tool_call.py:5447/5490 (renders '[YOUR TOOL INDEX]')
findings.evidence: System prompt advertises '── CONTEXT MANIFEST ────  files already loaded into context (⛔ no re-reads on these)' (line 328) and states 'Files in [CONTEXT MANIFEST] are already loaded. `[CODE:]` on them is rejected (⛔). This is the biggest token waste in practice.' (571-572), and 'Re-reading a file already in [CONTEXT MANIFEST] (marked ⛔)' (92). But every rendered round shows the section headed '[YOUR TOOL INDEX] — every tool call you've fired and what it returned' (e.g. line 1963) with its own '⚠ HARD RULES'. No section literally named CONTEXT MANIFEST is ever emitted by the runtime (grep of tool_call.py shows only YOUR TOOL INDEX is built).
findings.why_bug: Stale prompt anchor / model-facing contradiction (a P0 class per the project doctrine): the model is told the no-reread rule applies to '[CONTEXT MANIFEST]' and to expect that header, but the actual mechanism and header are '[YOUR TOOL INDEX]'. A model following instructions literally looks for a section that isn't there and may misjudge whether the re-read prohibition applies.
findings.fix: Rename the prompt references from '[CONTEXT MANIFEST]' to '[YOUR TOOL INDEX]' (or render the section under the name the prompt advertises). Make the section-name in prompts_v8.py match the header tool_call.py actually emits.

findings.title: Prompt documents empty-turn handling as 'treated as COMPLETE/accepted' but the planner runtime discards-and-nudges
findings.where: prompt SYSTEM RUNTIME text (trace lines 53-58, in core/prompts_v8.py) vs core/tool_call.py:4282-4292 (PLAN-ROLE no-plan dead-turn nudge)
findings.evidence: Prompt: 'No signal AND no tool tags → runtime treats the response as COMPLETE and accepts the current state. (Graceful, not a hang.)' (lines 53-58). Actual: round 2 was a [think]-only turn (no tool, no signal); the runtime did NOT accept it — round 3's prompt appended '⚠ Your last turn produced NO plan and fired NO tools ... An empty turn is discarded — do one or the other.' (line 2834; source 4287-4291).
findings.why_bug: The documented behavior contradicts the live behavior for a plan role: the prompt says an empty turn is accepted as final, but the runtime discards it and nudges (capped at 2, then breaks). A model that intentionally ends with no signal believing its state is 'accepted/saved' is actually discarded; conversely the doc undersells that it will be nudged. Stale instruction relative to the empty-turn handling.
findings.fix: Update the SYSTEM RUNTIME prose to state the real behavior: for plan/edit roles an empty turn (no signal, no tools) is DISCARDED and you get a one-shot nudge to retry (up to N times), not accepted as complete.

findings.title: Leaked reasoning-channel markers (<channel|>, </longcat_think>) are echoed back verbatim in [YOUR PAST THINKING] every round
findings.where: core/tool_call.py:421 history assembly from _round_texts (raw model output re-shown in [YOUR PAST THINKING]); salvage at 187-222 strips for execution but not for the echo
findings.evidence: ROUND 1 thinking is re-shown each round ending '...search for the route or the endpoint.<channel|>[tool use]' (e.g. lines 1991, 2803, 4474) and the round-5 response carries '</longcat_think>' (line 5377), both preserved in the echoed past-thinking that is fed back into context.
findings.why_bug: The salvage layer strips these harmony/LongCat channel markers for execution, but the verbatim echo in [YOUR PAST THINKING] re-injects the malformed tokens into the model's context every subsequent round, which can reinforce the model emitting leaked-channel output instead of clean bracket tags. (Hedge: confirmed the markers appear in the echoed history; I could not fully confirm the live echo uses the un-sanitized text vs the trace recording it raw — worth a 2-line check.)
findings.fix: Sanitize the per-round text before placing it into [YOUR PAST THINKING] (strip <channel|>, </longcat_think>, and other leaked channel markers with the same routine the salvage uses) so the echoed history doesn't teach the model the malformed pattern.

findings.title: Stale-anchor reject hint anchors on the model's LINE NUMBER, not its `old` CONTENT — shows an unrelated region and tells the model to 'copy your old VERBATIM from these'
findings.where: core/native_tools.py:_actual_region_hint (lines ~1494-1513), called from _old_not_found_msg (line 1558)
findings.evidence: ROUND 3 the model sent old = [`99 ⇥16|...raise ValueError(f"Invalid version format: {version}")`, `100 ⇥12|        return parts[0], parts[1], parts[2`]. The reject's hint (lines 170-176) showed: `97 ⇥4|    except ValueError:` … `102 ⇥8|        self.qutebrowser_version_changed = VersionChange.equal` — a region that contains NEITHER `raise ValueError` NOR `return parts[...]`. That content actually lives at lines 117-118 (proven by ROUND 4's hint, lines 186-191, which correctly showed 115-120 once the model happened to claim line 117). The message header literally says 'The ACTUAL current lines at that spot are below — copy your `old` VERBATIM from these'.
findings.why_bug: _actual_region_hint takes `anchor = sl-1` whenever `int(start_line)` is in range, and only falls back to the difflib content-locate branch (lines 1501-1511) when start_line is None/out-of-range. It never checks whether `cur_lines[start_line-1]` actually matches the model's `old`. But the ONLY reason this function runs is that the anchor was stale/wrong — so blindly trusting the same stale number shows a region unrelated to the edit and instructs the coder to copy wrong lines. Here it cost a full wasted round (3→4): had the hint located by content it would have shown 115-120 immediately.
findings.fix: After computing `anchor` from start_line, verify `cur_lines[anchor].strip()` (or the longest old line) matches the first non-blank `old` line. If it does NOT match, run the existing difflib SequenceMatcher / `_locate_block` content search (already implemented in the `anchor is None` branch) and re-anchor there. I.e. prefer content-location over a stale number whenever they disagree.

findings.title: Cumulative diff mixes OLD deletion line-numbers with NEW context/addition numbers in a single gutter, with a ~35-line discontinuity, while the prose insists 'your view is NOT stale — trust the line numbers'
findings.where: core/edit_diff.py:render_diff (lines 57-62) + the cumulative baseline used in core/native_tools.py:_do_edit (lines 2014-2034)
findings.evidence: After ROUND 1's two edits, the cumulative diff (trace lines 142-151) renders one contiguous hunk as: `133:  if 'general' in self:` / `134: old_qt_version…` / `135: old_qutebrowser_version…` / `98:- self.qt_version_changed = old_qt_version != qt_version` / `99:- …` / `100:- …` / `136:+ self._set_changed_attributes(…)` / `137: else:` … So the line numbers jump 135 → 98 → 136 within a single hunk. The surrounding message says 'TRUST that — your view is NOT stale … COPY the relevant line from your VIEW VERBATIM as `old` (keep its `LINENO ⇥INDENT|`).'
findings.why_bug: render_diff gives removed lines their OLD-file numbers (`a` index) and context/added lines their NEW-file numbers (`b` index). Standard for a 2-gutter git diff, but here there is ONE gutter and a +38-line method insert sits between step-start (the cumulative baseline) and the deletions, so the deletion numbers (98-100) are ~35 lines out of step with the adjacent context (133-139). A weak coder told to anchor edits on these numbers gets contradictory coordinates — consistent with the ROUND 2-5 line-number flailing (the model kept reusing 64-82 / 99-100 from earlier states).
findings.fix: In the single-gutter cumulative diff, render deletion lines with a marker that distinguishes 'old-file number' (e.g. a leading `~` or `(was N)`), or omit numbers on `:-` rows entirely and only number context/`:+` rows (which are what a re-read will show). At minimum, add one sentence: deletion `:-` numbers are PRE-edit and are gone; anchor only on the `:` / `:+` numbers.

findings.title: Unreachable-code reject cites POST-edit result line numbers (134-136) but the recovery hint prescribes PRE-edit current-file numbers (86-103), with no note they are different coordinate systems
findings.where: core/native_tools.py:_do_edit unreachable-gate path (lines 1986-1990, message built from _start=resolved[0][0] / _end) + _post_edit_syntax_gate `where` formatting (line ~1290)
findings.evidence: ROUND 1 turn-B reject (trace lines 71-72): '✗ … your edit leaves UNREACHABLE code — line 134: `old_major, old_minor, old_patch = old_parsed`; line 135: …; line 136: `if (old_major, old_minor, old_patch) == (new_major, new_mino` comes right after a return/raise at the same indent …' immediately followed by '↪ … For THIS one block, put its whole CONTIGUOUS span in `old` — every line from 86 to 103 …'. The error points at lines 134-136 but the fix instruction points at lines 86-103.
findings.why_bug: The unreachable check runs on the post-apply RESULT (then reverts), so its `where` carries result-file line numbers (134-136). The contiguous-span hint derives `_start/_end` from the model's submitted `old` range against the CURRENT (reverted) file (86-103). The two ranges never overlap and there is no statement that 134-136 are 'in your rejected output' vs 86-103 are 'the live lines to re-anchor on'. A weak coder cannot reconcile 'error at 134' with 'edit lines 86-103' and may hunt for line 134 in the live file (where the broken code does not exist).
findings.fix: Make the unreachable/parse-gate `where` numbers relative to the SAME coordinate system as the recovery hint (the model's `old` range / current file), or annotate them explicitly: 'in the result your hunk would produce (reverted), line 134 …; to fix, re-send old=lines 86-103 of the file as it reads now with corrected indentation.'

findings.title: Round counter does not advance across reject/retry turns — three distinct model responses in step 2 are all stamped 'round 1' (trace headers and the model-facing `_view_stamp`)
findings.where: core/native_tools.py:_view_stamp (lines 400-407) / the coder loop's ctx['round'] increment; surfaced via behavioral_audit/split_trace.py:57 ('ROUND {r.get("round")}')
findings.evidence: The trace shows three separate model turns each headed `========== ROUND 1 ==========` (lines 3, 49, 74) before `ROUND 2` (line 153). Turn A = a 3-hunk edit rejected on hunk #3; turn B = an 86-103 block rejected for unreachable code; turn C = two successful applies. BOTH of turn-C's apply messages read 'Applied … — step 2, round 1' (lines 80, 95) even though it is the third model response of the step. Title line says '10 rounds' but headers are numbered 1,1,1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8.
findings.why_bug: _view_stamp exists 'so the coder can ORDER multiple views of the same file and know which one is current (its latest)'. If two-plus rejected model turns share the same round number as the eventual successful apply, every diff/view in those turns carries an identical 'step 2, round 1' stamp, defeating the ordering purpose, and the round-by-round audit trace mis-accounts effort (3 turns collapsed into 'round 1').
findings.fix: Increment the round counter (ctx['round']) on EVERY model response including rejected/retried ones, so stamps and trace headers are monotonic; or, if reject-retries are intentionally a sub-step, stamp them 'round R.k' so the order is still distinguishable.

findings.title: `old:"0"` insert-intent gets the generic stale/'WRONG FILE — use search_text' reject, and atomic coalesce of independent flat ops discards the sibling hunks' work
findings.where: core/native_tools.py:_old_not_found_msg (lines 1559-1566) + _coalesce_edit_ops (merges same-path flat edit_file ops into one atomic `edits` batch)
findings.evidence: ROUND 1 turn-A: the model emitted three flat ops; the third (trace line 43) was `{"tool":"edit_file","args":{"path":"…configfiles.py","old":"0","new":"    def _set_changed_attributes…"}}` — clearly an insert sentinel. The harness coalesced all three into one edit_file with edits=[h1,h2,h3] (line 46) and returned '✗ edit_file hunk #3: the `old` line(s) are NOT in …. The file is UNCHANGED … (2) WRONG FILE — the symbol may live elsewhere; use search_text (or find_refs) to locate it and edit THAT file.' (line 47).
findings.why_bug: Two issues: (a) `old:"0"` (numeric/zero, an insert attempt) is not recognized as an insert; the reject suggests the symbol is in another file and to go search_text — sending the coder on a wrong-file detour. (b) Because _coalesce_edit_ops folds independent flat ops into one all-or-nothing edit_file, hunk #3's failure rejected the (possibly valid) hunks #1 and #2 too, leaving the file UNCHANGED — contradicting the flat-op 'run each, feed results back' model the JSON-OPS protocol advertises.
findings.fix: (a) Detect a pure-integer / '0' `old` and return an insert-specific message ('to insert NEW code anchor `old` on the existing line you want it placed before/after — there is no old=0 insert form'). (b) Consider applying coalesced hunks independently when they are not a single coherent refactor (or report per-hunk which applied), so one malformed sibling op does not silently discard valid edits.

findings.title: split_trace merges DIFFERENT coder invocations (models+protocols) into one coder_step file → duplicate round numbers + wrong single model label
findings.where: behavioral_audit/split_trace.py main() — coder_steps grouping (line 47) + header write (line 55) + round sort (line 52)
findings.evidence: coder_step_3.txt header says '=== CODER STEP 3 — 6 rounds, model gpt-oss-120b ===' and shows rounds labelled 1,1,2,3,4,5 as one continuous turn sequence. But the raw qute_f631cd44.jsonl step-3 records are THREE separate coders: rec40 json-coder/gpt-oss (round0 prompt only, 0 ops), rec41-42 coder/gpt-oss (round0 + round1 search_text, then bailed), rec43-48 coder/MEDIUM (round0..round5: the edit_file rejects, the successful edit, and BOTH finishes). The 'ROUND 1 search_text' reasoning belongs to gpt-oss; the 'ROUND 1 edit_file' and everything after belongs to mistral-medium. split_trace does `coder_steps.setdefault(r.get('step'),[]).append(r)` (groups by STEP ONLY, ignoring model and phase), then `recs.sort(key=lambda x: x.get('round',0))` and writes `model {recs[0].get('model')}` — so two coders that each legitimately start at round 1 collide into the duplicate '1,1' and the whole file is mislabelled with only the first model.
findings.why_bug: This is exactly the 'bad assumption' failure the doctrine warns about: an auditor (human or agent) reading coder_step_3.txt attributes one continuous chain-of-thought to gpt-oss, when in fact the search was gpt-oss and all the edits/finishes were a different model (mistral-medium) starting blind. It silently corrupts every overnight round-trace audit on any instance where the coder chain falls over (the common case). The live coder harness behaved correctly (fallover worked); the AUDIT renderer fabricates a false story.
findings.fix: Group coder records by (step, model, invocation) not step alone — e.g. start a new sub-group whenever round resets to 1 or the model/phase changes — and write one file per invocation (coder_step_3_a_gptoss.txt, coder_step_3_b_medium.txt) or at least annotate each ROUND header with its model+phase and never reuse a round number across invocations.

findings.title: split_trace writes a single fixed-name prompt_round0.txt, overwritten by every event=prompt record → only the LAST of 8 prompts survives
findings.where: behavioral_audit/split_trace.py main() — prompt branch (lines 39-45), p hard-coded to 'prompt_round0.txt'
findings.evidence: The instance has 8 event=prompt records: json-coder/gpt-oss steps 1,2,3 + coder/gpt-oss steps 1,2,3 + coder/medium steps 2,3. split_trace does `p = os.path.join(out, 'prompt_round0.txt')` then `open(p,'w')` for EACH one — same path every time. The surviving prompt_round0.txt header is '=== FULL CODER PROMPT (phase coder, step 3, model medium) ===' i.e. only the last record. manifest.append(('prompt', p)) is appended 8× with the identical path.
findings.why_bug: The doctrine demands rendering the FULL artifact the model received across MANY cases. This renderer destroys 7 of 8 prompts: an auditor told to verify 'the prompt the step-1 coder saw' or 'what gpt-oss saw on step 3' gets, by luck, only the final step-3 mistral-medium prompt. The fact that the slice's prompt_round0.txt happened to match step 3 is a coincidence (it was last); it is NOT gpt-oss's prompt, even though coder_step_3 is labelled gpt-oss — directly compounding finding #1.
findings.fix: Name the prompt file per (step, model/phase) e.g. prompt_step{N}_{phase}_{model}.txt, and add it to the manifest once per distinct prompt instead of overwriting one fixed path.

findings.title: Native loop tells the coder 'Task marked finished.' on a finish the verify-gate then REJECTS (un-finishes) in the same turn
findings.where: core/native_tools.py call_with_native_tools — finish handling sets result_str='Task marked finished.' (line 3430) before the verify gate at lines 3595-3610 sets done=False and appends _verify_nudge_msg()
findings.evidence: coder_step_3 ROUND 4: `finish` → result: 'Task marked finished.' Then ROUND 5 is a BYTE-IDENTICAL `finish` with the same summary and empty reasoning → 'Task marked finished.' again. In code, round 4's finish hits line 3427-3430 (done=True, result_str='Task marked finished.', appended to messages as the tool result at line 3493), but because files_changed is set and _run_code_verified is False, line 3599 fires: `done=False; messages.append(_verify_nudge_msg()); continue`. So the model receives, in consecutive turns: [tool result: 'Task marked finished.'] immediately followed by [user: 'Before you finish: re-trace your edit…/self-check'].
findings.why_bug: Contradictory signal to a weak coder: the tool result asserts the task IS finished, the very next message asserts it is NOT and to keep working. The model here just re-emitted the identical finish (empty reasoning), so the verify round produced zero value and the 'finished' acknowledgement was a lie about harness state. (JSON-ops loop does NOT have this — it `continue`s past the __FINISH__ tuple before appending any result, so the model never sees a false 'finished'.)
findings.fix: When the verify gate is going to fire (files_changed and not yet verified), set the finish tool result to a neutral/aligned string (e.g. 'Noted — one verification pass required before finishing.') instead of 'Task marked finished.', so the tool result and the following nudge agree.

findings.title: Coder-chain fallover discards the prior coder's lookups; gpt-oss's correct search_text result was thrown away and mistral-medium restarted blind
findings.where: workflows/code.py _CODER_CHAIN fallover (each coder invoked with a fresh call_with_native_tools/call_with_json_ops, no carry-over of ctx view/lookup history)
findings.evidence: rec42: native gpt-oss ran search_text 'qt_version_changed' and the harness returned the exact location (configfiles.py:84/138 + the _set_changed_attributes def). gpt-oss then fell over. rec43 shows mistral-medium starting at round0 with a FRESH prompt — it never saw that search result, and its first edit (ROUND 1) used a hallucinated `old` (`self.qt_version_changed = old_qt_version != qVersion()` at lines 139-140) that does not exist in __init__, earning the 'old line(s) NOT in file' reject. The discovered-symbol context was wasted.
findings.why_bug: The chain throws away expensive, correct exploration each time it falls over, forcing the next coder to re-derive (or, here, to hallucinate) — wasted rounds and a reject loop the prior search would have prevented. Not fatal (mistral recovered by round 3) but a recurring efficiency leak whenever the primary coders bail after looking.
findings.fix: Carry the prior coder's resolved file views / search results forward into the fallback coder's initial context (or at least seed it with the located symbol), so a fallover keeps the look-phase work instead of restarting blind.

findings.title: Plan STEP 3 over-specifies already-completed work — the if-branch _set_changed_attributes call existed before the step started, primed the wrong `old`
findings.where: planner merge output (the STEP 3 brief in prompt_round0.txt line 128) — the per-step plan is not reconciled against what prior steps already edited
findings.evidence: STEP 3 brief: 'replace the inline version comparison logic (lines 70-75) with a call to self._set_changed_attributes(old_qt_version, old_qutebrowser_version) when the general section exists, or self._set_changed_attributes(None, None) otherwise.' But the view the coder was given (prompt_round0 lines 267-273) ALREADY shows `if 'general' in self: … self._set_changed_attributes(old_qt_version, old_qutebrowser_version)` at line 136 — the if-branch was already refactored by an earlier step (step 2 also edited configfiles.py). Only the else-branch (138-139: qt_version_changed=False / qutebrowser_version_changed=False → _set_changed_attributes(None,None)) actually remained. Additionally 'lines 70-75' point at the unrelated `matches_filter` method (line 72 in the view), not version logic.
findings.why_bug: The brief describes the if-branch call as new work to be done, when it already exists, so a weak coder tries to 'replace inline logic' that isn't there and fabricates the pre-refactor form as `old` — contributing to the round-1/round-2 reject churn. The harness never reconciles a per-step plan against the edits prior steps already landed. (Partially overlaps the already-fixed plan-line-number caveat; the distinct, unfixed angle is over-coverage of completed work — low confidence.)
findings.fix: When emitting a per-step brief for a file an earlier step already edited, diff the plan's described target against the current sandbox state and trim/annotate steps whose described change is already present ('the if-branch already calls _set_changed_attributes; only the else-branch remains').

findings.title: Stale section name in [YOUR TOOL INDEX] HARD RULES — points the model at a section that no longer exists
findings.where: core/tool_call.py, call_with_tools() manifest assembly (line ~5494, the manifest_lines HARD-RULES block)
findings.evidence: The rendered prompt (round 2/3/4) shows: '⚠ HARD RULES for [YOUR TOOL INDEX]: ... • File IS listed → do NOT re-call. Reason from [YOUR TOOL RESULTS].' But there is NO section titled [YOUR TOOL RESULTS] anywhere in the prompt — the actual section is '[YOUR PAST THINKING]' (rendered at the '────── ROUND N — your tool result ──────' blocks). The source even documents the rename: tool_call.py:5213 'Replaces the previous split between [YOUR TOOL RESULTS] (all results lumped together) and [YOUR PRIOR TURNS]'.
findings.why_bug: A model-facing instruction tells the model to consult a section name that was renamed away. A weak planner (gemma-4-31b) told to 'Reason from [YOUR TOOL RESULTS]' has a dangling pointer — it cannot find that header. This is exactly the kind of stale-prompt-anchor drift CLAUDE.md flags as a P0-class consistency bug, even if low-impact here.
findings.fix: Change line 5494 to 'Reason from [YOUR PAST THINKING] below.' so the cross-reference matches the section the runtime actually emits.

findings.title: Tool-index summary line carries result metadata for CODE/KEEP but nothing for SEARCH/REFS
findings.where: core/tool_call.py, _manifest_line() (nested in call_with_tools, ~line 5432)
findings.evidence: In [YOUR TOOL INDEX] the read shows '[CODE: qutebrowser/config/configfiles.py] (R1 — 870 lines)' while the two searches show only '[SEARCH: VersionChange] (R2)' and '[SEARCH: changelog_after_upgrade] (R3)'. _manifest_line only extracts a hint via re.search(r'\((\d+) lines\)') or 'KEPT N/M lines'; SEARCH/REFS results match neither, so they get no hit-count or location summary.
findings.why_bug: The tool index is the durable summary that survives past-thinking budget-elision (`_past_thinking_keep_from`). When older rounds get elided, a CODE entry still tells the model '870 lines' but a SEARCH entry conveys nothing about whether it matched (e.g. '0 matches' vs 'configdata.yml'). The model then has only its own prior prose to recall what a search returned — exactly the kind of dropped/under-surfaced signal that pushes a weak model toward re-issuing the search. It didn't bite this 4-round run (nothing was elided), but the asymmetry is real.
findings.fix: In _manifest_line, add a SEARCH/REFS branch: count match lines / files in result_text (or reuse the `_not_found_terms` flag) and append e.g. '— 3 hits in 1 file' or '— NO MATCHES' so the index summary is informative for every tool type, not just file reads.

findings.title: Planner does independent lookups one-per-round (3 rounds of single lookups) — no model-facing guidance that multiple tool calls can fire in ONE round
findings.where: core/prompts_v8.py planner system prompt (TOOL PROTOCOL / 'Investigation is iterative' block) vs core/tool_call.py MAX_TAGS_PER_ROUND=15
findings.evidence: R1=[CODE: configfiles.py], R2=[SEARCH: VersionChange], R3=[SEARCH: changelog_after_upgrade], R4=plan. The two searches in R2 and R3 are independent (does the symbol exist? / what type is the setting?) and could have fired in one round. The harness explicitly supports this (MAX_TAGS_PER_ROUND=15; docstring at tool_call.py:3027 'JARVIS runs ALL requested lookups at once'), but the MODEL-FACING prompt only says 'Wrap every tool call in [tool use]...[/tool use], then [STOP]' (singular) and frames work as 'Investigation is iterative. Each tool result legitimately opens new questions — that's correct', which actively nudges serial one-at-a-time lookups.
findings.why_bug: On a tight planner round budget (this run finished in 4 rounds; planners are often capped well below max_rounds=20), serializing independent lookups burns a round that batching would save. The capability exists in the harness but is never communicated to the planner, so the model defaults to the slower path — a (small) wasted round attributable to a prompt-guidance gap, not model incapacity.
findings.fix: Add one line to the planner TOOL PROTOCOL: 'You may place SEVERAL independent [tool use] blocks in a single round — the runtime fires all of them at once and returns every result next round. Batch independent lookups instead of spending a round on each.'

findings.title: Merger burns the full 6-round budget re-requesting overlapping lookups and never commits a plan; the stall guard is evaded by varying one tag per round / re-reading the same file via different tag forms
findings.where: core/tool_call.py — call_with_tools stall detection (lines ~4628-4662 and the forced-commit at ~4700); _is_cached/_norm_tag_key keying
