findings.evidence: Model responses: R3 fires [REFS: qutebrowser_version_changed], [REFS: changelog_after_upgrade]; R4 RE-fires [REFS: qutebrowser_version_changed #ref_qvc] + [CODE: qutebrowser/app.py]; R5 RE-fires both REFS + [SEARCH: changelog_after_upgrade] + [CODE: qutebrowser/app.py L380 L400]; R6 RE-fires [REFS: qutebrowser_version_changed], [SEARCH: changelog_after_upgrade], [VIEW: qutebrowser/app.py L380 L400]. The merger re-states verbatim every round 'I need to check how qutebrowser_version_changed is used in app.py...' and NEVER opens a `=== PLAN ===`. The trace ends at R6 (max_rounds=6 in workflows/code.py:8953) still issuing tools.
findings.why_bug: The stall heuristic increments only when ALL keys are cached, the key-set equals last round, or repeat_ratio>=0.5, and resets to 0 on any low-repeat round. Reading the same target as [CODE: app.py], then [CODE: app.py L380 L400], then [VIEW: app.py L380 L400] produces three DISTINCT normalized keys, and adding one genuinely-new tag (SEARCH) per round keeps the ratio from holding — exactly the evade pattern the code comment at ~4632 admits is hard to catch. So the forced-commit never fires; 6 rounds are wasted and the merger emits no plan, forcing the downstream forced-commit/fallback (workflows/code.py:8961+).
findings.fix: Make stall detection target-aware, not exact-tag-keyed: collapse CODE/VIEW/KEEP of the same file (ignoring range) to one target for repeat counting, and count any round whose lookups are >=50% subset of all prior lookups as a stall. Also trip the forced-commit on a round-count ceiling for read-only roles (e.g. force a plan at round 4 of 6) independent of the lookup-repeat signal.

findings.title: `[CODE/VIEW: path L380 L400]` range syntax is not parsed — the merger's attempt to read the app.py:387 decision site is mis-scoped, and it repeats the malformed form with no correction
findings.where: core/tool_call.py — _parse_code_arg (line 1231) and the L-prefix normalization in _salvage_longcat_calls (line ~997)
findings.evidence: R5 `[CODE: qutebrowser/app.py L380 L400]`, R6 `VIEW qutebrowser/app.py L380 L400`. _parse_code_arg's range_pat requires bare `N-M` dash form; 'L380 L400' (space-separated, L-prefixed) matches neither range_pat nor the trailing-single-number fallback, so it returns filepath='qutebrowser/app.py L380 L400' with no ranges. The L-strip that would help only runs inside _salvage_longcat_calls (guarded by '<longcat' in text), which doesn't fire for bracket-form calls; even if it did, 'L380 L400'→'380 400' (space, not dash) still fails range_pat.
findings.why_bug: All three input plans hinge on app.py:387 `if not state.qutebrowser_version_changed` (the boolean-compat question that decides whether the enum needs __bool__/__eq__). The merger is trying to read exactly lines 380-400 to settle it, but the harness can't parse the range, so it gets FILE-NOT-FOUND / whole-file / wrong scope and re-issues the same broken form the next round — never seeing the one site it needs. No bad_tags correction reached it (or it was ignored), so it loops.
findings.fix: In _parse_code_arg, accept space-separated and L-prefixed two-number forms: normalize `L?<a>\s+L?<b>` and `L<a>-L<b>` to (a,b) before the dash regex. Apply the same L-strip outside the LongCat-only salvage. When a CODE/VIEW arg looks like 'path <num> <num>' that fails to resolve as a file, surface a malformed-range hint ('use [VIEW: path 380-400]') rather than FILE NOT FOUND.

findings.title: The prompt's 'HARD RULE: by your THIRD round, STOP reading and WRITE the === PLAN ===' is never enforced by the harness
findings.where: workflows/code.py — merge call (max_rounds=6, lines 8948-8959) vs core/prompts_v8.py MERGE_PROMPT_TEMPLATE; no round-count forcing in core/tool_call.py loop
findings.evidence: Prompt lines 387-390: 'HARD RULE: by your THIRD round, STOP reading and WRITE the `=== PLAN ===`'. Yet the loop ran R1..R6 issuing tools with no escalating 'you are on round N/6 — write the plan now, no more tools' nudge; the only forcing path is the lookup-stall guard, which (per the finding above) never fired.
findings.why_bug: A model-facing 'HARD RULE' that the harness does not back with a round-indexed forcing message is a stale/contradictory instruction — exactly the prompt/mechanism drift the project treats as P0. A weak model ignores prose threats; the round budget (6) is also double the instructed limit (3), inviting over-reading.
findings.fix: Track round_num for read-only/planner roles and, at the instructed cutoff (round 3), append a forcing block that disables further tool processing and demands the `=== PLAN ===` this turn (mirror the existing stall_note path at ~4707), then break after one more round. Align max_rounds with the prompt's stated budget or update the prompt to the real ceiling.

findings.title: JARVIS_ROUND_TRACE captures current_prompt[:60000] head-first, so for large-base roles (the merger) the appended tool-results/manifest are cut off and the audit label is false
findings.where: core/tool_call.py:3325 round_trace({... 'prompt': (current_prompt or '')[:60000]}) + the split/audit 'last 40000 chars = newest tool results + manifest' label
findings.evidence: Every round dump ends at the [INPUT PLANS] block (e.g. line 1860 'Trace the case old_version=None...') immediately followed by '--- model response ---'. No [YOUR TOOL INDEX], no [YOUR PAST THINKING], no [WRITE YOUR NEXT TURN BELOW], no '=== Code:'/'=== References' result block appears in ANY of the 6 rounds — yet the assembly at tool_call.py:5854-5876 appends exactly those sections AFTER the base {prompt}. The merger base (MERGE_PROMPT_TEMPLATE + all_plans_text[:48000] + context[:12000] + the ~480-line CORE preamble) alone exceeds 60000 chars, so [:60000] drops the appended results before they are ever recorded.
findings.why_bug: The trace header advertises 'last 40000 chars = newest tool results + manifest' but the recorded tail is the static INPUT PLANS, not results. This directly defeats the doctrine's 'verify the rendered artifact' loop: an auditor reading this trace would wrongly conclude the model received no tool results. It nearly produced a false 'results dropped' finding here.
findings.fix: Capture the TAIL of current_prompt (or raise the cap well above the largest base prompt, or store base+delta separately) so the appended [YOUR TOOL INDEX]/[YOUR PAST THINKING] sections are always in the dump. Make the audit-view label reflect what was actually sliced.

findings.title: The loud 'LOOP DETECTED — STOP re-investigating and COMMIT' banner is gated to CODE/KEEP/VIEW only; repeated REFS/SEARCH/SEMANTIC/DEPENDENCY get only the quiet inline marker
findings.where: core/tool_call.py — manifest repeat_offenders construction (lines ~5478-5488)
findings.evidence: repeat_offenders = [k for k in round_keys if k.split(':',1)[0] in ('CODE','KEEP','VIEW') and _reread_count...]. The merger re-issued [REFS: qutebrowser_version_changed] in R3,R4,R5,R6 and [SEARCH/REFS: changelog_after_upgrade] in R3,R5,R6 — none triggers the prominent 🛑 banner (only the inline '⛔ RE-READ N×' manifest annotation, which the model evidently ignored).
findings.why_bug: REFS/SEARCH are the merger's primary tools, and re-issuing the identical symbol lookup every round is the clearest loop signal — but it gets only the soft signal, while the loud COMMIT-NOW banner (which the model is more likely to act on) is reserved for file reads. Inconsistent with the comment at ~4617 that says all tag types are now tracked.
findings.fix: Include REFS/SEARCH/SEMANTIC/DEPENDENCY/PURPOSE keys in repeat_offenders (a re-issued symbol lookup with _reread_count>=1 has not changed), so the loud banner fires for them too.

findings.title: Round 1 fully wasted on an empty [tool use] block that fired no tool, with no 'empty tool block' nudge
findings.where: core/tool_call.py — call_with_tools tool-extraction / round handling (round_keys empty path); empty-response nudge at ~3302 only covers blank responses
findings.evidence: R1 model response renders as: 'I need to examine the actual code before writing the plan. Let me read the key files.[tool use]\n[STOP][CONFIRM_STOP]' — the [tool use] block contains NO tool tag (no [CODE:]/[REFS:]). It fired zero lookups; R2 prompt shows no accumulated result and no [SYSTEM NOTE] about an empty block. The model only self-corrected in R2 with [CODE: configfiles.py].
findings.why_bug: An opened [tool use] block with [STOP][CONFIRM_STOP] but no recognized tool tag inside produces an empty round_keys set, so neither the stall guard nor any warning engages — the round is silently consumed. The empty-response nudge at ~3302 only triggers when the whole response is blank, not when the response is non-empty but contains no firing tool.
findings.fix: Detect '[tool use]...[STOP][CONFIRM_STOP] with zero parsed tool tags' and inject a one-shot note ('your [tool use] block was empty — no tool fired; put a [CODE:]/[REFS:] tag inside it or write your plan'), analogous to the unterminated-block and dropped-tags handling.

findings.title: Ejected def/class slips through: number-authoritative indent honors a `def` declared `0|` with 4 typed leading spaces, producing a module-level function that PARSES but is semantically dead; the trust_spaces reconciliation is gated on parse-FAILURE so it never fires
findings.where: core/native_tools.py: _do_edit / _build_and_apply + _result_is_good + _has_indent_disagreement (≈1872-1931), feeding _expand_indent_lines (1614)
findings.evidence: Step-2 applied edit declared `"64 ⇥0|    def _set_changed_attributes(...)"` (number 0 BUT 4 typed leading spaces) — coder_step_2.txt line 94. Result diff: `82:+ def _set_changed_attributes(...)` at column 0 (line 100). The step-3 file view confirms the breakage: prompt_round0.txt L198 `82 ⇥0|def _set_changed_attributes`, L182 `64 ⇥0|def __bool__`, L185 `67 ⇥0|def __eq__`, L190 `72 ⇥0|def matches_filter` — all module-level — while L270 `136 ⇥12|self._set_changed_attributes(old_qt_version, old_qutebrowser_version)` calls it as a method. number-auth produced def@0/body@4 which parses as a module-level function (I verified: `@staticmethod`+`_parse_version` at indent 4 just nest inside it), so `_result_is_good` returns True and the trust_spaces retry (built for exactly this `0|    x` dual-channel slip) is skipped. No `_indent_autofixed` warning appears in the result message.
findings.why_bug: The harness held BOTH signals — INDENT number 0 and 4 typed leading spaces — which disagree, and it silently picked the number, ejecting the def to module scope. A def's indent IS its scope; an ejected def parses fine, so parse-success is not a safe gate. This single mis-application makes the entire 3-step patch dead (every method ejected; __init__'s self._set_changed_attributes call raises AttributeError, all fail_to_pass tests fail). This is the global-state-the-harness-should-own case the project doctrine names.
findings.fix: When a `new` line whose code (after lstrip) starts with `def`/`class`/`async def`/`@` has typed leading spaces that DISAGREE with its `INDENT|` number, do NOT blindly trust the number even if the result parses: either adopt the typed spaces for that line, or reject with an explicit scope warning ('your def is declared at indent 0 but you typed 4 spaces — is it meant to be a method?'). Run the trust_spaces reconciliation on def/class lines regardless of parse outcome.

findings.title: The view propagates a prior step's broken indent as the 'nearest sibling' reference the prompt tells the coder to copy
findings.where: core/prompts_v8.py: IMPLEMENT_NATIVE_PROMPT_V8 'KEEP THE SCOPE NUMBER' reflex + appended INDENTATION block; view renderer (tools/codebase.py / native_tools render)
findings.evidence: prompt_round0.txt L44/L56: 'For a BRAND-NEW def/class, copy the INDENT| number of the nearest SIBLING already at that level'. By step 2 the nearest sibling defs were ALREADY ejected to 0 by step 1 (step-1 final diff: `64:+ def __bool__` at col 0, coder_step_1.txt L80). The view faithfully shows them at `0|`, so the step-2 model dutifully copied `0|` for its new `def _set_changed_attributes`, reproducing the ejection.
findings.why_bug: A broken prior step seeds the next step's identical error: the harness renders the malformed region at its literal (wrong) indent and the prompt instructs copying that indent as a scope anchor. The 'nearest sibling' heuristic is only safe if the sibling is correctly scoped — nothing checks that.
findings.fix: Break the chain via the ejected-def detection in finding #1; additionally the prompt could caution that the sibling-indent rule assumes the surrounding region is well-formed, and the harness could flag a def whose 'sibling' indent would place it at module scope inside a class region.

findings.title: Step 3's task is stale/already-done — the planner decomposition overlapped step 2 into step 3, and the step text describes work that no longer exists, wasting a round
findings.where: plan decomposition/merger + per-step prompt assembly (workflows/code.py plan handling) that injects the STEP block
findings.evidence: prompt_round0.txt L126-128: 'STEP 3: Refactor StateConfig.__init__ ... replace the inline version comparison logic (lines 70-75) with a call to self._set_changed_attributes(...)'. But step 2 (assigned only 'add the _set_changed_attributes method') already rewired the 'general' branch (coder_step_2.txt L79: changed lines 98-100 to `self._set_changed_attributes(old_qt_version, old_qutebrowser_version)`), so the file handed to step 3 ALREADY has that call at L136. The step-3 coder, trusting the description, reconstructed the long-gone inline lines as `old` (coder_step_3.txt L62: `"139 ⇥12|...self.qt_version_changed = old_qt_version != qVersion()"`) → rejected, 'old line(s) are NOT in the file', wasting round 1.
findings.why_bug: The plan boundaries between step 2 ('add method') and step 3 ('wire __init__') were not enforced; step 2 over-reached, leaving step 3 with only the else-branch residual but a description that still presents the whole refactor as undone, plus a 'lines 70-75' pointer to a region that no longer holds that logic. The coder is fed input contradicting its own view.
findings.fix: Re-derive each step's `old`-region against the CURRENT sandbox before sending it to the coder (drop/condense a step whose change is already present), and have the merger detect when an earlier 'add X' step already satisfied a later 'wire X' step.

findings.title: run_code verify is structurally blind for this file — the top-level `import yaml` ModuleNotFoundError aborts before any edited logic runs, and the 'environment, not your bug' reflex tells the coder to stop verifying the one file whose edit was broken
findings.where: core/native_tools.py: _do_run_code ModuleNotFoundError/ImportError handling (smoke sandbox)
findings.evidence: coder_step_2.txt L214 the coder ran `from qutebrowser.config.configfiles import StateConfig, VersionChange; ... state._set_changed_attributes(...)`. Result L215-223: `ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'yaml'` at configfiles.py line 35 `import yaml`, with the reflex message 'this is an ENVIRONMENT limitation ... NOT a bug in your edit ... move on'. The actual AttributeError-on-ejected-method was never reached.
findings.why_bug: For any module that imports a 3rd-party dep at top level (yaml, PyQt5 here), run_code dies at import and can never exercise the edited code, so the coder gets a correct-in-general 'move on' signal that here MASKS a genuinely dead edit. The patch ships unverified. (Note: ckpt-166 claimed a venv mount fixes this, yet yaml is still missing for this instance — the dep-gap persists.)
findings.fix: When a verify ImportErrors on a 3rd-party dep at module import (so the module can't load), offer an AST-level structural check instead — e.g. confirm the step's target symbol is actually a method of the named class — since run_code cannot import the module.

findings.title: Prompt-header model label ('medium') contradicts the actual coder model (gpt-oss-120b) for the same step
findings.where: round-trace/prompt dump header writer (behavioral_audit trace splitter / FULL CODER PROMPT header assembly)
findings.evidence: prompt_round0.txt L1: '=== FULL CODER PROMPT (phase coder, step 3, model medium) ===' vs coder_step_3.txt L1: '=== CODER STEP 3 — 6 rounds, model gpt-oss-120b ==='.
findings.why_bug: The dumped artifact mislabels which model received the prompt, which misleads offline debugging (e.g. attributing the indent error to mistral-medium when gpt-oss-120b actually ran). Doctrine: the rendered artifact must be trustworthy end-to-end.
findings.fix: Stamp the prompt-dump header with the SAME resolved model string the coder loop actually used (the fallback-chain selection), not a placeholder/initial label.

findings.title: finish tool result says "Task marked finished." while the harness is about to UN-finish it for the self-check pass — a direct contradiction that defeats the verify gate and burns a round
findings.where: core/native_tools.py — call_with_native_tools: finish branch sets result_str at lines ~3418-3430; verify-gate un-finish at lines ~3595-3611
findings.evidence: Round 5: tool call finish → result "Task marked finished.". Round 6: finish again (empty reasoning, identical summary) → "Task marked finished." again. In the code, when finish fires WITH edits the dispatch loop unconditionally sets `done=True; final=out[1]; reason="finished"; result_str="Task marked finished."` (3427-3430) and appends that as the tool result (3493). Only AFTER the whole tool loop, at `if done:` (3595), does the gate fire: `if ctx.get('files_changed') and not _verify_nudged and not ctx.get('_run_code_verified'): done=False; reason='budget-exhausted'; messages.append(_verify_nudge_msg()); continue`. So in round 5 the model is told the task IS finished, then handed a 'now do a self-check' user turn. Edits here were pure mechanical renames (iterator.ITERATING_*→IteratingStates.*), so the model rationally re-finished verbatim instead of self-checking.
findings.why_bug: The verify gate's purpose is to make the coder reconsider before accepting finish. Prefixing it with 'Task marked finished.' tells the weak coder the work is done, so it re-emits the identical finish (observed: round 6 = empty reasoning, byte-identical summary). The gate produced zero value and cost a full ~minutes-long round. This fires for EVERY native finish-with-edits where run_code was not run — broad impact, not specific to this instance.
findings.fix: Decide the verify-gate BEFORE composing the finish result, or make the finish result conditional: when files_changed and not yet verified, do NOT say 'Task marked finished.' — emit something like 'Edits recorded — one self-check pass before I accept finish:' immediately followed by the verify body, so the model sees a single coherent instruction to re-trace, not a finished-then-recheck contradiction.

findings.title: round-trace omits the injected user nudges (verify / read-spin / trace) and the api-error / no-tool-call break rounds — the slice shows finish→finish and a coder fallover with NO visible cause
findings.where: core/native_tools.py — round_trace is called only at the empty-turn path (~3380) and the per-round end (~3551); the verify-nudge append (~3607), read-spin nudge (~3584), trace nudge (~3569) and the break paths (~3300-3302, ~3387-3390) emit no trace row
findings.evidence: The slice renders: ROUND 5 finish→'Task marked finished.', ROUND 6 finish→'Task marked finished.' — with NOTHING between them explaining why the loop continued (the _verify_nudge_msg user turn that drove round 6 is never recorded in `io`). Separately, the raw jsonl shows gpt-oss-120b step 2 has only round 0 (prompt) + round 1 (read_file) then the chain falls to mistral-medium — gpt-oss's terminating round (api-error/empty-turn-exhaust) emitted NO trace row, so the fallover is invisible in the grouped file. The empty-turn path DOES get a special trace row (line 3380), making the omission of the other nudges/breaks inconsistent.
findings.why_bug: Violates 'capture the whole story' — an offline auditor (this very task) cannot see WHY finish failed to terminate or WHY the primary coder bailed after one read. The injected user turns are the actual drivers of the next round; leaving them out of `io` makes the round sequence non-reconstructable and invites wrong root-cause assumptions.
findings.fix: Emit a round_trace event row whenever a steering user turn is injected (verify-nudge, read-spin-nudge, trace-nudge) — mirror the empty-turn row at 3380 with an 'event':'verify-nudge' etc. — and emit a final row on the api-error / no-tool-call / stuck breaks recording reason, so every round and every loop exit is auditable.

findings.title: trace grouping mislabels the model and merges two different coders' rounds into one step with a broken round count (two 'ROUND 1' blocks)
findings.where: trace splitter/grouping script (best guess: behavioral_audit/split_trace.py or the *_groups builder) — it takes the step's first coder model for the whole group
findings.evidence: coder_step_2.txt header: '=== CODER STEP 2 — 7 rounds, model gpt-oss-120b ===', and the file contains TWO consecutive 'ROUND 1' headers. But the raw jsonl shows step 2 ran two models: gpt-oss-120b (round 0 prompt, round 1 read_file) then it fell over to mistral-medium (round 0 prompt, rounds 1-6: read_file, batch, edit×2, edit, finish, finish). So the 7 grouped rounds = 1 gpt-oss + 6 medium, all labeled gpt-oss-120b, and the medium rounds are renumbered starting again at 1.
findings.why_bug: An auditor reads all 7 rounds as gpt-oss-120b's behavior when rounds 2-7 are actually mistral-medium's, and the duplicate 'ROUND 1' hides that a chain fallover happened. This directly produces wrong attribution of coder behavior during offline audits.
findings.fix: In the grouping/splitter, key rounds by (model, step) or print a per-model sub-header and a fallover marker when the model changes within a step; number rounds monotonically across the merged sequence (or restart with the model name) so two distinct coders are never silently fused under one label.

diagnoses.missed: The produced patch missed the entire core requirement in lib/ansible/executor/play_iterator.py: (1) enum class definitions (IteratingStates IntEnum and FailedStates IntFlag at lines 109-122 in gold), (2) backward compatibility layer (_redirect_to_enum function at lines 192-208 and MetaPlayIterator metaclass at lines 211-224 in gold), (3) PlayIterator class modifications to use metaclass and __getattr__ (lines 227-237 in gold), (4) HostState.__str__() simplification to use enum names directly (lines 140-168 in gold), (5) all internal state assignments in play_iterator.py to use enum members (e.g., line 132 changing from PlayIterator.FAILED_NONE to FailedStates.NONE), (6) __all__ export update (line 106 in gold), and (7) the changelog file. The produced patch only updated strategy/*.py files with imports and references but never defined the enums or backward compatibility mechanism required by the interface specification.
diagnoses.harness_cause: Planner under-scoped the plan or coder dropped/skipped the core implementation of enum definitions and backward compatibility layer in play_iterator.py, only partially addressing strategy file updates
diagnoses.fix: The harness should explicitly require the coder to: (1) define both IntEnum (IteratingStates) and IntFlag (FailedStates) classes in play_iterator.py with all required members, (2) implement _redirect_to_enum() function and MetaPlayIterator metaclass for backward compatibility before the PlayIterator class definition, (3) modify PlayIterator to use the metaclass and add __getattr__ method, (4) simplify HostState.__str__() to use enum member names directly instead of helper functions, (5) replace all internal state usages with enum members throughout play_iterator.py, (6) update __all__ to export the enums, and (7) create the changelog file. The planner should decompose this into explicit sub-tasks that include all required changes to the primary file (play_iterator.py) before strategy file updates.

diagnoses.missed: The JARVIS patch completely missed changes to `/openlibrary/catalog/marc/get_subjects.py` (lines 78-168 of gold patch). It removes the `f = rec.decode_field(field)` call on line 78 and replaces all 13+ instances of `f.get_subfields()` and `f.get_subfield_values()` with `field.get_subfields()` and `field.get_subfield_values()`. Additionally, the `get_linkage()` implementation in `marc_base.py` is incomplete: GOLD correctly implements `for tag, f in linkages:` (unpacking tuple from `read_fields()`) but JARVIS implements `for field in fields_880:` (incorrect iteration), and uses `field.get_subfields('6')` instead of the gold's `field.get_subfield_values(['6'])[0]`. The `get_fields()` method modification in `marc_base.py` (gold lines 191-194) is also completely missing from JARVIS.
diagnoses.harness_cause: Planner under-scoped the patch scope. The plan failed to identify that all three files needed modification: not just the base/binary/xml MARC classes, but also `get_subjects.py` which is the primary consumer of the field objects. The coder also did not fully implement the `get_linkage()` method signature and tuple-unpacking logic from `read_fields()`.
diagnoses.fix: The harness should prompt the planner to identify ALL files that reference `rec.decode_field()` and field variable usage patterns. Search for `decode_field` calls and their consumers. Also enforce that when moving from a decoded representation to a direct field representation, both the source method (changes to `read_fields()` return type in `get_fields()`) AND all consumers (removal of decode, switching from `f.` to `field.`) must be updated together. The `get_linkage()` method should match the gold exactly, including tuple unpacking: `for tag, f in linkages:` not `for field in fields_880:`.

diagnoses.missed:
The JARVIS patch has critical structural and logical errors in qutebrowser/config/configfiles.py:

1. **Enum methods outside class (lines 304-358)**: `__bool__`, `__eq__`, `matches_filter`, and `_parse_version` are defined at module level indentation, not as methods of VersionChange class. Gold properly indents these as class methods.

2. **matches_filter() logic wrong (lines 312-320)**: JARVIS uses `self.value >= filter_val.value` comparison, but gold uses an allowed_values dict that maps filter strings to lists of allowed VersionChange values (e.g., 'minor': [major, minor]).

3. **_set_changed_attributes() signature wrong (line 322)**: JARVIS signature is `_set_changed_attributes(self, old_qt_version: Optional[str], old_qutebrowser_version: Optional[str])`, but gold signature is `_set_changed_attributes(self) -> None:` with no parameters. JARVIS calls it with arguments in __init__ (lines 370, 374), but gold reads from self within the method.

4. **Wrong version parsing (lines 352-358)**: JARVIS implements custom `_parse_version()` method, but gold uses `utils.parse_version()` from the qutebrowser.utils module and checks `old_version.isNull()` for unparsable versions.

5. **Enum value assignment (lines 297-302)**: JARVIS uses hardcoded integers (0, 1, 2...) but gold uses `enum.auto()` for cleaner enum definition.
diagnoses.harness_cause: Coder (JARVIS) made incomplete and incorrectly-structured edits: failed to maintain proper enum class structure with indented methods, implemented wrong function signatures and logic for _set_changed_attributes() and matches_filter(), and did not reference the existing utils.parse_version() utility that gold depends on.
diagnoses.fix: Regenerate with explicit instruction to: (1) ensure VersionChange enum methods (__bool__, __eq__, matches_filter) are properly indented as class methods; (2) implement matches_filter() using an allowed_values dictionary mapping filter strings to lists of VersionChange values, not value comparison; (3) make _set_changed_attributes() take only self as parameter and read old versions from self config internally; (4) use utils.parse_version() and check isNull() on the parsed version objects as gold does, not a custom _parse_version method.

diagnoses.missed: The produced patch failed to apply DEFAULTS to the merged result in lists.py. Gold implements `i = {} | DEFAULTS | utils.unflatten(form_data)` to ensure missing keys get defaults, while Jarvis only calls `utils.unflatten(web.storage(body_dict))` without merging in DEFAULTS. This leaves 'key', 'name', 'description', and 'seeds' uninitialized when they're absent from the form data, violating the requirement that "Defaults may only fill keys that are absent."
diagnoses.harness_cause: The coder implemented the POST/form-data branch correctly but skipped the crucial step of merging in DEFAULTS. The implementation focused on parsing form data and extracting values but missed the final merge operation that applies defaults to absent keys.
diagnoses.fix: After `utils.unflatten(body_dict)`, apply: `i = {} | DEFAULTS | i` (or equivalent) to ensure all default keys are present in the final result when they're absent from the body. This ensures the ListRecord constructor receives all required fields with proper defaults for missing values.
